


THE ETERNALL TRVTH OF SCRIPTVRES, AND CHRISTIAN BELIEFE, THEREON WHOLLY Depending: manifested by it owne LIGHT. THE FIRST BOOKE.


SECT. I. [I beleeue in God the Father, etc.]
IF in any at all, most of all in this present argument, may the truth of that vsuall axiome best appeare,
Dimidium facti qui bene coepit, habet.
What's well begunne, is wel nigh done.
If God shall enable mee rightly to vnfold the contents and meaning of this first word [Beleeue] I may iustly presume, the one halfe of this intended worke to be finished in it; seeing it is an essentiall part of euery Article in the Creed: such a part, as, if it be vnderstood amisse, we can not possibly vnderstand any one proposition of this whole confession aright. I shall not therefore seeme tedious (I trust) vnto the iudicious Reader, although I be somewhat long in vnfolding the nature and conditions of beliefe; the diuers acceptions and degrees of the same; with the meanes how it is, or may be wrought in our hearts.
Whether we speake of the Act, and operation of beleeuing, or of the disposition, and inclination of the mind, whence this operation
 proceedeth, it skilleth not much: hee that knoweth the true meaning of the one, without anie further instruction may know the other. And because the Act or Operation is more easie to bee knowne, let vs beginne with the most common and general thereof, that is, with the best and most vsually knowne acception or notion of beliefe.

CHAP. I. Of Beliefe in generall.
TO beleeue a thing, is to assent vnto it as true, without any euident certaintie of the trueth thereof, either from our sense or vnderstanding.
2 That beliefe is an assent; that to beleeue is to assent, all agree: but what more besides assent is required to some, especially to Christian beliefe, is much controuersed amongst Diuines: of which (God willing) in the Articles following.
3 That euident certaintie, either of sense or vnderstanding, must be excluded from the assent, which is properly called beliefe, is euident and certaine by our vsuall and common speech. Thus whiles we demaund of him that relates any thing vnto vs for true (as newes or the like) whether he know his relation to be certainly true, or no: if he neither haue immediately heard nor seene the things which he relates, but haue onely taken them at the second hand, his vsuall answere is: Nay, I know not certainly, but I verily beleeu^ they are true, for diuers reasons and credible reports: but if he had either seene or heard them himselfe, he would not say, I beleeue, but, I know they are most true. For euident certaintie doth drowne beliefe.
4 Yet is this euident knowledge (whether sensitiue or intellectuall) to be excluded only from the thing it selfe which is to be directly beleeued, not from other things that are linked or vnited to it by nature.
5 That which we euidently know, may oft-times be the cause, why we beleeue some other matters, that haue affinity with it. As he that seeth it very light in the morning, when he first openeth his eyes, may probably beleeue the Sunne is vp, because he euidently
 seeth the ayre to be light. But no man (if you should aske him the same question) would say, that hee beleeued the Sunne was vp, when either the heate thereof doth scortch his face, or the beames dazell his eyes; for now he knoweth this trueth directly and euidently in it selfe. Nor is there any man that hath his right minde, that will say he verily beleeueth twice two make foure: for this is euident, and certaine to ordinarie capacities, and hee that onely beleeueth this, knoweth nothing. For what men know certainely and euidently, they will not say, they only beleeue, but know: what they so know not, they may truely and properly say, they beleeue, if their assent to it be greater than to the contrary.
6 Some againe distinguish this vneuident assent (which is properly called beliefe) from other assents or opinions, by the grounds on which it is built. The ground of it (in their opinion) is authoritas docentis, the authoritie of the teacher or auoucher of the points proposed to be beleeued.
7 This distinction in some cases is true, but it is not necessarie to all beliefe; nor doth it fully and properly distinguish beleefe from other vneuident assents or perswasions. For euen those assents or perswasions, which seeme most to relie vpon authorities, may be strengthened by other motiues or inducements: yea our beliefe, or relying vpon authorities, vsually (alwayes if it be strong) ariseth from experiment of our Authors fidelity and skill, as shall appeare hereafter. For our present proceedings we take it here as granted or supposed, that this word [Beleefe] as it is vsually taken, is more generall than that assent or perswasion, which relies vpon authoritie; yet not so generall, as to comprehend these assents or perswasions, which are euidently certaine.
8 It may be obiected, that the Apostle calleth euident knowledge, beliefe, when he saith, The diuells beleeue there is a God, and tremble. For it should seeme, that the diuels know as euidently that there is a God, as we do that the Sunne did shine but yesterday, or this morning. For they once enioyed the presence of God, and sawe his glorie, and since haue had euident experience of his power.
9 Of Gods being (no doubt) they haue euident certaintie, albeit of his other attributes their knowledge is not so directly nor euident, but coniecturall, or a kind of beliefe. Wherefore vnto this
 place of our Apostle we may answere two wayes: Either that vnder this word [Beliefe] he comprehends not onely their assent vnto the being, or existence of the Godhead; but their assent vnto other attributes of God, which they know not so euidently, and therefore may be said to beleeue them. Or if he vnderstand onely that assent, which they gaue vnto the existence or beeing of the Godhead, he calleth this (though ioyned with euident knowledge) a beliefe, in opposition or with reference vnto the beliefe of hypocrites (against whom he there speakes) which was much lesse than this assent of diuells. For albeit that which is greater in the same kind, cannot be properly and absolutely said to be the lesse; as wee cannot properly and absolutely say that foure is three, but rather containes three in it: yet vpon some reference of the greater vnto the lesse contained in it, or vnto some other third, we may denominate the greater with the name of that which is lesse in the same kind: as wee may say of him that promised three and gaue foure, that he gaue three, because three is contained in foure. So the Philosopher saith, that Habitus est dispositio, euery habite is a disposition, not absolutely and properly, for it is more; yet because it is more, with reference vnto that which is lesse, or vnto the subiect in whom it is, we may say it is dispositio, that is, it containes disposition in it, albeit no man would say that habitus were dispositio, if he should define it.
10 And men vsually obiect to such as scoffe at matters of religion, that the day will come, wherein, if they repent not, they shal beleeue the things which now they little regard. Albeit they cannot be said in that day to beleeue them, if wee speake properly and absolutely, without reference to their former incredulitie. Our meaning is, they shall doe more than beleeue them, for they shall feele them. Nor can we say properly, that the elect after the resurrection shall beleeue the articles of faith: seeing all agree, that of these three principall vertues, Faith, Hope, and Loue, onely Loue shall then remaine. The reason is that which you haue heard alreadie; because euident knowledge must be excluded from the nature of faith and beliefe; and the godly shall then clearely see Christ face to face, and fully enioy the fruit of his passion, which now they only beleeue.
11 As for certaintie, we may not exclude it from the nature
 of beliefe, vnlesse this speech be warily vnderstood. For the certaintie of the articles of our saith ought to be greater, than the certaintie of other knowledge; for we must beleeue them, although they be contrarie to the capacitie of our vnderstanding: for euen this must we beleeue, that many things (as all supernatural things) surpasse the reach of our vnderstanding. Yet this we may safely say, that the certaintie of the articles of our beliefe, [as of Christ his death, of his and our resurrection,] cannot be so great to vs in this life, as it shall be in the world to come, when we shall euidently know them. This rule then is infallible; that the knowledge of any thing is more certaine, than the beliefe thereof: although the beliefe of some things (as of Christ his passion) bee more certaine, than the knowledge of other things; as namely, than the knowledge of humane sciences. So then out of this it is euident, that beliefe, taken generally, doth neither exclude all certaintie, nor necessarily require any; seeing some beliefe hath a kind of certaintie adioyned with it, and some cannot admit it. Wherefore it remaineth, that assent is the essence of beliefe in generall; I say such an assent, as is not ioyned with euidencie.
12 This assent may be weaker or stronger, and so come nearer vnto, or be further from certaintie, according to the nature of that obiect, whereunto we giue assent; or according to the nature of that, whereupon our beliefe is grounded; or lastly, according to our apprehension either of the obiect, or that which is the ground of our assent. Excesse in the first of these [to wit, in certaintie or stabilitie of the obiect] doth rather argue a possibilitie of firmer beliefe, or more credibilitie; not more firme or actuall beliefe. For as many things are more intelligible than others, and yet are least vnderstood of many: so many that are most credible, are least beleeued.
13 Excesse in the second of these, whence the assent of beliefe may be strengthened, [that is, in the ground of beliefe,] doth rather argue a stronger hypotheticall beliefe, than any absolute beliefe; vnlesse the apprehension or conceit of this ground be strong and liuely. In ordinary reports or contracts, it skils not of what credit the partie be, vnto whose credence or authoritie we are referred for the truth of any promise or report; vnlesse wee haue good inducements to thinke, that he did either say, or promise as
 we were told. If we be not thus perswaded by some apprehension of our owne; we giue onely conditional assent vnto the report or promise, and beleeue both with this limitation, [if he say so, whose credit wee so esteeme.] But if we can fully apprehend that he said so; we beleeue absolutely.
14 As in science or demonstrations it is requisite, both that we know the true cause of the effect, and also that we apprehend it certainly as the true cause; (otherwise we haue only an opinion:) so in true and absolute beliefe it is requisite, that we haue both a sure ground of our beliefe, and a true apprehension of that ground* otherwise our beliefe must needs be conditionall, not absolute. It remaineth therefore that we set downe, first the nature of the obiects that may be beleeued: secondly, the seuerall grounds of beliefe: and thirdly, the manner of apprehending them, albeit in some the apprehension of the obiect in it selfe, and the ground of beliefe, are in a sort all one; as in that beliefe, which is not grounded vpon the auhoritie of the teacher. This rule is generall; wheresoeuer the obiects are in themselues more credible, the ground may be more strong, and the apprehension more liuely, so men be capable of it, and industrious to seeke it: and equall apprehension of such obiects as are more credible in themselues, (vpon such grounds as are more firme) makes the beliefe stronger, than it could be of obiects lesse credible, or vpon grounds lesse firme. Ceteris paribus, euery one of these three, first, Greater credibilitie of the obiect, secondly, Surer ground of beliefe, thirdly, More liuely apprehension of the obiect or ground, encrease beliefe.
15 For the obiects of beliefe, (whence this assent must bee distinguished,) they are either naturall, or supernaturall; but first of tha^ which is naturall. The obiects of naturall beliefe are of two sorts, either scibilia, or opinabilia; either such things as may be euidently knowne in themselues, but are not so apprehended by him that beleeues them; or else such things, as we can haue no euident or certaine knowledge of, but onely an opinion. And of this nature are all the monuments of former ages, and relations of ancient times, in respect of vs which are now liuing: all future contingents, or such effects, as haue no necessarie naturall cause why they should bee, nor no ineuitable let or hinderance why they may not be; as whether wee should haue raine or faire weather the next
 moneth, whether such or such nations shall wage warre against each other the next yeare. These matters past, and contingent which are not yet, but may be, albeit they agree in the generall nature of opinabilia, that neither of the^ can be exactly knowne, but only by opinio^ beleued: yet both differ in that which is the ground of our assent or beliefe. The ground or reason why we beleeue things past, (as that Tully liued in Iulius Caesars time, or that the Saxons inhabited this land,) is the report of others. The ground or reason why we beleeue future contingents, is the inclination or propension which we see in second causes to produce such effects; or the coherence betwixt any naturall or morall contingent cause, and their possible or probable issue. As if we see one kingdom mightie in wealth, and at peace and vnitie in it selfe, bearing inueterate hate to another; or if we know that the one hath suffered wrong, not likely to be recompenced, and yet able in politique estimation to make it selfe amends: we beleeue that such will shortly be at open hostilitie one with another. Or if we see the ayre waterish, we beleeue it will shortly be rainy. Yet are not the grounds why we beleeue things past, and the grounds of beleeuing future contingents, alwayes so opposite, but that they may iumpe in one, and conspire mutually for the strengthening of beliefe. For we would beleeue our former coniecture of warre or weather a great deale the better, if a cunning States-man should giue iudgement of the one, or an Astronomer, or some that we know very weather-wise, his opinion of the other. For now, besides the probabilitie of our owne coniectures, we haue other mens authoritie to confirme our beliefe. In both kinds (either where the grounds of each are seuerall, or where both conspire together) as the ground of beliefe, or our apprehension of the ground is greater, so our beliefe waxeth stronger. THVS we beleeue the Romane stories of Caesars times more firmely, than the relations of Herodotus concerning matters of Aegypt, or other countries; because more Writers, and they such as are lesse suspected of vanitie or imposture, do testifie the truth of Romane affaires.
16 Other things, which are credible, or may be beleeued, are (as we said) scibilia; such things as may be exactly knowne by natura^l reason, though not of the partie which only beleeues them; (for exact knowledge alwayes expels meere beliefe of the same
 thing in the same partie.) That the Sunne is bigger than the earth, or that the motion thereof is swifter than any arrowes flight, may bee knowne exactly by a Mathematician; but ordinarie country-men (such as are not rustically wayward) do beleeue it; euidently and exactly know it they cannot. The ground of their beliefe in such a case is authoritas docentis. And this authoritie of teachers, or others, vpon whose assertions we rely, consisteth partly in a perswasion of the teachers or relators skill in those matters which he teacheth or relateth; and partly in his honestie, fidelitie, or veracitie in his dealings, or sayings. And as these are reputed greater, so do we more beleeue him in these things which he auoucheth for true, and rely more securely vpon his authoritie. For as we said before, Caeteris paribus, the certaintie of beliefe encreaseth as the ground of beliefe doth, both for the number of points belieued, and for the firmenesse of the beliefe it selfe. If two of the same facultie teach vs diuers things, whereof we haue no other ground but their assertion, we beleeue him better, whose skill and fidelitie we account of better; and the more the parties be that report or auouch the same thing, the more we beleeue them, if they be reputed skilfull and honest. And where the authoritie is the same, both for extension and degrees: yet we beleeue the things taught better, from the better or more immediate apprehension of the authoritie. As if Aristotle, Euclide, or Archimedes were aliue, and in that reputation for skill in their seuerall professions, which their workes are in; we would beleeue those conclusions, which wee heard them teach, better than such as we had from them by others, or (as we said) at the second hand. For though the authoritie in both cases were the same: yet should not our apprehension of it be so; but more immediate in the former. We see by daily experience, how opinions, only grounded vpon the authoritie of teachers for their skill in such matters well reputed of, doe inforce others (especially inferiours in that kind of skill) to giue an assent vnto the same truth, although they haue good shew of reason to the contrarie. As what country-man is there, but would thinke he might safely sweare, that the earth were an hundred times greater than the Sunne? yet if an Astronomer, (of whose skill he hath had experience in other matters, which he can better discerne) one whom he knew to be an honest plaine dealing man, not accustomed
 to cog with his friend, should seriously auouch the contrarie, that the Sunne is bigger then the earth; few country men would be so wayward as not to beleeue their friend Astronomer, albeit (his authoritie set aside) they had no reason to thinke so, but rather the contrarie. And it were a signe of ignorant arrogancie, if punies or fresh-men should reiect the axiomes and principles of Aristotle, vsuall in the schooles, because they haue some reasons against them, which themselues cannot answere. For reason might tell them, that others (their betters) which haue gone before them, haue had greater reasons to hold them, then they can yet haue to deny them. This perswasion of other mens skill or knowledge will winne the assent of modest and ingenuous youths, vnto such rules or axiomes, as otherwise they would stiffely deny, and haue wittie reasons to ouerthrow. But albeit this assent, which men giue to conclusions, they know not themselues, but onely beleeue vpon other mens asseuerations, may be very great; as many countrymen will beleeue an Astronomer affirming that the Sun is greater then the earth, better then they will the honestest of their neighbours in a matter that may concerne both their commodities: yet if the relators or auouchers could make them conceiue any probable reason of the same conclusions, [as if the Astronomer in the mentioned case could shew, how euery body the further it is from vs seemeth the lesse, and then declare how many hundred miles the Sunne is from vs:] mens minds would be a great deale better satisfied, and this assent or beliefe, which formerly did onely relie vpon authoritie, would be much strengthened by this second tie or hold-fast. And if we would obserue it, there is vsually a kind of regresse betwixt our beliefe of authorities, and our assent vnto conclusions taught by them. First (vsually) we beleeue authoritie, and afterwards the conclusions taught by it, for the authorities sake. But after we once find experiment of the truth of conclusions so taught, we beleeue the authoritie the better from this experimentall truth of the conclusion.
17 But of all these acceptions and degrees of beliefe or assent, something may be gathered for better expressing the seuerall degrees of true Christian beliefe; which like Iacobs ladder reacheth from earth to heauen. The first step whereof is beliefe or assent vnto things supernaturall.




The first generall part. SECT. 2.

CHAP. I. Of assent vnto obiects supernaturall.
 THings supernatural we call such, as the naturall reason of man cannot attaine vnto; or such, as naturally can neither bee knowne or assented vnto as probable, but are made knowne or probable by reuelation. Such are the mysteries of our saluation, and the articles of christian beliefe. For no article of our beliefe (if we consider them with all the circumstances, and in that exact manner as they are proposed in Scripture to be beleeued) could euer haue come into corrupted mens cogitation, vnlesse God had reuealed it vnto him. Seeing then we cannot know them in any sort by humane reason and authoritie; neither can humane reason or authority be the ground of our assenting to them; it remaineth then, that authoritas docentis, the word of God, be the ground of our beliefe.
2 Here then must you call to mind what wee said before, that authoritas docentis did consist in two things; namely in the ski^l, and fidelitie, or sinceritie of the Teacher: and by how much we know those to be greater, by so much is our assent or beliefe strengthened. Now it is euident to reason that God is infinitely wise, and therefore can not be deceiued; whence necessarily it followeth that hee knoweth, and can tell vs the truth. Againe it is euident that God is most iust and true, and therefore will not deceiue vs, but will tell vs the truth if hee professe so. Againe, wee can not conceiue of God aright, but wee must conceiue of him as omnipotent, and full of power; and consequently such a one as needes not in policie, or iealousie of our emulating him in knowledge, to tell vs otherwise than he knowes.Lib. 1. ca 2. And therefore Aristotle in the beginning of his Metaphysickes reprehendeth the Poets, for saying,
 that the Gods did enuie men knowledge. His resolution is in English to this effect,
That Poets should the Gods belie,(...).More like, than Gods should men enuie.
3 And if the Heathen were of opinion, that the Gods did not enuie men knowledge; then must they needes beleeue, that if they taught them any, they would teach them true knowledge. Wherfore this must be laid downe as a certaine ground, that whatsoeuer God teacheth vs, is most true. Nor is there any (admitting there were a God) but would assent vnto this. But here is the difficultie. How can we be assured that God doth teach vs any of these things? Or, how shall we know, that this wee call Scripture, is the word of God? If our apprehension of this ground be sure, our faith is firme and absolute: if our apprehension hereof be doubtfull, our faith must needs be vnstable, or, at the best, but conditionall.
4 Let vs first therefore looke what this conditionall assent or beliefe doth bind vs vnto.
Although many, that firmely beleeue whatsoeuer God saith, is true, either doe not acknowledge or doe not firmely beleeue that these Scriptures are the word of God: yet thus much in all sense and reason any naturall man will grant; there bee great presumptions and probabilities, why they should be taken for the word of God. And he that doth acknowledge but thus much, doth by this acknowledgement bind himselfe to reuerence them aboue all humane writings. For all men naturally know, that if they be Gods word, they are worthy all possible reuerence. Wherefore if a man suppose it only as probable, that they are, or know nothing ro the contrary why they may not be Gods word: he is bound to reuerence and esteeme them aboue all words or writings of man. As for example; If any subiect in this land should receiue letters concerning some lawfull and indifferent request from any other his equal or fellow subiect, whom he had great reason wel to respect; suppose he certainly knew that they were such a mans letters, and no counterfaite: yet if hee should receiue letters in his Maiesties name, containing the same or other as reasonable request: although he knew not so certainly that these were his Maiesties letters,
 as that the others were some wel-respected Subiects, yet is he in duty bound to vse them with greater respect and reuerence, than the former. The bare presumption and probabilitie that they were the Kings letters, doth bind him to inquire further, whether they were his Letters or no: nor were his fault excusable, if hee should shew any signe of disloyalty, or irreuerence towards them, vntill hee knew that they were not such, as their Title or Superscription did import.
5 He that hath but the same probabilitie, that the Scriptures containe in them Gods owne wordes, as that Liuie his Histories containe the Roman affaires, must needs esteeme of them infinitly aboue all humane workes. And this fruit hypotheticall or supposed beliefe may bring forth, euen in the vnregenerate or natural man. And what hath beene said of reuerence to the Scriptures vpon this supposall, is also most true of mans actions. If men doe but beleeue it as probable, that the Scriptures are the Word of God: this beliefe will procure many good morall actions, and much amendment of life; though not such spirituall perfection, as God in his word requireth. And the reaso^ of this assertion is euident. For we see daily, that men vndertake actions of great difficultie and danger, not so much according to the probabilitie of attaining some good, as according to the greatnesse of that good which possibly may be attained. So we see many, that might liue in ease at home with certaintie of moderate gaines, to vndertake voyages to the West or East Indies, onely vpon this resolution, That if it be their lucke or lot to bee rich, there they may haue enough; although the aduenture bee subiect to great dangers, and obnoxious to infinite casualties. And many there be, that wil not vsually lay out a penny, but vpon very faire ground of some gaine or sauing thrift, who yet will be well content to venture a crowne or an angell in a Lotterie, where there may bee some possibilitie, though no probabilitie, of obtaining twentie or thirtie pounds. These and infinite other examples, obuious to daily experience, may serue as a perfect induction of our generall assertion: That the meere possibilitie of obtaining some great and extraordinary good, is of greater moment in swaying mens actions, than certaintie of accomplishing pettie desires, or greatest probabilities of purchasing ordinarie commodities or delights. To deduce then out of this
 generall the particular we intended: In the Scripture are promised to all such as loue God and do his will, farre greater blessings, than humane knowledge could euer haue conceiued. The like is true in auoyding dangers. Men oftentimes vndertake matters of more difficulty and charges, to preuent some grieuous mischaunces which may insue, than they would doe to escape some imminent but ordinary daunger, or to release themselues from some smaller harmes that already haue befallen them. Could men consider these things seriously, and account of them but as probable: what is there in this life, which in any reason they should not venture for the obtaining of so great a good? Were men but probably perswaded that there were (as the Scriptures and the articles of our Beliefe tell vs) a life euerlasting, full laden with all the fruites of true life, ioy, peace and all choisest pleasures, without any annoyance: how could they not be most readie, and willing to spend this whole transitorie life (whose dayes are but few, and most of them euill, full fraught with griefe and distresse) in the seruice of God, who would thus reward them? Yea, how could they not be desirous to lay downe this life it selfe, vpon hope of obtaining such a life? For this life compared to that to come, hath not the proportion of a farthing to whole millions of Golde, or all the treasures in this world. Nor is the case herein like vnto that of aduentures or Lotterie; where a man may venture his life, or goods if he list, but if hee doe not, none calleth him to an accompt for not doing it: But in the Scriptures euerlasting torments, griefe, and perpetuall horror are threatned to all such, as frame not their liues according to Gods will in them reuealed.



The first generall Part. SECT. 2. CHAP. II. Of generall incitements to search the truth of Scriptures or Christian beliefe.
 WE may hence clearely see how inexcusable, euen in the iudgement of flesh and bloud, all men are, that either by hearing or reading haue anie accesse vnto the Gospell, and doe not vse the best indeuours of their naturall witte (if God as yet haue touched their hearts with no better grace) to search out the truth thereof. For seeing in the Scriptures are proposed to euery mans choice euerlasting life, or euerlasting death: what extreame madnesse is it for men to enter into any course of life, or to vndertake any matter of moment, which may exact their chiefe imployments, before they haue diligently looked to the maine chaunce? before they haue tried the vtmost of their wits, and others best aduise, to know the tenour of their owne estate? Wee see daily what great paines men of no small account doe take in the studie of Alchymie, spending their spirites, and most of their substance in trying conclusions, and searching out the truth of those things, for which they haue but weake grounds of Philosophie or reason; onely the conceit of the good they aime at, (which is rather possible than probable for them to attaine) inforceth a kinde of hope, and incourageth them to goe forward.
2 To speake nothing of the good the Scripture promiseth, the very conceit of eternall death (me thinkes) should moue, either the Chymickes, (which spend much gold only vpon hope of getting more) or any other man whatsoeuer, to spend all the treasure, whatsoeuer either this their Art, or all other could yeeld, to secure themselues from such horrible torments, as the Scriptures
 threaten to their Contemners or negligent Hearers. And why should not all men then in reason bestow most time and paines, in searching the truth of those things, which concerne their soules estate; whose securitie in all reason they should purchase with the highest hopes, and vtmost aime of all other trauelles in this life. Here then (as I said) the full height of mans iniquitie, and his inexcusable madnesse, is most plainely discouered; that hauing these two motiues, which in naturall reason doe sway all humane actions, offering themselues to incourage him in searching the Scriptures: yet notwithstanding most men bestow lesse labor in them, than in other ordinarie studies. First, if we compare the good they set before vs, as a recompence and reward of our trauelles, it is beyond all comparison greater than the scope of any other trade, or science. For here is a double infinitie of solid good; first, they promise ioy two waies infinit, both in degree and continuance; secondly, they threaten vnto their contemners and despisers death and torments doubly infinite, both in degree and continuance. Now if the probabilities of the truth of Scriptures were farre lesse, than is vsually found in other studies, or humane hopes: yet could this in humane reason be no reason why wee should labour lesse in them, than in other affaires; seeing the incomparable excesse of the good they promise, doth aboundantly recompence this. But if the probabilitie of the truth of Scripture, be in naturall reason equall to the probabilities which men vsually take for their grounds in many greatest attempts; then certainly not to bestow as great paines and trauell, in trying the truth of their promises, as in any other humane attempts, or affaires, doth argue infinit madnesse. Aske we the Chymick, what reason he hath to toile so much in the studie of Paracelsus, or other intricate writers of his facultie; (the like we may say of any Physitians:) their answere (as you may reade in their writings) is this: Many Philosophers in former ages haue laboured much in this studie, and haue set downe good rules of their experiments; who (as is probable) would neuer haue taken such paines vpon no ground. And verily this tradition, or the authoritie they giue to their Writers, is their chiefe motiue. For I thinke few of their ancient Authors haue bequeathed to their successors any gold made by this art, thereby to encourage them. If then tradition, consent of time, or approbation of authors, or
 relation of experiments, be an especiall inducement for men to aduenture their charge, paines, and trauell in this facultie, as in all other affaires: without all controuersie the Scriptures in all these motiues haue an especiall prerogatiue aboue all other faculties or sciences, albeit humane reason were admitted iudge. For the authoritie of Gods Church is farre more generall, then the consent of any writers in one faculty whatsoeuer. The consent of time likewise is greater. For no age since Christs time in these ciuill parts of the world, but by the report of other Writers, as well as Christians, hath yeelded obedience vnto Scriptures as the word of God. Men of most excellent spirits and learning in euery age, haue addicted their studies vnto this truth. About the time of our Sauiours comming, curious arts, and other ciuill disciplines did most flourish. The Grecians sought after wisdome and secular Philosophie with the like; the Romans after policie, state-knowledge, and discipline of warre; all the world almost, (aboue others, those places wherein Christianitie was first planted) was then set vpon curious arts: yet we see how the studie and search of Scriptures in short time did proue, as Aarons rod amongst the Magitians serpents. It hath deuoured all, and brought them to acknowledge allegiance vnto it; vsing the helpe of best secular arts, as it were nutriment for the growth of Christianitie, and expelling the rest as excrements out of the Church. Nor can the Atheist name any age, wherein the Heathen had an Oliuer to oppugne our profession,Porphyrius in vita Plautini. but we had a Rowland to defend it: if they had a Porphyrie or Celsus to oppose philosophie against it, we had an Origen (a man, by their owne confession, of the most rare wit and hope for philosophie then liuing,) to forsake philosophie and follow Christianitie. It was not despaire which made him and many other excellent schollers, Christians: but the sure hope which they found in this profession, made them contemne all other hopes, and cleaue to it with their hearts and soules; albeit their soules should for so doing be violently separated from their bodies. This triall, I am perswaded, few of their greatest Philosophers would haue endured; but they had the potentates of the world, as readie to applaud them, as to disgrace the Christians; and yet the Christians multiplied as the Israelites did by oppression in Aegypt. How resolute they were, if we may not be beleeued bearing witnes of our own
 profession, letEpistol. lib. 10. ep. 100. ad Traianum. In ijs, qui ad me tanquam Christiani deferebantur, hunc sum sequntus modum. Interrogaui ipsos, an essent Christiani: confitentes iterum, ac tertio interrogaui, supplicium minatus persenerantes duci iussi. Neque enim dubitaui, qualecunque esset quod fatere^tur? peruicaciam certe, and inflexibilem obstinationem debere puniri. Plinie testifie, in whose iudgement, constancie and resolution was the only crime in our profession deseruing punishment. And for this cause he tooke want of resolution, in such as had been accused before him vnder the name of Christians, as a sufficient argument that they were not ChristiansIbid. Propositus est libellus sine autore multorum nomina continens, qui negint se esse Christianos aut fuisse. Quum prae eunte me deos appellarent, and imagini tuae, quam propter hoc iusseram cum simulachris numinum adferri, thure, ac vino s^p^l carent, praeterea maledicerent Christe; quorum nihil cogi posse dicuntur, qui sunt re vera Christiani; dimittendos putaui. in deed or heart; for such, as he had been informed, could not be inforced to any such idolatrous practise as he perswaded these men vnto.
3 Lastly, the experiments which are related by Authors of this profession, men (in any reasonable mans iudgement) as much to be beleeued herein, as any other writers in theirs; are farre more notable, and apt to produce beliefe, and hope of attaining the truth in this profession, than any others can haue in theirs. The experiments of others were but ordinarie and naturall; these are extraordinarie and supernaturall. If the Atheist should impudently deny the truth of their report: we may conuince him with Saint Augustines acute dilemma. If the miracles related by our Writers be true, they giue euident experiment of the truth of Scripture: if there were no such particular miracles, but all feigned; then this was a miracle aboue all miracles, that Christian religion should preuaile against all other arts, power, or policie, without any extraordinary euent or miracle. It was not so easie a matter to cozen all the Romane Emperours, and their deputies, with feigned tales; the world, which hated Christians so much, was inquisitiue enough to know the truth of their reports. I may conclude; nisi veritas magna fuisset non praeualuisset. It was miraculous, doubtlesse, that it should so encrease without armes; without any promise of carnall pleasure, or securitie: but euen against their naturall inclination that did professe it, and all the worlds opposition against it. It had enemies both priuate and publike, domestique and forren, euen the flesh and sence of those which followed it fought against it.
4 Mahomet since that time hath found a multitude of followers; but all either inforced to follow him by threats of shame, disgrace, and tortures in this life; or else allured thereto by faire promises of carnall pleasures to be perpetuall without interruption in the life to come. He hath set his followers such a course, as they might be sure both of winde and tide. And if the hauen whereat they arriue were as safe as their course is e^sie, they were of all men
 the most happy. But Christianitie from its first beginning, was to rowe against the streame of flesh and bloud, and to beare out saile against all the blasts that the diuell, world, or flesh could oppose against it. In a word, the increase of Mahumetisme hath followed the barbarous Turkish monarchies aduancement, as moysture in bodies doth the increasing fulnesse of the Moone. And it had bin an extraordinary miracle, if a barbarous multitude (neuer acquainted with any ciuill pleasures,) should not haue composed theyr mindes vnto their Emperours; in following a religion, framed, as it were, to court the senses, and wooe the flesh. But Christianitie then flourished most, when the scorching heat of persecution was at the height: When the countenance of Emperours, as terrible to their foes (for their heroicall valour) as plausible to their friends (for their louely carriage) were most fiercely set against it. What Princes either more terrible to their enemies, or more amiable to their friends, than Traian, Dioclesian, or others of the Christians persecutors were? What man liuing is there of ciuill education, that would not haue lothed Mahomet, and the whole succession of the Ottoman familie, in respect of these Roman Princes? And yet a great part of their natiue subiects, men, as otherwise excellently qualified, so of a quiet and peaceable disposition; yet readie alwayes to venture their liues for these heathen Princes, in most dangerous seruice against the enemies of the Roman Empire, but most ready to follow the crucified Christ through fire and sword, against their Emperors command (dearer to them than this mortall life) and all the worlds threats, or allurements. It were sottish to thinke, that such men had not perfect notice of some higher powers commaundement to the contrarie, (whom they thought it safer to obey) when they contradicted the commaundements, or faire allurements of these supreame earthly powers. And it were as seely a persuasion to thinke, that, if the great Turke would change his religion for any other, that might yeeld like hopes of carnall pleasure after this life, any great number of his subiects would loose their dignities for refusing Subscription.
5 The briefe of what hath beene, or may be said concerning the grounds or motions of our assent vnto obiects supernaturall, may be comprised in these 4. propositions following; of which the first two are axiomes euident in nature, and receiued by all:
 The two latter vndoubted axiomes amongest true beleeuers, but suppositions onely to meere naturall men, or nouices in Christianitie.
6 The first, The stile or title of these sacred Books pretending diuine authoritie, binde all men to make triall of their truth, commended to vs by our ancestours, confirmed to them by the bloud of Martyrs their predecessors; to vse the meanes which they prescribe for this triall; that is, abstinence from things forbidden, and alacritie in doing things commaunded by them.
7 The second, Ordinarie apprehension, or naturall beliefe of matters contained in Scriptures, or the Christian Creede, are of more force to cause men vndertake any good, or abstaine from anie euill, than the most firme beliefe of ordinarie matters, or anie points of meere naturall consequence.
8 The third, Obiects and grounds of Christian beliefe, haue in them greater stabilitie of truth, and are in themselues more apt to found most strong and firme beliefe, than any other things whatsoeuer meerely credible.
9 For, as the most noble essences, and first principalls of euery Art, are most intelligible: so are diuine truths of all other most credible. Not that they are more easie to be assented to of anie, at their first proposall, but that they haue a greater measure of credibilitie in them: and as their credibilitie and truth is inexhaustible, so beliefe of them once planted, can neuer grow to such fulnesse of certaintie, as not to receiue daily increase, if we apply our minds diligently vnto them: so that true Christian beliefe admits no stint of growth in this life, but still comes nearer and nearer to that euidencie of knowledge, which shall swallow it vp in the life to come. For the conceit of impossibilities are repugnances in nature, obiected by the obdurate Atheists to make the principles of Christian Religion seeme incredible (that they might like olde truants haue the company of nouices in Christianitie to loiter, or mis-spend good houres with them:) we shall by Gods assistance dispell them, and all other cloudes of like errours, in vnfoulding the truth of those Articles, which they most concerne.
10 The fourth, the means of apprehending the truth of Scriptures, and experiments confirming their diuine authoritie, are, both for varietie of kindes, and number of indiuidualles in euerie
 kind, farre more and more certaine, than the meanes of apprehending the grounds of any other beliefe, or the experiments of anie other teachers authoritie.
11 Some particulars of euery kinde, with the generall heads or common places whence like obseruations may be drawne, we are now to present, so farre as they concerne the confirmation of the truth of Scriptures in generall. For the experiments, which confirme the truth of such particular places of Scripture, as teach the Articles of our Creede expresly, will come more fitly into the vnfolding of the Articles themselues.




The second generall part of the first Booke.

SECT. 1.
Containing Experiments or Obseruations confirming the truth of Scriptures in generall.
ALthough the Experiments confirming the trueth of Scriptures be, as I haue said, many and diuers; yet all may bee reduced into these two generall heads or kinds. They may be found, either in the stile or character of these writings themselues; the affections or dispositions of their writers; or in euents or experiments (whatsoeuer the course of times affords) answerable to the rules set downe in scriptures.

SECT. I. Of Experiments of the former kind. CHAP. I. Of Historicall Characters of sacred writings.
TO anie man indued with reason not peruerted, the Bookes of Moses giue more perfect proofe of matters done and acted, than anie other historie in the world can possibly doe: albeit we set aside the secret Characters of Gods spirit speaking in them, which
 we suppose can be discerned of none, but such as haue the marke of the Lambe answereable to it ingrauen vpon their hearts: But now wee seeke such inducements to beliefe, as may perswade the naturall vnregenerate man of the historicall truth of these sacred volumes.
2 The preiudices arising from the strangenesse of matters related by him, the Reader shall finde mitigated in the next discourse: In the meane time I must request him to suspend his iudgement of them, and onely to intend the liuely characters of historicall truth in other relations, of matters neither strange nor incredible in themselues. Either he wrote a true historie, or else his wordes are but a fiction: either poeticall to delight others, or politicall to aduantage himselfe or his successors. Ler such as doubt of theyr historicall truth, duely examine, whether many things related by him can possibly be referred to any of these two ends. As for example, if these relations, Genesis 4.1, and the 25. had not beene either reall adiuncts of some famous truth then sufficiently known, or else appoynted by God to be notified for some speciall purpose to posteritie: Low could it possibly haue come into anie mans thought, or to what end should it haue gone thence into his pen, to shew the reason why Euah should call her first son Caine, or her third Seth?
3 He that would set himselfe to contradict, might replie; Moses his inuention was so copious, as to forecast that those insertions might make his Historie seeme more probable: or that hee spake vnawares according to the custome of the times wherein he liued. But why then should he omit the like in all the generations from Cain and Seth vnto Noah; the reason of whose name giuen him by his father he likewise specifies, Genesis 5.29. Then Lamech begate a sonne, and called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort vs concerning our worke, and sorrow of our hands, as touching the earth which the Lord hath cursed? It was doubtlesse from some diuersitie in the matter presupposed vnto this worke, not from the workemans choice or inuention, why the reason of these three mens names should be specified; as afterwards will more plainly appeare. For the positiue notes, or sure tokens of a true historie, they are most plentiful^ in the Stories of Abraham, Iacob, and Ioseph. Each part of which the Diuine Prouidence, (where of these
 are the most ancient, most perfect, most liuely patternes,) would haue set out with such perspicuitie of all circumstances, that the Reader might bee an eie-witnesse of their historicall truth. The subiect and issue whereof is in it selfe so pleasant, as will rauish sober and attentiue mindes, and allure them to follow the maine current of Diuine Mysteries, which flowe from these Histories mentioned, as from their first heads or fountaines. To point at some few, rather than handle any particulars.
4 If we may iudge of the truth of mens writings by their outward forme or character, as wee doe of mens honestie by their lookes, speach or behauiour: what historie in the world beares so perfect resemblance of things done and acted, or yieldes (without further testimonie than its owne,) so full assurance of a true narration, as the stories of Abrahams departure out of his Land, his answers to God; Sarahs distrust vpon misconceit of Gods promises; her seeking to fulfill it by giuing her Maid to Abraham, the maner of her speach vpon her Maides contemning her; the debatement and issue of her controuersie, the dialogue betweene the Angells of God and Abraham, with Sarahs apologie for her laughing at their message; Abrahams iourney to mount Moriah, his seruants expedition to Aram Naharim, with his commission to prouide his young maister a wife? There appeares not in any of these the least surmise of any politicall respect, not any signe of affected delight, or poeticall representations: seeing this Author falles immediately into other matters, and relates euerie thing (though many of most diuerse natures) with such naturall specification of euerie circumstance, as vnlesse our hearts were praepossest with beliefe, that hee had writ them by his direction, who perfectly knoweth all things, aswell fore-past, as present, or to come, we would be perswaded that most of them were relations of such as acted them, vttered to their familiar friends immediatly vpon the fact, whilest all circumstances were fresh in memorie.
5 How others are affected I can not tell, mee thinkes when I reade that storie, Genesis 34. I am transformed into a man of the old world, and become a neighbor of old Iacob, ouer-hearing him and his sonnes debating the slaughter of the Sichemites. The old man complaines; Yee haue troubled mee, and made me stincke among the inhabitants of the Land, as well the Canaanites as the Perizites:
 and I being few in number, they shall gather themselues together against me, and so shall I and mine house be destroyed and they answered and said; Shall he abuse our sister as a whoore?
6 Or if this description, though issuing as naturally out of the reall disposition of the true, no fained parties described, as brightnesse out of the bodie of the Sunne, yet because but short, may seeme more imitable by art: I will propose a longer dialogue betwixt this old man and his sons for a patterne: of which the fairest colours, that Art or Inuention can put vpon any fained Subiect, will come as farre short, as Salomons gawdy, but artificiall attire, did of the natiue beautie of wild Lillies; or any die that Arte can giue of the naturall splendor of finest pearles, the Onix or other more precious stone. The story is,Genesis 42.29. And they came to Iacob their father vnto the land of Cana ^, and told him al that had befallen them, saying, vers. 30. The man, who is Lord of the Land, spake roughly vnto vs, and put vs in prison as Spies of the Countrey v. 31. And we said vnto him, wee are true men, and are no Spies. verse 32. Wee bee twelue brethren, sonnes of our father: one is not, and the yongest is this day with our father in the Land of Canaan, verse 33. Then the Lord of the Countrey said vnto vs, Hereby shall I know if yee be true men. Leaue one of your brethren with me, and take food for the famine of your houses, and depart, verse 34. and bring your yongest brother vnto me; that I may know that ye are no Spies, but true men: So wil I deliuer you your brother, and ye shal occupie in the Land. verse 35. And as they emptied their sackes, behold, euery mans bundell of money was in his sacke, and when they and their father saw the bundels of their money, they were afraid verse 36. Then Iacob their father said vnto them, Ye haue robbed me of my children: Ioseph is not, and Simeon is not, and ye will take Beniamin: all these things are against me, verse 37. Then Reuben answered his father, saying, Slay my two sonnes, if I bring him not to thee againe: Deliuer him to mine hand, and I will bring him to thee againe, verse 38. But he said, My sonne shall not goe downe with you: for his brother is dead, and hee is left alone: if death come vnto him by the way which yee goe, then yee shall bring my gray gead with sorrow vnto the graue. Chapter 43. verse 1. Now great famine was in the land. verse 2. And when they had eaten of the vict^als, which they had brought from Aegypt, their father said vnto them, Turne aga^ne, and buy vs a little food. verse 3. And Iudah answered him, saying, The man charged vs by an oath, saying, Neuer see m ^^ce, e^cept your brother be with you. verse 4. If thou wilt send our Brother with vs, wee will goe downe and buy thee food: verse 5. but if thou wilt not send him, wee will not goe downe: for the man said vnto vs, Looke me not in the face, except your Brother be with you. Genesis 42. from the 29. vnto the 15. verse of the 43. Chap. The circumstances, which I would especially commend vnto the Readers consideration, are; first, the old mans iealousie, verse 36. vpon his sonnes relation what had befallen them in their iourney, and the Gouernours desire of seeing Beniamin, verse 31.32.33.34.35. his peremptorie replie, v. 38. to Reubens answer, v. 37. the manner of his relenting, chap. 43. v. 6. vpon necessitie of their going for more food; and his sonnes peremptorie refusall to goe without Beniamin, in the fiue first verses
 of the 43. Chap.Verse 6 And Israel said, Wh^refore draft yee so euill with me, as to tel the man, whe^her yee had yet a brother, or no? verse 7. And they answered, The man asked straitly of our selues, and of our kinred, saying, Is your father yet aliue? Haue yee any brother? And we told him according to these wordes. Could we know certainly that hee would say, Bring your brother down? verse 8. Then said Iudah, to Israel his father, Send the boy with me, that wee may rise and goe, and that wee may l ue and not die, both we, and thou, and our ch ldren verse 9. I will bee suretie for him: of my hand shalt thou require him: If I bring him not to thee, and se^ him before th^e, then let mee beare the blame for euer. verse 10 For except yee had made thi^ tarrying, doubtlesse by this wee had returned the second time. verse 11. Then their Father said vnto them, If it must needes bee so now, doe thus: Take of the best fruits of the Land in your vessels* and bring the man a present, a little rozen, a little hony, spices and myrrhe, nuts, and almonds, vers^ 12. And take double mony in your hand, and the mony that was brought againe in your sack^ mouths(...) carry it againe in your hand, lest it were some ouersight. verse 13. Take also your brother, and arise, an^ goe againe to ^he man, verse 14 And GOD Almightie giue you mercie in the sight of the man(...) that hee may deliuer you your other brother, and Beniamin: but I shall bee robbed of my child, as(...) haue beene. his condescending, verse 11. vpon their iust apologie for mentioning their yongest brother to the Gouernours and Iudahs vndertaking for Beniamins safe conduct backe, and forth, in the 10.9.8. and 7. verses; lastly, the close or epiphonema of his speech, verse 13 and 14. Whilest I compare one of the circumstances with another, and all of them with other precedent and consequent, (chiefly with Iudahs speech to Ioseph, Genesis 44. from the sixteenth verse to the end of the Chapter) although I knew no other Scripture to make me a Christian, this one place would perswade me to become a Pythagorean, and thinke that my soule had been in some of Iacobs sonnes, where it had heard this controuersie; rather than to imagine, that it could haue beene fained by any that liued long after.
7 Or if we consider not the particular relations onely, but the whole contriuance and issue of this storie; what paterne of like inuention had Moses to follow? If the Atheist graunt such a Diuine prouidence, as hee describes; let him tell vs whence hee learned it. If from any more Ancient description, let this be suspected for artificiall; if not, let this bee acknowledged for the first and naturall representation of it. Without either a former patterne to imtate, or true resemblance of such a Diuine Prouidence in euents immediatly to bee related; how could such a Supreame power, gouerning and disposing all thinges contrary to the designes and purposes of man, be by mortall man conce^ued? More probable is the Poets fiction, that Minerua should be conceiued in Iupiters braine; than that humane fancie should bring forth a more Omnipotent, more wise or excellent deitie, than the Poets make their Iupiter, without any true Image of his Prouidence, manifested in the effectes? But after the manifestation of it in the story of Ioseph,
 and the liue-picture of it taken by Moses; all imitation of it was not so difficult, though he that would seeke to imitate him fully should herein come as far short of the solide marks of his historicall truth, as the Egyptians Iuglers tricks did of true miracles.
8 As all these, and many other places yeelde vndoubted characters of true historicall narrations, so doe his speeches vnto this people, Deut. 39.30, 31. infallible symptomes of a dying man, and one that indeed had borne this mighty nation, as an Eagle beares her yong ones vpon her wings. These admirable straines of his heauenly admonitions, and diuine Prophecies, compared with the liue images of former truths, witnesse, that hee was the Ianus of Prophets, Va^es oculatus tam praeteritorum quam futurorum, one that could both cleerely see what had beene done before his birth, and what should fall out after his death: Both which shall hereafter (God willing) better appeare, by matters related, and euents foretold by him.
9 But to proceede, the whole historicall part of the Bible, not Moses his Bookes alone, yeeld plenty of such passages, as beeing compared with other circumstances, or the maine drift and scope of the entire stories, whereof they are parts, leaue no place for imagination, either why they should, or how possibly they could haue beene inserted by Art or Imitation; or haue come into any mans thoughts, not moued by the reall occurrence of such occasions a^ are specified in the matters related. And seeing all of them are related by such as affect no Art; many of them by such as liued long after the parties that first vttered or acted them: wee can not conceiue how al particulars could be so naturally and fully recorded, vnlesse they had beene suggested by his spirite, who giueth mouth and speech to man, who is alike present to all successions, able to communicate the secret thoughts of forefathers to their children, and put the very words of the deceased, neuer registred before, in the mouths or pennes of their successours for many generations after, as distinctly and exactly, as if they had bin caught in characters of steele or brasse as they issued out of their mouths,
10 When I reade that Speech in Ouid, 4. Metam. Fab. 8.


Siue es mortalis, qui te genuere beati


Et mater foelix, and fortunata profecto


Si qua tibi soror est, and quae dedit vbera nutrix.


If mortall thou, thrice happy sure thy parents bee;
Or if thou any sister hast, thrice happy shee;
Thrice happy Nurse, whose breasts gaue sucke to thee.

I see no inducement to beleeue this for a true Storie, because I know the end and aime of his writing was, to inuent Verisimilia, to faine such speeches, as best befitted the persons whose part hee tooke vpon him to expresse, thereby to delight his hearers with varietie of liuely representations. But when I reade that narration of our Sauiors Apologie for himselfe against the Iewes, which said he had an vncleane spirit, Luke 11.14. and a woman comming in with her verdict; Now blessed is the wombe that bare thee, and the pappes that gaue thee sucke, verse 27. This vnexpected straine, with our Sauiours reply vnto it; Yea rather, blessed are those that heare the word of God and keepe it, verse 28. so briefly inserted into the storie, inforce me to thinke, that it was penned by one that sought only to relate the truth, part of which was this womans speech. But with the meanes of knowing the New Testament to be the word of God, I will not heere meddle: the Old Testament sufficiently proueth it, besides many other experiments to bee prosecuted in the vnfolding of sundry articles.


CHAP. II. Of the Harmonie of sacred Writers.
AN other Inducement for beleeuing the truth of the Old Testament, is the harmonie of so many seuerall writers, liuing in such distance of Ages, handling such diuersitie of arguments, and couering them with stiles, for the maiestie of some, and the familiaritie of others, more different, than Virgils verses, and the rudest countrymans talke; and yet all of them retaining the selfe same relish. Whiles we reade Tully, Virgil, Liuie, Salust and Ouid, though all liuing neare about one time; yet their writings differ as much as flesh and fish. Many learned men like some one or few of these, and yet much mislike others, reputed as excellent writers in their
 kind, liuing about the same time: much more might he that should haue read the common or vulgar Historiographers, Poets, or Orators of that time, haue contemned them as base in respect of the former. But the Prophets of the Old Testament, and the Historiographers of the same, though differing infinitely in degrees of stile and inuention; yet agree as well in the substance or essentiall qualitie of their writings, as the same Pomander chafed and vnchafed. There is the same odour of life, and goodnesse in both, but more fragrant and piercing in the one than in the other. And no man that much likes the one, can mislike the other: hee may like it ^esse, but dislike it he cannot, if he like the other:
Omnibus est illis vigor and coelestis origo.
2 Many other inducements of this kind are set downe at large, by that Flower of France, and glorie of Christian Nobilitie, in the 24. and 25. chapters of his booke De Veritate Christianae religionis, of the Truth of Christian religion: as also in Ficinus and Viues, whose labours it is hard to say whether he hath more augmented or graced. One especiall motiue is from the drift, and scope of all these sacred writings; whether Histories, Prophets, Psalmes, or the Gospel. The end and scope of all these, is onely to set out the glorie of God, and the good of mankinde. In their most famous victories, and good successe of their best contriued policies, they ascribe the glorie wholly to God. There is no circumstance inserted to erect the praise of man, not of the chiefest managers of such affaires. They account it the greatest praise that can be giuen vnto their Worthies, to let the world know they were beloued of God, and that God did fight for them. Not one Writer in this sacred volume bewraies the least signe of enuy towards others, that liued with him, or had gone before him: Not one that giueth the least suspition of seeking his owne praise by lessening others deserts, as if hee had corrected wherein others had erred, or finished what they had well begunne, but left imperfect. No imitation in any of them to let posterity vnderstand, that it should thinke it selfe beholding to them for their good directions. They seek no thanks, as if they vndertooke their labors voluntarily, only for the good of others; but proclaime a necessitie laide vpon them, for doing that which they doe, and a woe, if they doe it not. They spare not to rehearse the iniquitie, and shame of their progenitors
 and nearest kinsfolkes, with Gods fearefull iudgements vpon them for the same, to register their prince and peoples, or their owne disgrace (as the world counteth disgrace) to all posteritie: so Gods name may thereby be more glorified, and his church edified, Ierem. 9. verse 23, 24. Let not the wise man glorie in his wisedome, nor the strong man glorie in his strength: but let him that glorieth, glorie in that he vnderstandeth and knoweth the Lord. Ieremy himselfe reuealeth his owne slackenesse, in vndertaking his appointed charge, Iere. 20. verse 7, 8, 14, 15, 16, 17, 19: hee no where bewrayes any desire of praise, as if he had excelled all his equalls in wit: all that is good in him, or his people, hee giueth to God. Daniel, who did excell in the interpretations of dreames and prophecies, and had the estate of many kingdomes for many yeares to come reuealed vnto him; so as if he would haue chalenged the reuelation of his Countries returne from captiuitie, he could not haue bin disproued, yet ingeniously sheweth that he learned this out of the Prophecie of Ieremie, Dan. 9 verse 2. Although his measure of knowledge was exceeding great, yet he affects not the reputation of Knowing aboue that measure, which God had giuen him, Rom. 12.3.
3 This one qualitie (in them all) of not seeking their owne, nor their countries praise, but onely the praises of their God, and the profit of his church (if we consider it well) may sufficiently testifie, that they speake not vpon priuate motions, who were thus cleere from all suspition of priuat respects. Nor can we suspect, that they should thus conspire together vnto one end, from the will and purpose of man. For what man could limit others thoughts, or rule their wits which liued after him? Least of all can Chaunce be imagined the author of so many seuerall Writers constancie, in conspiring thus to one end in seuerall Ages. Let vs coniecture what causes we can, Saint Peter must resolue the doubt, 2. Peter 1.20, 21. All of them spake as they were moued by the holy Spirit, which was present one and the same to all? If they had not spoken as they were moued by the Spirit, but as if they had moued themselues, to finde out matter, or stretched their wits to inlarge inuention: then would the later sort especially haue catched at many by-narrations, and inserted many (...), little pertinent to that foundation which others had laied before them. But now wee see the
 continuall drift of their writings so seriously set vpon one and the selfe same end, as if they had all wrought by an others direction, who had cast the platforme of the edifice himselfe, not minded to finish his worke in any of the first worke-mens age: and yet will haue the later to beginne where the other left, without any alteration or trickes of their owne inuention.
4 All these properties of these sacred Writers, doe sufficiently witnesse their motiues to haue been Diuine, but more abundantly whilest we consider the vanitie of the Iewish people, if we take them as they are by nature, not sanctified by the Spirite of God. For naturally they are giuen to magnifie their owne nation, more than any other people liuing, yea to make God beholding vnto them for their sanctitie: few of them would seek the praise of their God, but with reference to their owne. Hence our apostle S. Paul brings it as an argume^t of the truth of his gospel, 2. Cor. 4.5. in that he did not preach himselfe but Christ Iesus the Lord, and himselfe their seruants for his sake, so doth our Sauiour, Iohn 7. verse 18. He that speaketh of himselfe seeketh his owne glory, but he that seeketh his glory that sent him, the same is true, and no vnrighteousnesse is in him. This sinceritie in teaching, (especially in a man of Iewish progenie) when it is tried to continue without all affectation or dissimulation, is the true (...) or touchstone, the liuerie or cognisance of a man speaking by the Spirit of God. The like liue characters of sinceritie are not to be found in any else, saue only in these sacred Writers, or such as haue sincerely obeyed their doctrine. And in many of those Books which our Church accounts Apocryphall, there euidently appeares a spice of secular vanitie; howsoeuer the pen-men of them were truely religious sanctified men, and haue sought to imitate the writings of the Prophets, and other Writers of this sacred volume; But much more eminent is the like vanitie in Iosephus, a man otherwise as excellent for meere naturall parts, or artificiall learning, as his Countrey yeelded anie, not inferior to any Historiographers whatsoeuer.
5 Seeing in this whole bodie of Scriptures there appeares one and the same Spirit, albeit the members be of diuerse fashion and qualitie: this sacred volume it selfe may serue as a liuely type or image of that Vnitie of the Spirite in the bond of peace, which ought to be in the Church and mysticall bodie of Christ, Ephesians 4. v. 3.
 They all indeuour to keepe the vnitie of the Spirite in the bond of peace: None of them presumed to vnderstand aboue that which was meete for them to vnderstand: all according to sobrietie, as God dealt to euery one of them the measure of faith. They are as many members of one body, which haue not one office, verse 4. And wee may see that verified in the Canon of the Old Testament, which Saint Paul attributes vnto the Church in Christ;1. Corint. 12.4. There are diuersities of giftes, but the same Spirite.Verse 14. And againe, To one was giuen the spirit of wisedome, as vnto Solomon; to another knowledge, as vnto Ezra, Nehemiah; to another faith, as vnto Moses, Abraham; to another prophecie,Verse 11. as vnto Esay, Ieremy: All these giftes were wrought by one and the same Spirite, which distributeth to euery one as hee would. The best meanes to discerne this harmony in their seuerall writings, would be, to retaine the vnitie of the Spirite by which they wrote. But alas wee haue made a diuision in the bodie of Christ, whilest one of vs detracts, enuies or slaunders another; or whiles we wrangle vnmanerly about idle questions, or termes of art or iarres, (ours that haue the name of Christs messengers) make all the world besides, and our selues oft-times (wee may feare) doubt of the true and reall vnitie betwixt Christ and his members, now eclipsed by our carnall diuisions. But howsoeuer, these here mentioned are in their kinde good motiues vnto sober minds; and the more diligent and attentiue men are to obserue these and the like, the more fully shall they be perswaded, that these writings are the dictates of the holy Ghost.


CHAP. III. Of the affections or dispositions of the sacred Writers.
WIth the experiment of this kinde wee may rancke the vehemencie of affection, which appeares in many of these sacred writers, most frequent in the booke of Psalmes: And to distinguish fained or counterfait from true experimentall affections is the most easie, and most certaine kind of Criticisme. He that neuer had any himselfe may safely sweare, that most Poets ancient or moderne,
 haue had experience of wanton loues. For who can think that Catullus, Ouid, and Martial had neuer beene acquainted with any but painted women, or written of loue matters onely as blinde men may talke of colours? Or, who can suspect, that either Ouid had penned his bookes de Tristibus, or Boetius his Philosophicall Consolation, onely to moue delight, (as children oft-times weepe for wantonnesse:) or fained these subiects to delude the world, by procuring reall compassion to their counterfait mourning? But much more sensible may wee feele the pulses of our Psalmists passions beating their ditties, if wee would lay our hearts vnto them. Albeit wee seeke not to prooue their Diuine authoritie from the strength of passion simply, but from the obiects, causes, or issue of their passions. And the argument holds thus; As the Ethnicke Poets passions, expressed in their writings, bewray their experience in such matters as they wrote of; as of their carnall delight in loue inioyed, or of earthly sorrow for their exiles, death of friends, or other like worldly crosses: So doe these sacred Ditties witnesse their pen-mens experience in such matters as they professe; as of spirituall ioy, comfort, sorrow, feare, confidence, or any other affection whatsoeuer. If we compare Ouids elegie to Augustus with that Psalme of Dauid,Trist. lib. 2. (in number the 51.) why should wee thinke that the one was more conscious of misdemeanour towards that Monarch, or more sensibly certaine of his displeasure procured by it; than the other of foule offences towards God, and his heauie hand vpon him for them? Dauids penitent bewayling of his soules losse, in being separated from her wonted ioyes, his humble intreatie and importunate suite for rest^uration to his former estate, argue hee had beene of more entire familiar acquaintance with his heauenly, than Ouid with his earthly Lord; that hee had receiued more sensible pledges of his loue, more deepely touched with the present losse of his fauour, and better experienced in the course and meanes of reconcilement to it againe. Haue mercie vpon me, O God,Psalme 51.^. according to thy louing kindnesse: according to the multitude of thy compassions put away mine iniquities. Wash me throughly from mine iniquitie, and cleanse me from my sinne. For I know mine iniquities,Verse 2. and my sinne is euer before me. Against thee, against thee only haue I sinned, and done euill in thy sight. What was it then which caused his present griefe?Verse 3. bodily paine? exile, losse of goods, want, or restraint of sensuall
 pleasures? Yea, what was there that worldly minded men either desire or know, which was not at his command? And yet, he, well for health of bodie, onely oppressed with griefe of mind, most desirous to sequester himselfe from all solace, which his Court or Kingdome could afford, in hope to finde his company alone who was inuisible, and to renew acquaintance with his Spirite. Create a cleane heart (O God) and renew a right spirite within me. Cast mee not away from thy presence, and take not thy holy Spirite from me. He accounts himselfe but as an exile, though liuing in his natiue soile, but as a slaue, though absolute Monarch ouer a mightie people; whilest hee stoode separate from the loue of his God, and liued not in subiection to his spirite. If one in hunger should loathe ordinarie or course fare, wee would coniecture hee had beene accustomed to more fine and daintie meates. Heereby then it may appeare, that Dauid had tasted of more choice delights, and purer ioyes, than the carnall minded knew, in that hee loathes all earthly comfort in this his anguish, (wherein he stood in greatest neede of some comfort,) desiring only this of God; Restore me to the ioy of thy saluation, and establish me with thy free Spirit. So farre was hee from distrusting the truth of that ineffable ioy, which now hee felt not (at the least) in such measure as hee had done before, that hee hopes by the manifest effects of it once restored, to disswade the Atheist from his Atheisme, and cause lasciuious or bloud-thirstie mindes to wash off the filth, wherein they wallow, with their teares. For so hee addeth; Then shall I teach thy wayes vnto the wicked, and sinners shall be conuerted vnto thee. Deliuer me from bloud, O GOD, which art the GOD of my saluation, and my tongue shall sing ioyfully of thy righteousnesse. Open thou my lippes, O Lord, and my mouth shall shew foorth thy praise; which as yet hee could not shew foorth to others, because aboundance of ioy did not lodge in his heart; for God had sealed vp sorrow therein, vntill the sacrifice of his broken and contrite heart were accomplished. From the like aboundant experience of this heauenly ioy, the Psalmist in the threescore and sixt Psalme and sixteenth verse, bursteth out into like confident speeches; inuiting vs, as Christ did his Apostle Thomas, to come neere and lay our hands vpon his healed sore, and by the scarres to gather the skill and goodnesse of him that had thus cured him beyond all expectation:
 Come and hearken all yee that feare God,Psalme 66.16. and I will tell you what hee hath done to my soule; I called vnto him with my mouth,Verse 17. and hee was exalted with my tongue.Verse 20. Praised bee GOD which hath not put backe my prayer, nor his mercy from mee. The fulnesse of his inward ioyes was such, and Gods prouidence ouer him so manifest and wonderfull, that the present age wherein hee liued could not (to his seeming) but take notice of it, whilest the particulars, wherein the Lord had heard him, were in fresh memorie: and all posteritie, he presumes out of the aboundance of his owne beliefe, should still beleeue the goodnesse of God, from this experimentall relation of his goodnesse towardes him. Hee that hath least experience of the like in himselfe, would hee but attentiuely marke the feruencie of those mens zeale, and vehemencie of their godly passions, expressed in these heere mentioned, and many like vnaffected straines, could not but acknowledge that famous inscription, which a later degenerate lasciuious Poet, (out of such a vaine glorious humour, as moues some basely descended to vsurpe the armes of Noble men whose names they beare,) sought to bestow on all, euen vpon such as himselfe was, Vates in name, but not in qualitie, to belong of right onely to these Psalmists or auncient sacred Poets.



Est Deus in vobis, agitante calescitis illo:


Impetus hic sacrae semina mentis habet.



Since in your breasts Gods Spirit hath his seate,
T'is Diuine motion breeds this heauenly heate.


For who can imagin, that the Author of the three score and fourteenth Psalme and the ninth verse should complaine, without some touch of that Spirite, which hee knew had beene more plentifull in such as had gone before him; Wee see not our signes, there is not one Prophet more, not any that knoweth how long. Verse 10. O GOD, how long shall the aduersary reproach? shall the enemie blaspheme thy name for euer? Verse 11. Why withdrawest thou thine hand, euen thy right hand? Drawe it out of thy bosome and consume them. Verse 12. Euen God is my King of olde, working saluation in the middes of the earth. These sober and constant motions, as it were of Systoles and Diastoles betweene Despaire and Hope, exprest
 in this and the foure and fortieth Psalme, argue that those woonders and noble workes, which they had heard with their eares, and their fathers had told them, were no fables, but matters truely and really acted, which had left deepe impression in their forefathers hearts, who had so thorowly felt and tasted the extraordinarie goodnesse of their God, that the longing desire of like fauour is transfused as hereditarie to posteritie, as the desire of such meates as parents best affect, and vse most to feede vpon, vsually remaines in their children.
2 Or, to vse the Author of the two and fortie Psalme his owne comparison; Braying doeth not more sensibly notifie the Hartes panting after the water brookes, than that Psalme doth his thirsting after the Spirite of Life; which sometime had beene diffused through his faculties, and had fructified in ioy and comfort, but now in these stormes of affliction lieth hid in his heart, onelie supporting it with hopes of like fruit against a better season; as the sap whereby trees flourish in Summer, retiring to the roote in Winter, preserueth them sound within, so that although frosts may nippe, and stormes outwardly deface them, yet they breake forth againe, and beare fruit in the Spring. And although I neuer mistrusted the truth of that dissension, betwixt the willingnesse of the Spirite, and weakenesse of the flesh, oft mentioned in Scripture; yet, I know not how, it addeth more life to my beliefe, whilest I see this conflict acted by the Author of the two and fortie and the three and fortie Psalme. The flesh complaines, as if his heart were readie to close with deiected feare; My soule is cast downe within mee, all thy waues and flouds are gone ouer mee: the Spirite like a good Physitian, by reiterating that speech of comfort, Why arte thou cast downe, O my soule, and why art thou so disquieted within mee, raiseth it vp againe, and dilateth his heart with hope in God against all hope in worldly sight. For so hee concludeth both these Psalmes, Wait on God: for I will yet giue him thankes: hee is my present helpe and my God. Generally, though the Psalmists complaints bee oft-times grieuous, yet they neuer end them but with hearty praier; though God oft-times lay great plagues vpon them; yet is their confidence alwayes as great that hee will heale them. The beginning of their mournefull Ditties,
 alwayes represent the stormes of griefe and sorrow, that had gone ouer their soules: their end and cloze is like the appearing of the Morning Starre, fore-shewing the remoueall of the shadow of death, wherein they sate.
