Their sodaine transitions from griefe to ioy, is euen as the breaking out of the Sunne from vnder a thicke tempestuous cloude: So that the outward Character of theyr Songs, is a liuely representation of that trueth, which one of them out of his inmost experience hath left registred to the world; His wrath endureth but the twinckeling of an eie, and in his pleasure is life: heauinesse may endure for a night, but ioy commeth in the morning.
3 This Patience in Aduersitie, and confident expectation of deliuerance from aboue, compared with the Heathens impatience, alwayes readie to accuse their Gods in their vnexpected calamities, and seeking to vent their griefe in Poeticall Inuectiues against them: infallibly testifie, that the one did onelie know the Diuine Powers by heare say, the others by experience; and that GOD was neare to this people in all which they called vppon him, and beheld the affaires of the Heathen onelie a farre off.
4 Yet beside these particular liuely Characters of experimentall ioy or griefe, feare or confidence: their consonancie with the historicall trueth of alterations in the state of Iewrie, will much illustrate the former obseruations. For albeit the Psalmists in their greatest distresse or calamities, murmure not against the Lord God, as the heathens doe: yet the tenour of some late mentioned, with diuerse other Psalmes, argue that the people of God in those times wherein they were written, either had not such manifest signes of Gods fauour; or else found not such speedie deliuerance from the daungers feared, or calamities suffered by them, as the Prophet Dauid in the seauen and twentie Psalme and the first verse, and other of their godlie Auncestours had done. The Lord (saieth Dauid) is my light and my saluation, whome shall I feare? The Lord is the strength of my life, of whome shall I bee affraide? Though an hoast pitched against mee, mine heart should not be afraide: though warre bee raysed against me, yet I will trust in thi^; to wit, vppon his former experience of
 Gods mercies specified in the second verse; When the wicked mine enemies came, and my foes came vpon me to eate vp my flesh, they stumbled and fell. But greater was his confidence from the more often experience of Gods fauour, when as his case otherwise, for the multitude and malignitie of his enemies, was more desperate, Psal. 3. vers. 1. Many were his aduersaries that rose vp against him; and many that said vnto his soule (when hee fled from his sonne Absolon) Verse 2. There is no helpe for him in his God. Yet hee, as an expert souldier, can not be terrified with stales or bragges, but betaketh himselfe vnto his weapons; Verse 3. Thou Lord art a buckler for mee, my glorie, and the lifter up of my head. So little is he dismaied, that after his prayers hee taketh his quiet rest; Verse 4. I did call vpon the Lord with my voyce, and hee heard mee out of his holy mountaine. I laide mee downe and slept, and rose againe, for the Lord sustained me. Verse 5. I will not bee afraide for tenne thousand of the people, that should beset me round about. The same confidence, raised from the experience of Gods assistance, was in the Author of the sixe and fortie Psalme; God is our helpe and strength, a very present helpe in trouble: Therefore will wee not feare though the earth be moued, and though the mountaines fall into the middest of the Sea. The manner of Dauids carriage, his confident presaging of good successe in times more apt to breede despaire in others lesse experienced in assistance from aboue, exprest in sundry Psalmes, composed when hee fled from Saul, yeeld aboundance of obseruations pregnant for this purpose.
5 Other-whiles this Kingly Prophet expostulates the wrongs offered by his enemies so confidently, and relateth his owne integritie in such patheticall and serious manner, that vnlesse the Inscription of his Petitions, or other historicall circumstance, did giue vs notice, to whome hee tendered his complaints, wee would thinke that they had beene so many reports, of what hee had openly pleaded at some Barre, or Court of ciuile Iustice, in the personall presence, sight, or audience of some visible Iudge, ready to giue sentence for him vpon the first hearing of his cause. If any ciuile Heathen, that neuer had heard of any inuisible God, should haue taken vp some of his Psalmes (the ninth for example) in the streetes, hee would haue imagined that the Author
 of them had either heard some supreame Magistrate in his time, deepely protesting his resolution for righting the poore, or else had beene most intimately acquainted with the integritie of his proceedings in matters of Iustice, that hee durst so confidently auouch vnto the world on his behalfe; Psalme 9. verse 8. He shall iudge the world in righteousnesse, and the people with equitie. Verse 9. The Lord also will bee a refuge for the poore, a refuge in due time, euen in affliction. Verse 10. And they that know thy Name, will trust in thee: for thou Lord hast not fayled them that seeke thee. So liuely was Dauids, and other ancient Psalmists experience of the Inuisible Gods assistance alwayes readie, aswell in warre as in peace, as well in executing iudgement vpon their treacherous, deceitfull, or secret enemies, as in giuing them victorie ouer their profest and potent foes.
6 But posteritie had not often times so full experience of the same assistance, as appeareth from the manner of their complaints. The reason of this Diuersitie, in the auncient and latter Psalmists apprehension of GODS fauour; eyther in deliuering them from daunger, or righting them from wrong; was from the Diuersitie of Times, the later not yeelding so manifest and frequent Documents of GODS mercie or iustice, as the former had done. As Gods plagues vppon the auncient Israelites were oft times sodaine, and (for the time) violent: so their deliuerance from them was speedie, because their stubbornenesse was lesse, and the sinnes, for which they were to repent, of lesse continuance. But the continuall increase of this peoples wickednesse, in their successions, and Posterities slackenesse, in sorrowing either for theyr owne or Predecessours sinnes, made Gods plagues inflicted vppon them more durable, as appeareth by the long captiuities and oppressions of this people in later Ages, if wee compare them with the often, but short afflictions, which in former times had befallen them. This long duraunce of great calamities, made Posteritie lesse apprehensiue of Gods promises than theyr Forefathers had beene; at the least whiles these continued, they were lesse acquainted with Gods fauour than their Predecessours were. And from the want of like sensible experience of his present helpe in the time of trouble, of later
 generations are more querulous and lesse confident in their prayers vttered in their distresse, as wee may see in the foure score and eighteenth and other Psalmes, conceiued by the godlie amongest this people in the calamities of later Times. Thus wee may see how truely the diuersitie of Gods dealing with his people in different Ages, is represented in the Character, Stile, or Affection of these sacred Writers, all much different in former and later Ages: much more may wee presume, that the generall and true Diuersitie of Times, and GODS diuers manner of proceeding with mankinde in their seuerall Generations, is most truely related and exemplified in the Historicall relations of the same sacred Volumes; of which in the Section following.
7 Thus much of Experiments, or Obseruations drawne from the Character, or tenor of these sacred Writings themselues, or their Writers affections represented in them. These I haue gathered, not that I can hope to perswade any man so much by reading them, as by occasioning him to obserue the like, whiles hee readeth these sacred Volumes. For euerie man that readeth them with attentiue Obseruation, may apprehend much more for the framing of true beliefe in his owne heart, than hee can expresse to others: yea, to seeke to make full resemblance of our inward beliefe, or such Experiments as confirme it, by outward discourse, were all one, as if a man out of the slight impression or transitorie representation of his owne face which hee had lately beheld in a Glasse, should seeke to describe it as fully and perfectly to an other mans apprehension, as if he had looked vpon it with him in the same Glasse.
8 As the representation of our bodily shape, is liuely and perfect whilest wee beholde it in a true and perfect Glasse; but the memoriall or phantasie of it when wee are gone thence, imperfect and dull: So is the apprehension of our owne, or Experiments of others beliefe, sensible and fresh, whilest we set our harts and mindes vnto this perfect Lawe of Libertie, the onely true Glasse of our Soules; but more hard to retaine in memorie, or to bee fully represented to an other by Discourse, than our bodily shape is by a bare description. And as in the Arte of Painting, generall rules may bee giuen for the right drawing of Pictures, yet
 he that will take any particular mans, must looke vppon the liue face it selfe, or vse the benefite of his Glasse: So in this case, there may bee good directions giuen, how men should drawe Experiments, or take Obseruations of this kinde, which being taken, can not bee fully imprinted in an other, by him that tooke them: but euerie man must haue continuall recourse vnto this Spirituall Glasse, which farre surpasseth all bodily Glasses in this; that in it wee may see, not onely the true shape and proportion of our Soules as they are, or of what fashion they should be; but it hath also an operatiue force of assimilating them vnto the patternes of godly and religious mens Soules represented herein, yea euen of transfourming them into the similitude of that Image wherein they were first created. The Idaea of Sanctitie and Righteousnesse contained in this Spirituall Glasse, are the causes of our edification in good life and vertue: as the Idaea or platforme in the Artificers head, is the cause of the materiall house that is builded by it.



SECT. 2.
Of Experiments and Obseruations answerable to the rules of Scriptures.

CHAP. I. Containing the topicke, whence such Obseruations must be drawne.
1 IF the Bookes of some auncient rare Author, who had written in sundry Arts, should be found in this Age; all bearing the Authors name and other commendable Titles prefixed: a reasona- man would soone be perswaded, that they were his whose name they bore; but sooner, if hee had any positiue arguments to perswade himselfe of their Antiquitie, or if they were
 commended to him by the authority or report of men in this case credible. But, besides all these, if euery man according to his experience or skill in those arts and faculties which this ancient writer handles, should vpon due examination of his conclusions or discourse finde resolution in such points as he had alwayes wauered in before; or be instructed in matters of his profession or obseruation, whereof he was formerly ignorant: this would much strengthen his assent vnto the former reports or traditions concerning their Author, or vnto the due praises and titles prefixed to his workes; albeit he that made this triall, could not proue the same truth so fully to another, nor cause him to beleeue it so firmly as he himselfe doth, vnlesse he could induce him to examine his writings by like experiments, in some facultie wherein the examiner had some, though lesse, skill. And yet, after the like triall made, he, that had formerly doubted, would beleeue these workes to be the supposed Authors, and subscribe vnto the titles and commendations prefixed; not so much for the formers report or authoritie, as from his owne experience. Now we haue more certaine experiments to proue that the Scriptures are the word of God, than we can haue to proue any mens workes to be their supposed Authors: for one Author in any Age may be as good as an other; he perhaps better, of whome we haue heard lesse. We could in the former case onely certainely beleeue that the Author, whosoeuer, was an excellent Scholler; but wee could not bee so certaine that it was none other but hee whose name it did beare: for there may be many Aristotles, and many Platoes, many excellent men in euery profession, yet but one God that is all in all; whose workes we suppose the Scriptures are, which vpon strict examination will euince him alone to haue beene their Author.
2 The meanes then of establishing our assent vnto any part of Scripture, must be from experiments and obseruations agreeable to the rules in Scripture. For when we see the reason and maner of sundry euents, either related by others, or experienced in our selues, which otherwise we could neuer haue reached vnto by any naturall skill; or, generally, when we see any effects or concurrence of things which cannot be ascribed to any but a supernaturall cause, and yet they fully agreeing to the Oracles of Scriptures
 or Articles of Beleefe: This is a sure pledge vnto vs, that he who is the Author of Truth, and giues being vnto all things, was the author of Scriptures.
3 Such euents and experiments are diuerse, and according to their diuersities may worke more or lesse on diuerse dispositions: some may finde more of one sort, some of another, none all; some againe may be more induced to beleeue the truth of Scriptures from one sort of experiments, some from others. Those obseruations are alwaies best for euery man, which are most incident to his vocation. With some varietie of these obseruations or experiments we are in the next place to acquaint diuerse Readers.


CHAP. II. That Heathenish fables ought not to preiudice diuine Truth.
1 NOthing more vsuall to men, wise ynough in their generation, than for the varietie or multitude of false reports concerning any subiect, to discredit all that are extant of the same. And all inclination vnto diffidence or distrust is not alwayes to be misliked; but onely when it swayes too farre, or extends it selfe beyond the limits of it proper circumference, that is, matters of bargaine or secular commerce. As this diffident temper is most common in the cunning managers of such affaires: so the first degree or propension to it were not much amisse in them, did they not transcendere a genere ad genus; that is, were not their mistrust commonly too generally rigid and stiffe. For most men of great dealings in the world, finding many slipperie companions, hold it no sinne to be at the least suspitious of all: others, being often cosened by such as haue had the name and reputation of honest men, begin to doubt whether there be any such thing indeed, as that which men call honestie; and from this doubting about the reall nature of honestie in the abstract, they resolue vndoubtedly, That if any man in these dayes doe not deale ill with others, it is onely for want of fit oportunitie to doe himselfe any great good. But as facilitie in yeelding assent, vnlesse it be moderated by discretion,
 is an infallible consequent of too great simplicitie, and layes a man open to abuse and wrong in matters of this life: so generall mistrust is the certaine fore-runner of infidelitie, and makes a man apt ynough to cousin himselfe, without a tempter, in matters of the life to come; though otherwise this is the very disposition which the great Tempter workes most vpon: who for this reason, when any notable truth of greater moment falls out, labours by all meanes to fill the world with reports of like euents, but such as vpon examination he foresees will proue false: for hee knowes well, that the beleefe of most pregnant truthes may by this meanes be much impaired, as honest men are vsually mistrusted when the world is full of knaues. And, to speake the truth, it is but a very short cut betwixt generall and rigid mistrust in worldly dealings, and infidelitie in spirituall matters; which indeed is but a kind of diffidence or mistrust: and he that from the experience of often cousenage comes once to this point, That he will trust none in worldly affaires but vpon strong securitie or legall assurance, may easily be transported by the varietie or multitude of reports, in spirituall matters, notoriously false, to beleeue nothing but vpon the sure pledge and euidence of his owne sense or naturall reason. This is one maine fountaine of Atheisme; of which (God willing) in the Article of the Godhead. In this place I onely desire to giue the Reader notice of Sathans policie, and to aduertise him withall, that as there is a kind of ingenuous simplicitie, which if it maketh with sobrietie and serious meditation, doth prepare our hearts to Christian beleefe: so there is a kind of suspition by which we may out-reach the old Serpent in his subtiltie, and preuent his former method of deceit. So, whilest wee read or heare varietie of reports concerning any notable euent, or many Writers beating about one matter, euery one of which may seeme vnprobable in particular circumstances, or else their diuersitie such as makes them incompatible; we should be iealous, that there were some notable truth, whose beleefe did concerne vs, which Sathan hath sought to disparage by the mixture, either of grosse improbable fruitlesse fables, or else of dissonant probabilities.
2 Truth is the life and nutriment of the world, and the Scriptures as the Veines or Vessels wherein it is contained; which
 straight corrupts and putrifies, vnlesse it be preserued in them, as in it proper receptacles, as both the fabulous conceits of the Heathen and foolish practises of the Romish Church in many points may witnesse. But as from Asphaltites, or the dead sea, we may finde out the pleasant streames and fresh springs of Iordan: so from the degenerate and corrupted rellish of decayed truth, which is frequent in the puddle and standing lakes of Heathen Writers, we may be led into the pure fountaine of truth contained in these sacred volumes of Scripture.
3 The experiments, which now wee seeke or would occasion others (chiefely young students) to obserue, are such as the Heathen did guesse at, or men out of the workes of nature, by reading of Poets, or auncient Writers, may yet doubt of; whereas the true resolution of them onely depends vpon the truth set downe in Scripture.


CHAP. III. Obseruations out of Poets in generall, and of Dreames in particular.
1 THe most exquisite Poemes are but a kind of pleasant waking dreame, and the art of Poetrie a liuely imitation of some delightfull visions. And as nothing comes into a mans fancie by night in dreames, but the parts or matters of it haue bin formerly in his outward senses (for euen when we dreame of golden Mountaines or Chymeraes, the seuerall ingredients haue a reall and sensible truth in them; onely the frame or proportion is such, as hath no sensible example in the workes of nature:) so in auncient Poemes, which were not made in imitation of former, as pictures drawne from pictures, but immediately deuised (as wee now suppose) from the sensible experiments of those times (as pictures drawne from a liuing face) many parts and limmes haue a reall and sensible truth, onely the composition or frame is artificiall and fained, such as cannot perhaps be paralelled in euery circumstance, with any reall euents in the course of times. And albeit
 the euents (which the most auncient Poets relate) through long distance of time, seeme most strange to vs, yet is the ground of their deuises (especially) such, as vpon better search may alwaies be referred to some Historicall truth, which yeelded stuffe to Poeticall structure, as dayes spectacles doe vnto nights visions. This Aristotle had obserued out of the practise of the best auncient Poets, and prescribes it as a rule to Poets, to haue alwayes an Historicall truth for their ground. Nor durst the Poets haue been so audacious in their fictions at the first, seeing their profession was but either to imitate nature, or adorne a knowne truth; not to disparage any truth by prodigious or monstrous fictions, without any ground of like experience. For this is a fundamentall law of their Art;
Hieronym. Vida, ^b. 2. Poet.Curandum, vt quando non semper vera profamur,Fingentes: saltem sint illa simillima veri.Though all's not true that faining Poets sing,Yet nought on Stage but in truths likenesse bring.
None, I thinke, will be so foolish as to take Homer in the literall sence, when he tels vs how Iris by day, and Sleepe by night, runne errands for the greater Gods, and come with these and the like messages vnto Kings chambers:
(...),Who will by counsell guide a State,Must early rise, and lye downe late.
Yet with such artificiall and painted plumes oftentimes are couered true and naturall bodies, though the messengers be poeticall and fained; yet these kinds of night-messages had an Historicall truth: for not the Poets onely, but many great Philosophers of the old world, haue taken nocturnall presages for no dreames or fancies. Hence did Homer vsurpe his libertie, in faining his Kings and Heroickes, so often admonished of their future estate by the gods: he presumed at least, that these fictions might carrie a shew of truth in that age, wherein such admonitions by night were not vnusuall. And his conceit is not dissonant vnto the sacred
 storie, which beares record of like effects in auncient times, and giues the true cause of their expiration in later.
2 So vsuall were dreames amongst the Patriarchs, and their interpretations so well knowne, that Iacob could at the first hearing interprete his young sonne Iosephs dreame, Gen. 37. vers. 10,Gen. 37.10. 11. What is this dreame that thou hast dreamed? Shall I, and thy mother and thy brethren come indeed, and fall on the ground before thee? Nor did he take it only for a fable, no more than his brethren had done his former for a fancie; for, as the Text sayth,Vers. 11. his brethren enuied him, but his father noted the saying. And Ioseph himselfe comming to riper yeares, was as expert in interpreting Pharaohs and his seruants dreames, Gen. 40. vers. 12, 13. Then Ioseph said vnto him, this is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three dayes; within three dayes shall Pharaoh lift vp thine head, and restore thee vnto thine office, and thou shalt giue Pharaohs cup into his hand, after the old manner when thou wast his Butler. And vers. 19. Within three dayes shall Pharaoh take thine head from thee, and shall hang thee on a tree, and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee. These considerations will not suffer me mistrust diuerse auncient Historiographers, making report, how Princes and Fathers of Families haue had fore-warnings of future euents, either concerning themselues, their Kingdome, or Posteritie. Nor were all dreames among the Heathens illusions of wicked spirits:Iob. 33. for Elihu spake out of the common experience of those auncient times wherein hee liued; God speaketh once or twice (that is,Vers. 14. Vers. 15. vsually) and one seeth it not. In dreames and visions of the night, when sleepe falleth vpon men and they sleepe vpon their beds, then he openeth the eares of men,Vers. 16. Vers. 17. euen by their corrections, which he had sealed, that he might cause man to turne away from his enterprise, and that he might hide the pride of man, and keepe backe his soule from the pit, and that his life should not passe by the sword. A liuely experiment of Elihu his obseruation wee haue Gen. 20.3. When Abimelech King of Gerar had taken Sarah, Abrahams wife, God came to him in a dreame by night, and said to him, Behold, thou art but dead, because of the woman which thou hast taken; for she is a mans wife. And againe, vers. 6, 7. God said vnto him by a dreame, I know that thou didst this euen with an vpright minde, and I kept thee also that thou shouldest not sinne against me, therefore suffered I not thee to touch her: Now then deliuer the man his wife
 againe; for he is a Prophet, and he shall pray for thee, that thou mayest liue: but if thou deliuer her not againe, be sure that thou shalt die the death, thou, and all that thou hast. And Moses witnesseth the ordinarie prophecie of auncient times to haue consisted of dreames and visions, Numb. 12.6, 7. If there be a Prophet of the Lord amongst you, I will be knowne vnto him by a vision, and will speake vnto him by a dreame. My seruant Moses is not so (that is, he is no ordinarie Prophet) vnto him will I speake mouth to mouth, and by vision, and not in darke words, but he shall see the similitude of the Lord.
3 These allegations sufficiently proue, that night-dreames and visions were frequent, and their obseruation (if taken in sobrietie) to good vse, in auncient times, euen amongst the nations, vntill they forgot,Gen. 40.8. as Ioseph said, That interpretations were from God, and sought to find out an art of interpreting them: Then night-visions did either cease, or were so mixt with delusions, that they could not be discerned; or, if their euents were in some sort foreseene, yet men being ignorant of Gods prouidence, commonly made choise of such meanes for their auoidance, as proued the necessarie occasions or prouocations of the euents they feared.
4 Much better was the temper of the Nations before Homers time: They, amongst other kindes of Prophecyings and Southsayings, held dreames and their interpretations (as all other good gifts) to be from God; as no euill was done in the Grecian campe, which the gods, in their opinion, did not cause. So Homer brings in Achilles, aduising Agamemnon to consult their gods interpreters with all speed, for what offence committed against them they had sent the pestilence into their Campe.


Hom. Iliad. 1.(...)
(...).


But to what Priest, or Prophet shall we wend,
Or dreamer? for euen dreames from Ioue descend.


All those kindes of predictions had beene in vse amongst the Heathens, as they were amongst the Israelites; albeit in later times they grew rare in both: for the encrease of wickednesse throughout the world, the multiplicitie of businesse and solicitude of humane affaires, and mens too much minding of politike meanes,
 and other second causes of their owne good, did cause the defect of true dreames and other diuine admonitions for the welfare of mankind.
5 This cause the Scriptures giue vs, 1. Sam. 28.6. After Saul (who had followed the fashions of other Nations, not the prescripts of Gods word) asked counsell of the Lord, and the Lord answered him not, neither by dreames, nor byi. by Priests. Vrim, nor by the Prophets, his sinnes had made a separation betweene him and the god of Israel: who for this cause will not affoord his presence to his Priests or Prophets, that came as mediators betwixt Saul and him; much lesse would hee vouchsafe his spirit vnto such Priests or Prophets as were carnally minded themselues. This was a rule so well knowne to the people of God, that Strabo from the tradition of it (for Moses his storie he had not read) reckons vp this as a speciall point of Moses his doctrine, concerning the worship of the god of Israel; his words are to this effect: Moses taught,Strabo, lib. 16. in the description of Iurie. that such as liued chastly and vprightly should be inspired with true visions by night, and such men, it was meete, should consult the Diuine Powers in the Temple by night-visions: but others, who were not so well minded, ought not to intrude themselues into this sacred businesse; or, if they would, they were to expect no true visions, but illusions or idle dreames; from God they were not to expect any. Yet may it not be denied, but that the Heathens were oft times, by Gods permission, truly resolued by Dreames or Oracles (though ministred by diuels) of euents that should come; but seldome were such resolutions for their good: So the Witch which Saul most Heathen-like consulted, when God had cast him off, did procure him a true prediction of his fearefull end. This is a point wherein I could be large; but I will conclude. As the Heathens relations of sundrie euents vsuall in auncient times, confirme the truth of the like, recorded in Scripture; so the Scriptures giue the true causes of their being, ceasing, or alteration: which the corrupt and polypragmaticall disposition of later ages, without reuelation from the cause of causes and disposer of times, could neuer haue dreamed of; as may partly appeare from what hath beene said of Dreames, more fully from that which followes next of Oracles.





CHAP. IIII. Of Oracles.
I Haue often and daily occasion (for the satisfaction of my mind in sundrie questions that might otherwise haue vext me) to thanke my God, that as he made me a reasonable creature, and of a reasonable creature a student or contemplator, so he did not make me a meere Philosopher: (though Plato thought this deserued the greatest thankes, as being the greatest benefit bestowed vpon him by his God) but neuer was I more incited in this respect to blesse the day wherein I was made a Christian, than when I read Plutarchs Tract of the causes why Oracles ceased in his time. Whether Heathen Oracles were all illusions of diuels, or some vttered by God himselfe for their good (though oft times without successe, by reason of their curiositie and superstition) I now dispute not. That Oracles in auncient times had beene frequent; that such euents had been foretold by them, as surpassed the skill of humane reason: all records of vnpartiall antiquitie beare vncontrollable euidence. Nor did the Heathen Philosophers themselues, which liued in the ages immediately following their decay, call the truth of their former vse in question: but from admiration of this knowne change, they were incited to search the cause of their ceasing.Lib. de Defectu oraculorum. Plutarch, after his acute search of sundrie causes and accurate Philosophicall disputes, referres it partly vnto the absence of his daemoniacall spirits, which by his Philosophie might dye or flit from place to place, either exiled by others more potent, or vpon some other dislike; and partly vnto the alteration of the soyle, wherein Oracles were seated, which yeelded not exhalations of such a diuine temper as in former times it had done; and without a certaine temperature of exhalations or breathing of the earth, the daemoniacall spirits (hee thought) could not giue their Oracles, more than a Musitian can play without an Instrument. And this decay or alteration of the soyle of Delphi, and like places, was (in his iudgement) probable, from the like knowne experience in sundry Riuers, Lakes, and hot-Baths; which, in some places, did quite
 drie vp and vanish; in others, much decay for a long time, or change their course; and yet afterwards recouer their former course or strength, either in the same places, or some neere adioyning. Thus he expected Oracles should either come in vse againe in Greece, or else burst out in some more conuenient soyle. The Atheists of this age (our English home-bred ones at least) haue altogether as great reason to denie the decay or drying vp of Riuers and Lakes, as to suspect the frequencie of Oracles, or other euents in times past: for neither they nor their fathers haue had any more experience of the one than of the other. Plutarchs testimonie (amongst many others) is authentike for the vse and decay of Oracles: but neither his authoritie, nor the reasons which he brings, can giue satisfaction to any man that seekes the true cause of their defect. He referres it (indeed) in a generalitie to the gods; not that they wanted good will to mankind still, but that the matter did decay which their ministers (the daemoniacall spirits) did worke vpon, as you heard before. Wee may vpon sure grounds with confidence affirme, That euen this decay of matter, which he dreames of (had it conferred ought to the vse of Oracles) was from God. And he (as the Psalmist speakes) that turneth the flouds into a Wildernesse, and dryeth vp the water Springs, and maketh a fruitfull land barren, for the iniquitie of them that dwell therein, did also bring, not onely the Oracle of Delphi, so much frequented amongst the Grecians, but all other kinds of diuinations, vsed amongst his owne people in the old world, to desolation: and by pouring out his spirit more plenteously vpon the barren hearts of vs Heathen, hath filled the barbarous nations of Europe with better store of riuers of comfort than the ancient Israel, his owne inheritance, had euer knowne. Or if wee desire a more immediate cause of these Oracles defect amongst the Heathens; the time was come,Matth. 12.29 that the strong mans house was to be entred, his goods spoyled, and himselfe bound;Iob. 12.31. now the Prince of this world was to be cast out.
2 Plutarchs relation of his daemoniacall spirits mourning for great Pans death, about this time, is so strange, that it might perhaps seeme a tale, vnlesse the truth of the common bruite had beene so constantly auouched by eare-witnesses vnto Tiberius,
 that it made him call a conuocation of Wisemen, as Herod did at our Sauiours birth, to resolue him who this great Pan, late deceased, should be. Thamus, the Aegyptian Master, vnknowne by that name to his passengers, vntill he answered to it at the third call of an vncouth voice, vttered (sine authore) from the land, requesting him to proclaime the newes of great Pans death, as he passed by Palodes, was resolued to haue let all passe as a fansie or idle message, if the winde and tide should graunt him passage by the place appointed: but, the wind fayling him on a suddaine, at his comming thither, he thought it but a little losse of breath to crie out aloud vnto the shoare, as hee had beene requested, Great Pan is dead. The words, as Plutarch relates, were scarce out of his mouth, before they were answered with a huge noyse, as it had beene of a multitude, sighing and groaning at this wonderment. If these spirits had beene by nature mortall, as this Philosopher thinkes, the death of their chiefe Captaine could not haue seemed so strange: but that a farre greater than the greatest of them, by whose power the first of them had his being, should die to redeeme his enemies from their thraldome, might well feeme a matter of wonderment and sorrow vnto them. The circumstance of the time will not permit me to doubt, but that vnder the knowne name of Pan was intimated the great Shepheard of our soules, that had then layd downe his life for his flocke; not the fained sonne of Mercurie and Penelope,(...). Plut. vt supra. as the Wisemen foolishly resolued Tiberius: Albeit euen this base and counterfeit resolution of these Heathens coyning, beares a liuely image (for the exact proportion) of the diuine truth, charactred out vnto vs in Scripture. For it shall appeare by sufficient testimonies, in their due time and place to be produced, that sundry generall, confused, or aenigmaticall tradiditions of our Sauiours conception, birth, and Pastorall office, had beene spread abroad amongst the Nations. Hence, in stead of him, they frame a Pan, the God of Shepheards; in stead of the holy Spirit, by whom he was to be conceiued, they haue a Mercurie (their false God, fained Messenger and Interpreter) for Pans father; in stead of the blessed Virgine, who was to beare our Sauiour, they haue a Penelope for their young Gods mother.
 The affinitie of qualitie and offices, in all the parties here paralelled, made this transfiguration of diuine Truth easie vnto the Heathen; and the manner of it cannot seeme improbable to vs, if we consider the wonted vanitie of their imaginations, in transforming the glorie of the immortall Godhead into the similitude of earthly thinges, most dislike to it in nature and qualitie. Thus admitting Plutarchs storie to be most true, it no way proues his intended conclusion, that the wilde goatish Pan was mortall: but the Scriptures set foorth vnto vs the true cause, why both he, and all the rest of that hellish crue should at that time howle and mourne, seeing by the great Shepheards death they were become dead in law; no more to breath in Oracles, but quite to be depriued of all such strange motions, as they had seduced the ignorant world with before. All the anticke trickes of Faunus, the Satyres, and such like creatures, were now put downe; God had resolued to make a translation of his Church; and for this cause the Diuels were enforced to dissolue their old Chappels, and seeke a new forme of their Liturgie or Seruice. Whilest the Israelites were commaunded to consult with Gods Priests, Prophets, or other Oracles, before they vndertooke any difficult warre or matters of moment, Sathan had his Priests and Oracles as much frequented by Heathen Princes vpon the like occasions. So Strabo witnesseth,Lib. citato. That the auncient Heathen, in their chiefe consultations of State, did relye more vpon Oracles than humane policie. If Moses were fortie dayes in the Mount, to receiue Lawes from Gods owne mouth, Minos will be Iupiters Auditor,Plato in Min^e. in his denne or caue, for the same purpose. In emulation of Shiloh, or Kireath-jearim, whilest the Arke of God remained there; the Heathens had Dodona; and for Ierusalem they had Delphi, garnished with rich donatiues of forraine Princes as well as Grecians; so magnified also by Grecian Writers, as if it had beene the intended paralell of the holy Citie. Insomuch,Plut. ibid. that Plutarch thinkes the storie, commonly receiued of that Oracles originall, to be lesse probable, because it ascribes the inuention of it to chaunce, and not to the diuine prouidence, or fauour of the Gods: when as it had beene such a direction vnto Greece, in vndertaking
 warres, in building Cities, and in time of pestilence and famine. Whether these effects in auncient times had beene alwayes from the information of Diuels (as I said before) I will not dispute. That this Oracle had beene often consulted, it is euident; and that oftentimes the Diuels deluded such as consulted them, is as manifest. But since that saying of the Prophet was fulfilled,Ier. 31.33. I will put my Law in their inward parts, and write it in their hearts; since the knowledge of Truth hath beene so plenteously made knowne and reuealed, and the principles of Religion so much dilated and enlarged by discourse, the Diuell hath chosen prowd hearts and busie braines for his Oracles; seeking by their subtiltie of wit, and plausibilitie of discourse, to counterfeit and corrupt the forme of wholesome Doctrine; as he did of old, the truth of Gods visible Oracles, by his apish imitations.
3 This conclusion then is euident, both from the ioynt authoritie of all auncient Writers, as well prophane, as sacred; That God in former times had spoken vnto the world by Dreames, Visions, Oracles, Priests, and Prophets; and that such reuelations had beene, amongst the Israelites, as the Starres or Night-lampes; amongst the Heathen as Meteors, fierie Apparitions, or wandering Comets, for their direction in the time of darkenesse and ignorance. But when both the sensible experience of our times, and the relations of former ages, most vnpartiall in this case, haue sufficiently declared vnto vs, That all the former twinckling lights are vanished; the reason of this alteration, I see, men might seeke by naturall causes, as Plutarch did. But this doubt is cleared, and the question truely resolued by our Apostle in these wordes:Heb. 1.1. At sundrie times, and in diuerse manners, God spake in the old time to our Fathers by the Prophets; in these last dayes he hath spoken vnto vs by his Sonne, whom he hath made heire of all thinges, by whom also he made the world: who, being (as the Apostle there sayth) the brightnesse of his glorie, hath put the former lights, which shined in darkenesse, to flight. The consideration hereof confirmes that truth of our Apostle to all such as are not blinded in heart, where he sayth, That the night was past, and the day was come: For the suddaine
 vanishing of all former lights, about this time assigned by Christians of our Sauiours birth, abundantly euince, That this was the Sunne of Righteousnesse which (as the Prophet had foretold) should arise vnto the world.Malach. 4.2. It was the Light which had lately appeared in the Coasts of Iurie, then approaching Italie, Greece, and other of these Westerne Countries, which did cause these sonnes of darkenesse (the daemoniacall Spirits) to flit Westward, as Darkenesse it selfe doth from the face of the Sunne, when it beginnes to appeare in the East. And Plutarch tells vs,Plut. ibid. That after they had forsaken the Countrey of Greece, they haunted little desart Islands, neere adjoyning to the coasts of this our Britanie; where they raysed such hideous stormes and tempests, as Nauigators report they haue done of late in that Island, called by their owne name. Both reports had their times of truth; and the like may be yet true, in some places, more remote from commerce of Christians. But the Heathen (as Heathenish minded men doe euen to this day) sought the reasons of such alterations from sensible Agents, or second Causes, which haue small affinitie with those effects: or if they had, yet the disposition of such causes depends wholly vpon his will; who, though most immutable in himselfe, changeth times and seasons at his pleasure. And wheresoeuer the light of his Gospell commeth, it verifieth that saying of our Apostle, Ecce vetera transierunt, and noua facta sunt omnia: And new times yeeld new obseruations; which cannot be taken aright, nor their causes knowne, without especiall directions from this rule of Life. By which it plainely appeareth, that the second maine period of the World, since the Floud (whose beginning wee account from the promulgation of the Law, and the distinction of the Israelites from other people, vntill the time of Grace) yeeldes great alteration and matter of much different obseruation from the former. And in the declining, or later part of this second age, wee haue described vnto vs, as it were, an ebbe or staunch in the affaires of the Kingdome of Israel, going before the generall fullnesse of time: After which wee see the tenure of all thinges in Iurie, and of other Kingdomes of the world, quite changed. But the particulars of this change I intend to handle
 hereafter: I now would prosecute my former obseruations of the old world.
4 Continually, whilest we compare ancient Poets or stories with the booke of Genesis, and other volumes of sacred Antiquitie: these sacred bookes giue vs the patterne of the waking thoughts of ancient times; and the Heathen Poems, with other fragments of Ethnike writings (whose entire bodies, though not so aged as the former, being but the workes of men, haue perished) containe the dreames and fancies which succeeding ages by heare-say, and broken reports, had conceiued, concerning the same or like matters: So no doubt had God disposed, that the delight which men tooke in the vncertaine glimpse of truth in the one, should enure their mindes the better to obserue the light which shineth in the other; and that the vnstable varietie of the one should prepare mens hearts more stedfastly to imbrace the truth and stabilitie of the other, when it should be reuealed vnto them. And, as any man almost, if hee bee obseruant of his former actions, cogitations, and occurrents, may finde out the occasion how dreames (though in themselues oftentimes prodigious, absurd, and foolish) come into his braine or fancie: so may any iudicious man, from the continuall and serious obseruation of this Register of truth, finde out the originall, at least, of all the principall heads or common places of Poeticall fictions or ancient Traditions, which cannot be imagined they should euer haue come into any mans fancie, vnlesse from the imitation of some Historicall truth, or the impulsion of reall euents stirring vp admiration. For admiration (as shall afterward appeare) did breede, and imitation spoile, the diuine art of Poetrie.



CHAP. V. Of the apparitions of the Heathen Gods and their Heroicks.
1 WEre all the workes of auncient Poets vtterly lost, and no tradition or print of their inuentions left, so as the art of Poetrie were to begin anew, and the Theater to bee raysed from the ground; the most curious wits in this or neere adioyning Countries might for many generations to come beat their braines, and sift their fancies, vntill they had runne ouer all the formes and compositions which the whole Alphabet of their fantasmes could affoord, before they could euer dreame of bringing the gods in visible shape vpon the Stage, or interlacing their Poemes with their often apparitions. And vnlesse ensuing times should yeeld matter of much different obseruations from that which these present doe, this inuention would be accounted dull, and finde but sorrie and vnwelcome entertainement of the auditors or spectators. That the like inuention finds some acceptation now, it is, because mens mindes haue beene possessed with this conceit from the tradition of their forefathers. For many inuentions, which in succession cease to be of like vse and consequence as they were in former times, become yet matters of delight and sport vnto posteritie: as Shooting continues still an Exercise of good recreation to vs of this Land, because it hath beene a practise of admirable vse and consequence vnto our worthie auncestors. But whence came this conceit of the gods appearing in sensible shapes, into Homers and other auncient Poets heads? How became it a common place of Poeticall inuention, whilest Poetrie it selfe was but beginning? Surely, as God had spoken in diuerse manners vnto the old world, so had he appeared in diuerse formes (perhaps) not onely to the Israelites, but vnto other nations also before the distinction of this people from them: howsoeuer, as the diuels had counterfeited Gods manner of speaking to his people, so did they the manner of his, or his Angels apparitions.
2. Such apparitions of God or his Angels, the sacred Storie
 tels vs, were frequent, not onely in Abrahams, Isaacks, Iacobs, and the Patriarches times, but in the ages immediatly going before the times that Homer wrote of: so that the traditions of these vndoubted experiments (if Greece or Asia had not the like in Homers time) might then bee fresh and vnquestionable.Exod. 3.2. Numb. 22.22. So God appeared to Moses, in the bush; his Angell to Balaam, to Gedeon, to Manoah and his wife.Iudg. 6.11. Iudg. 13.3. The like apparitions in times following were more rare in Israel; not that the date of Gods, or his Angels extraordinarie presence, was vtterly expired: but their presence was seldome apprehended, by reason of that peoples blindnesse of heart, and want of Prophets eyes. For Elishaes seruant had not seene so much as a glimpse of any Angell, albeit a mightie host of these heauenly souldiers had pitched their tents about him, vnlesse his master, by his prayer to God, had opened his eyes. His master and hee may be a perfect embleme of the heauenly and worldly wise. The seruant did see the host of the Assyrians as clearely, perhaps more clearely, at the least hee descryed it sooner than his master did: And when the seruant of the man of God arose earely to goe out,2. King. 6.15. and sequent. behold, an Hoste compassed the Citie with horses and chariots. Then his seruant said vnto him, Alas master, how shall wee doe? And hee answered, Feare not: for they that bee with vs, are moe than they that bee with them. Then Elishah prayed, and said, Lord open his eyes that hee may see. And the Lord opened the eyes of the seruant, and hee looked, and behold, the mountaine was full of horses and chariots round about Elishah. This place, and that other of the Angels appearing to Balaam, may instruct vs that such apparitions might bee conspicuous to some one or few whom they concerned, though not to others present with them: and that the eyes of some, which were open enough to worldly spectacles, might be close shut to these celestiall visions, as the eares of others haue beene in like case.Act. 22.9. For S. Paul onely heard the voice which cryed alowd vnto him: though those that were with him did see the light that shone at his conuersion, and were astonished at it. From the like experience about Greece or Asia, in his owne, or the vsuall tradition of the like in former times, did Homer bring in Pallas appearing thus to Achilles:
(...):Iliad. ^.Of all the rest not one but heeThe Goddesse did, though present, see.
3 As the end and purpose which Homer assignes for these apparitions of his gods; so are both these, and many other particular circumstances of his gods assisting the ancient heroickes, such as might iustly breed offence to any serious reader, if a man should auouch them in earnest, or seeke to perswade him to expect more than meere delight in them. Yet I can not thinke, that he would haue fained such an assistance, vnlesse the valour of some men, in former times, had beene extraordinarie, and more than naturall. Which supernaturall excellencie in some, before others, could not proceede but from a supernaturall cause. And thus farre his conceit agrees with Scripture: that there were more heroicall spirits in olde times, than in later, and more immediate directions from God, for managing of most warres. And from the experience hereof, the auncient Poets are more copious in their hyperbolicall praises of their Worthies, than the discreeter sort of later Poets durst be, whilest they wrote of their owne times. Not that the auncient were more licentious, or lesse obseruant of decorum in this kinde of fiction, than the other: but because the manifestation of a Diuine power in many of their victories, was more seen in ancient, than in later times: so that such fictions, as to the ancient people might seem, by reason of these extraordinarie euents, then frequent, very probable; would haue beene censured as ridiculous and apish in succeeding Ages, wherein no like euents were manifested. The like extraordinarie manifestation of Gods power in battell, and of this heroicall valour inspired into men, we see most frequent in the auncient Stories of the Bible, as in the bookes of Moses, Iosuah, Iudges, Samuel: some one man in those times, was worth a thousand others: but in the Histories of the later Kings of Iudah and Israel, such extraordinarie heroical spirits are very rare. One or two miraculous victories Israel had in Elishahs, and Iudah in Ezekiahs time: but by the power, of Angells: no extraordinarie valour of anie man was much seene in them. And, these few excepted, their battels, their victories, and maner of fight, come neere vnto the nature of other nations in the same time. Generally from Rehoboams time, the Histories of Iudah and
 Israel fall much more within the compasse of moderne and ordinarie obseruation, than did the euents of former Ages. And if we had any perfect register of such matters as had fallen out in other nations and kingdomes during the time of Moses, Iosuah, and the Iudges: wee should finde them much more consonant to the sacred Stories of these times, than are any Histories of later times, or of former ages, wherein any Historiographer of better account did liue. And albeit I can not excuse Herodotus and Ctesias, either from affecting fabulous narrations, or too great facilitie in admitting the superstitious report or hearesay-traditions of others: yet is mens mistrust of them vsually more vniuersall, than in scholastike discretion and obseruation it neede to be; onely because the like euents haue not beene vsuall in any Age throughout these parts of the world, since the times whereof they wrote. No maruell if many since that time suspect the signes and wonders of the old world, when as the Psalmist, that liued before most heathen Writers that are extant (besides such as relate like strange euents) complaines; Psal. 75.9. We see not our signes, there is not one Prophet more. Generally, after Iudah had beene captiued by the Gentiles, that and other kingdomes lie, as it were, vnder one paralel, and may almost be measured by the same line: the eleuation of the Iew and Gentile is much-what the same, and the same celestiall obseruation may serue for both. The Priests after their returne from captiuitie, were forbidden to eate of the most holy thing, till there arose vp a Priest with Vrim and Thummim, Ezra 2.63. But; either no such did arise at all, from the erection of the second temple vntill Christs time: or, if any did, it was but to giue this people a farewell of Gods extraordinarie speaking vnto them, either by Priests or Prophets.Antiquitatum. lib. 3. cap. 9. Iosephus confesseth, that reuelations by Vrim and Thummim did finally cease two hundred yeares before his time. But more probable is the opinion of others, that this, as all other kind of Prophecies, and many extraordinary signes of Gods power and presence (sometime most frequent in that nation) did cease with that generation which returned from captiuitie, or immediately after the finishing of the second temple: as if God during all that time, had appoynted a Fast or Vigil, as an introduction to the time of fulnesse, Ioel 2.28. Wherein their sonnes and daughters should prophecie, and their old men dreame dreames, and
 their yong men see visions, and his Spirit should be powred out on all flesh; as well vpon the seruant, as the master; as well vpon the Gentile, as the Iew, Malach. 1.11. For this cause. as I said, God had enioyned this long fast in Iudah, to humble the Iew, and ^each him, that he was no more his God than the Gentiles; and imposed silence to all his prophets, dis-inuring his chosen Israel from his wonted call: that so, this people might grow more mild, and apt to herd together with his other flocke, now to bee brought into the same fold, wherein both might ioyntly heare the great Shepheards voice. As God, elsewhere, had threatned, so it came to passe, that visions had ceased in Iudah, before the rising of the Romane Empire: and likely it is that presages by dreames, or like meanes, formerly vsuall among the Ancient heathen, did either altogether determine, or much decrease in many Nations about the same time. For which reasons, the Romans of that Age, being the onely wise men of the world (giuen too much by nature vnto secular policie:) did giue lesse credite to the relations of the Ancient Greekes, or the euents registred by their Auncestours in their owne Countrey. The like incredulitie remaineth in most of vs: but may be easily remoued by discouering the root of it.


CHAP. VI. The reasons of our mistrusting of Antiquities.
1 IT is the common practise of men to measure matters of Ancient times, by obseruation of the times and place wherein they liue: as commonly wee passe our censure on other mens actions and intentions, according to our owne resolutions and secret purposes in like cases. And, besides this generall occasion of mistaking other mens actions, and euents of other times, euery particular sort of men seek to assigne causes of things sutable vnto their proper faculties. The naturall Philosopher striueth to reduce all effects to the matter and forme, or some other sensible quality; the Mathematician, to abstract formes or figures
 or insensible influences; the Politician thinks no alteration in publike States, or priuate mens affaires, f^lles out, but from some politike cause or purpose of man: and whilest in the Annales of Antiquities hee reades of sundrie euents, surpassing the reach or skill of mans inuention, or contrarie to the ordinary course of nature, hee attributes all vnto the simplicitie or credulitie of their Ancestors. Albeit, if we should search the true cause of their credulitie in yeelding assent vnto such strange reports, it will easily confute the error of posteritie: for this credulitie in such particulars, could not haue beene so great in their Ancestors, vnlesse their mindes had beene first inclined to the generall, from the tradition of their Predecessors. But why their forefathers should either haue inuented such strange reports, or be so inclinable to beleeue them: if we search into the depth or first spring of this perswasion, we cannot imagine any other cause, but the reall and sensible experience of such strange euents as they reported to posterity. This did inforce beliefe vpon the first Progenitors of any Nation: and from the fulnesse of this perswasion, or actuall beliefe in them, was bred this credulitie or aptnesse in posteritie, to beleeue the like, which yet in successe of time did by little and little weare out. It is great simplicitie and vncharitable credulitie in vs, to thinke, that either the most Ancient, or middle Ages of the world, were generally so simple, credulous, or apt to beleeue euery thing, as some would make them. It had beene as hard a matter to haue perswaded men of these times, that there were no Gods, no diuine power or prouidence: as it would be to perswade the moderne Atheists, that there is an Almightie power, which created all things; gouerneth and disposeth all things to his glorie. The most politike Atheist now aliue, is as credulous in his kinde, as the simplest creature in the old world was: and will yeeld his assent vnto the Epicures or other brutish Philosophers conclusions, vpon as light reasons, as they did their beliefe vnto any fable concerning the power or prouidence of the Gods: the reason of both their credulities in two contrarie kinds, is the same. The often manifestation of an extraordinary power in battels, or presence in oracles, and sensible documents of reuenge from heauen, made the one prone to intertain any report of the Gods, though neuer so strange: and the want of like sensible signes or docume^ts of the same power in our daies
 (whilest all mens minds are still set on politike meanes and practises for their owne good,) doth make the other so credulous and apt to assent vnto any politike Discourse, and so auerse from beliefe of the Prophets or sacred Writers, which reduce al effects to the first cause. But this we can not doe so immediately as the ancient did: because God vseth his wisedome more in the managing of this politike world, than he did in times of old; and men naturally are lesse apprehensiue of his wisedome, than of his power; so that his present wayes are not so obuious at the first sight vnto sense as sometimes they were, though more conspicuous to sanctified reason now at this day than before, and the manner of his proceeding more apt to confirme true beliefe in such as follow his prescripts, than euer it was. For the same reason were the auncient Israelites more prone to idolatrie, than their successours were, after the erection of the second Temple; or eyther of them were at any time to serue their GOD. For the sensible signes and bewitching inticements of some extraordinarie powers, mistaken for Diuine, were then most common; and Gods wonders and miracles grew more rare, because they swarued from his commaundements. What Iew was there, almost, in the time of the Machabees, but would haue giuen his body for an Holocaustum, rather than sacrifice to any of the heathen Gods? The vndoubted experience of long woe and miserie for their former idolatrie, made them so auerse from this sinne; and the certaine signes of the Messiahs approach, did support them from falling into Atheisme. Such violence as these later willingly suffered at the hands of heathen Princes, rather than they would consent vnto idolatrie, their forefathers in the wildernesse were as readie to offer vnto Aaron, for not furthering them in their idolatrous imaginations So we reade Exod. 32. when God had but for a while withdrawne his extraordinarie presence from them, and Moses his instrument in working myracles, had bin but a short time out of their sight: they complaine he taried long, and gathered themselues together against Aaron, and said vnto him; Vp, and make vs Gods to goe before vs: for of this Moses, the man that brought vs out of the land of Egypt, we know not what is become of him. There was no danger lest they should turne Atheists, this was a sinne vnknowne in that age. And this people had experience enough of extraordinary
 powers in Egypt, which they took for Gods. So far are they from thinking there was no god that guides the world, that they thoght there were many: and if one did withdraw his presence, an other might serue to conduct them: one they must haue, otherwise all help of man was in vaine. As Iannes and Iambres had withstoode Moses miracles with their magick; so had the diuels, their masters, sought to work wonders about the Egyptian idols, which did obstupifie the people. For albeit their wonders were not so great, as gods: yet were they more delightfull to their outward sense; for their seruice for the most part, was sport and play. They were neuer daintie to shew their iugling tricks, for their owne aduantage; alwaies pliable to the humors and lusts of men: whereas the omnipotent maiestie of God would haue all to frame their liues and actions according to his written Laws, which might not be altered or mis-interpreted, at the pleasure of men; nor would he vouchsafe to worke his miracles in all ages, or vnto such as were vnworthie spectators of them. Thus had Satan his oracles and sacrifices oft times better frequented, than God had his; as in these times, such Preachers as will accommodate themselues vnto the peoples humors, are most frequented, but such as hold this sinne as sacrilege and dishonour to their God, are despised and set at naught. And though we may not mitigate Aarons fault, nor diminish these Israelites transgression (as their foolish posterity doth) by transferring the blame of this idolatrous fact vpon the Magicians which followed the host of Israell out of Egypt: yet it is more than probable from the circumstance of the Text (besides the tradition of the ancient Hebrews,) that there was some magicall or daemoniacall skill practised in the sodain moulting of this Egiptia^ god, whence this peoples superstition towards it was increased.Plutarch. de defecta oraculor. pag. 742. edit. H. Steph. The heathen princes of those times were no babies; as wary (we may presume) vpon what occasion to forgoe their children, as misers are to part with mony: and yet these were wrested from them, and their blood shed by their owne hands, to pacifie the rage of powers, then manifestly knowne for supernatural. But when both Gods wonders grew rare, and the diuells trickes waxed scant, either by restraint from aboue, or of their owne free choice, as if by their long continuance they had growne out of request: they see it more boote to draw the politike world vnto Atheisme, which neuer did florish
 vntill the rising of the Roman monarchie. Vnto this maine inconuenience of the late Romans, and other worldly wise mens distrust of wonders past, this second mischiefe did accrew, That sundry Writers of those times, did hold it a part of their profession, to fill their bookes with such stuffe, as they found in ancient stories; as if their Histories or Poems had not been currant, without as many parts or heads of inuention, as others had in former Ages. And this experience of counterfeit wonders, meeting with the want of experience of anie true wonders of that time, did concurre as the forme and priuation for the producing of infidelitie in mens minds already disposed to this euill by secular policie. And these were enough to carry our mindes below the lowest degree of any credulitie, or suspition of truth, in like reports: vnlesse the Scripture did forewarne vs of this guile and policie of Satan, which we may the better preuent, if we diligently obserue, first, the difference of times and places; secondly, how strange fables and lying wonders receiue being from notable and admirable decayed truths, as baser Creatures doe life, from the dissolution of more noble bodies.


CHAP. VII. Of the diuersitie of euents in different Ages.
1 THe diuerse Characters of different times rightly taken, giue vs as easie and perfect a crisis between the fictions of later, and the true annalls of former ages: as out of ordinarie discretion men vsually make between foolish trauellers reports of great wonders in Spaine, or France; and the iudicious records of vncouth sights, and strange euents, in the East or West-Indies. And we haue altogether as litle reason to enuy either the generall truth of strange euents recorded by the auncient, or the prototypos of poeticall inuentions in former times, for the want of like experience in later: as we haue to discredit Benzos martyrs, or other late nauigators obseruations of the East or West Indies,
 because many, who haue trauelled France, Spaine, and Italy, while they make true relatio^s of their trauels, relate no such euent, as the Indies afford many. And yet Gulles, when they flie abroad, will relate (amongst such as know them not) as strange matters of neare adioyning countries, as he that had compassed the vtmost endes of the world. Now it were a great folly to discredit all late nauigators, for the absurditie of some few Gulles: and as great madnesse it were to disparage all ancient stories, for the absurd and preposterous imitation of later writers, against all experience of later times. For diuersitie of times yeelds as great diuersitie of obseruations, as the diuersitie or distance of place; onely this is the difference; Daily obseruation yeeldes experiments of this diuersitie in place, whereas the word of God alone which indures for euer, giueth vs the sure rules and grounds of alterations in the euents of different ages. And yet in many remote places lately made known vnto the inhabitants of Europe, such strange euents as Antiquity hath told vs, were sometimes frequent in these Countries which wee now inhabite, are not at this day altogether vnusuall. And the face of Time is now there much-what such, as the ancient Registers of Times haue pourtrayed vnto vs: as if the affaires and fashion of this visible world, were framed according to some inuisible patterns or supercoelestiall Characters; which varying their aspect in reuolutions of time, did now (by reflecting that force vpon these remote countries, which they did on these neare adioyning in times past) produce the like shape and fashion of things there, as they haue done here. And those places shal, in time, com to the selfe same temper and disposition which we now enioy: and the posterity of such as now liue there, shal hereafter suspect the vndoubted stories of our times concerning their ancestors, as we do many ancient stories of Iewry, Syria, Asia, or our owne countries, for want of like moderne experiments in our land.
2 For the better rectifying of our assent, which must be by the right balancing of credulity and mistrust, it shall not be amisse to consider, that, besides these general diuersities of times and places, particular kingdomes and nations haue their seueral ages proportionable to infancie, youth, virilitie, and old age in men: nor is the period either of the whole age, or the seuerall parts thereof, one and the same in all, but varies in diuerse kingdomes, as the course of
 life, or seuerall ages do in diuerse men. Some kingdoms beare age well vnto a thousand; some to sixe hundred yeares: others breake and decay in halfe that time. Againe, as in the course of mans life diuersity of ages requires diuers manners or conditions: so, in the same people or nation, some euents are vsuall, as best befitting them in that degree of their growth which answeres to youth, or infancie, which seldome or neuer fall out in that part of their age which answeres vnto mature or old age in men, because not conuenient for their constitution then; and yet the want of like experience makes them as distrustfull and incredulous of what formerly had beene, as old men are forgetfull of their own disposition or temper in youth. Generally, when the fulnesse of any nations iniquitie (wherein their decrepit age consisteth,) is come, they grow more and more incredulous: so as they verifie the Latine prouerb concerning the disposition of old men: Nullus senex veneratur Iouem: more true of states;
As they grow old,Their zeale growes cold.
3 As the world was redeemed by Christ, so doe nations begin a new computation of their ages, from their admission to Christianitie. Some were come to youth, or virilitie in their profession, before others were borne in Christ: as Asia and Africa, for the most part, were Christians before Europe. Againe, the ancient inhabitants in some prouinces had beene Christians, long before other people that afterward subdued them and liued in their countries: as the Britaines in this Iland had beene long partakers of Gods mercy in Christ, before the Saxons; and the ancient Gaulles, before the Franks, which afterward seated themselues in their habitations. Generally, miracles were vsuall in the infancie of Christianitie, as we read in Ecclesiasticall stories: nor can it be certainly gathered when they did generally cease. To say they indured no longer than the Pimitiue Church, can giue no vniuersall satisfaction, saue only to such as thinke it enough for all the world to haue the light of the Gospell lockt vp in the Chancell of some one glorious Church: for some Churches were but in the Prime, or Change, when others were full of Christian knowledge. The vse of miracles at the same instant was befitting the one, not the other. For God vsually speakes to new-borne children in
 Christ, by miracles or sensible declarations of his power, mercy, or iustice: as parents deterre their children from euill, in tender yeres, by the rod, or other sensible signes of their displeasure; and assure them to goodnes with apples, or other like visible pledges of their loue: but, when they come to riper yeares, and are capable of discourse, or apprehensiue of wholesome admonitions, they seeke to rule the^ by reason. Proportionally to this course of parents doth God speak to his church: in her infancie, (wheresoeuer planted) by sensible documents of his power; in her maturitie, by the ordinarie preaching of his word, which is more apt to ripen and confirm true Christian faith than any miracles are, so men would submit their reason vnto the rules set down in Scripture, and vnpartially examine all euents of time by them, as elsewhere, God willing, we shall shew.
4 These grounds, well considered, will moue any sober spirit at the least to suspend his assent, and not suffer his mind to be hastily ouerswaied with absolute distrust of all such miracles, as either our writers report to haue bin wrought in this our land at the Saxons first comming hither, or the French Historiographers record in the first conuersion of the Franks, or in the prime of that Church.
5 And the Franks and Saxons before their conuersion to Christianity, were much-what of that temper, in respect of their present posteritie, as Greece was of in Homers, or Italy in the daies of Romulus, in respect of Ciceroes, or Plutarchs time: nor would I denie, but that admonitions by dreames were vsuall amongst them, as they had beene amongst the Easterne nations.
And, without preiudice to many noble Patriots and worthie members of Christ this day liuing in that famous Kingdome of France, I should interpret that dreame of Bassina, Queene vnto Childerick the first,Annonius in vita Childerici, lib. 1. of the present state of France: in which the last part of that threefold vision is more truly verified, than it was euer in the lineall succession of Childerick and Bassina, or any of the Merouingian, or Carlouingian families.
6 The vision was of three sorts of beasts; the first, Lyons and Leopards; the second, Beares and wolues; the third, of Dogs or lesser creatures, biting and deuouring one another. The interpretation which Bassina made of it, was registred certaine hundred yeares ago; That these troupes of vermine or lesser creatures, did signifie a
 people without feare or reuerence of their Princes, so pliable and deuoutly obsequious to follow the Peeres or Potentates of that Nation in their factious quarrels, that they should inuolue themselues in inextricable tumults to their owne destruction.
7 Had this vision beene painted, onely with this general notification; that it was to be emblematically vnderstood of some state in Europe: who is he that can discerne a picture by the knowne partie whom it represents, but could haue knowne as easily, that this was a mappe of those miseries that lately haue befallen France; whose bowels were almost rent and torne, with ciuill and domesticke broyles? God grant her closed wounds fall not to bleed a fresh againe, and that her people be not so eagerly set to bite and teare one another (like dogs or other testie creatures,) vntill all become a prey to Wolues and Beares, or other great rauenous beasts; which seeke not so much to teare or rent in heat of reuenge, as lie in waite continually to deuoure and swallow with vnsatiate greedinesse, the whole bodies of mightie Kingdomes, and to dye her Robes, that rides as Queene of Monsters vpon that many-headed beast, with streames of bloud that issue from the bodies squezed and crushed betweene their violent teeth; yea, euen with the royall bloud of Kings and Princes. Many such examples of admonitions by dreames and other extraordinary signes of future woe or calamities, both foretold and fulfilled many hundred yeares since Bassina and Childerickes daies, I could bring, which might confirme the historicall truth of the like mentioned in Scriptures to any ciuill heathen, as they haue inforced such as we most suspect for Atheists, to acknowledge a diuine power or prouidence in them. And I know not what reason any professor of truth can haue to denie the like presignifications in later times vpon extraordinarie occasions, or in some transmutations of Kingdomes; if he haue so much Religion as not to doubt of Nabuchadnezers vision. But of such signes, (whether good, or ominous) and of their lawfull vse elsewhere. Of the vse of prophecies amongst the heathen and barbarous people, and of that offence which some take at such as seem to giue any credit to them, as if this were against the truth of scriptures, we may perhaps take occasion to speake, when we come to vnfold the diuers kinds of prophecies amongst the Iewes, with their interpretations.
 Thus much may suffice for the remouall of that preiudice, which Atheists, Infidels, and other worldly-wise men haue, of their ancestors credulitie, or fabulositie, which were not incident to primarie antiquitie, but vnto ages in succession nearer to those times wherein wonders had beene plentifull: as commonly the braggadochian humour doth haunt the degenerate race of worthy ancestors, before their posteritie come to be sneaks or peasants, which in the race of priuate persons answeres in proportion to infidelity, the common symptome of decrepit age in kingdomes.


CHAP. VIII. Of the originall and right vse of Poetrie: with the manner of its corruption by later Poets.
1 THe positiue truth, which in the remoual of impedime^ts and offences hath bin made more tha^ probable in the former discourse, may yet be made more euident from more particular obseruations concerning the manner how monstrous fables haue descended from true wonders: which will best appeare by setting downe the original and right vse of Poetry.
2 A Poet being (as his name imports) a maker, according to the Latine prouerb, is not made by art, but framed to this diuine facultie by nature. Not that any amongst the Romans became Poets on a sodaine: but that many very fruitfull wits in all other kinds of learning, could not be forced by any industrie, art, or culture to such a temper, as was befitting this plant of Eden, which groweth not in any moderne breast without more tender care and greater cherishing, than any other slip or branch of the tree of knowledge; and yet when all is done seldome comes to any proofe, vnlesse it borrow grounds from the Ancient: as tender plants can hardly be remoued from a better foile to a worse, without some of the earth wherein they naturally grow. Were arts to begin a-new, Poetrie, which was the first and most common among the Ancient, in all probability, would spring the last, and grow the slowest amongst vs. Their wits of old were not naturally or generally better than ours:
 why then was the way to Pernassus, which vnto vs, vsing all help of art and imitation, is laborious and hard to ascend, so plaine and easie to them, without any guide or helpe; all other artificial learning being then either vnknown, or very scant? Such knowledge or obseruatio^s as they had or cared for, they knew not otherwise how to conuey vnto posterity, than by poetical numbers and resemblances. He is a Poet by nature (saith that excellent Poet and diuine Philosopher) That is apt to be rauished with the true and natiue beautie of such obiects as are represented to his senses, and can expresse his conceit by such pleasant resemblances, as ofte^ as he shal haue occasion to vtter his mind in writing or set speech. This inclination or disposition is as the ground or soile wheron Poetry doth naturally grow, whether in ancient or moderne breasts: but the ancient had this aduantage. The fashion of the world in their times was more apt to rauish their thoughts with admiration: wonderfull euents were then frequent: nor did their freque^cy abate, but rather increase wonderme^t, because their varietie was great, and the apprehension of inuisible or supernatural powers in them vsuall. So that admiration was then inforced vpon men, and the breasts of such as diligently obserued these euents, or were any way disposed by nature to the faculty, were inspired with liuely and sublimate affections, apt to vent themselues in such poetical phrase and resemblances, as we cannot reach vnto, vnlesse we raise our inuention by art and imitation, and stirre vp admiration by meditation and studie. And because neither our senses are moued with any extraordinary effects of Gods power, nor our minds bent to obserue the waies of his wisdome, so as we might be stricken with true admiration of them, we haue fewer good sacred poemes, than of any other kind. But as the Ancients chiefe learning did consist of Poetry: so the excellency of their Poetrie was chiefly seene in the power and natiue subiect of this faculty, that is, in matters of sacred vse or obseruation: whence the title of Vates did descend vnto secular or prophane Poets, which retained the number and manner of speech vsed by the former.
3 That such as were Vates indeed, were raught to this sublimity of speech by admiration of extraordinary euents, may be confirmed by the historicall narrations of the sacred story: wherin poeticall hymns or songs are the vsual consequents of strange or wonderful euents. As, Iud. 5. after the victory gotten ouer Sisera, Then sang


 Deborah, and Barach the son of Abinoam, the same day saying; Praise ye the Lord, etc. So likewise Hannah, after the Lord had heard her prayer, and taken away her barrennesse, burst out into the like Poeticall hymne, 1. Sam. 2. So did the blessed Virgin vpon her cosen Elizabeths salutation, and Iohn Baptist springing in her wombe, take vp her song, Luke. 1.46. My soule doth magnifie the Lord, etc. So doth Zacharie, Iohn Baptists father, take vp his prophecie, Luk. 1.68. and Simeon. Luke. 2.28. So likewise after the manifestation of Gods wonders in the red Sea, all his people (as if they had beene baptised in a sacred Helicon) presently turned Poets, Exod. 15.1.
