For seeing it could not content him to haue vanquished so many Kings and Kingdomes, but he will prouoke the King of Kings in his owne house, by his vnmannerly intrusion into his most secret closet, reserued alone, of all places of the earth besides, (though all the earth besides were his) for his Holinesse presence and his Priests: it seemed iust to this Lord of heauen and earth, the supreme disposer of all successe, to giue the Kingdomes subdued by Pompey into his fatall enemies hand, not leauing him so much firme ground of all his conquests, as might decently couer his miserable corps. Since the foundations of the Earth and Sea were laid,Hic, post tres consulat^s and totid^m triumphos domitumque terra rum orbem, sanctissimi ac praestantissimi viri, in id euecti super quod ascendi non potest, duodesexagesimu^ annu^ agentis, pridie natale^ ipsius vitae fuit exitus: in tantu^ in illo viro a se discordante fortuna, vt cui modo ad victoria^ terra defucrat, deesset ad sepultucam. Vell^i. Pater. Histor. lib 2. neuer had so high a flow of all good fortunes, so sudden, so strange, so low and naked an ebbe, Vt cui modo defuerat terra ad victoriam, deesset ad sepulturam: that he, who, as the Romaine Orator saith, had conquered more Prouinces than almost any of his Countrie-men had seene; HeeQui mare vniuersum, quod Romanis parebat, pacauerat, in eo ipso perijt, qui olim mille nauibus (tot enim serunt) prefuerat, tunc in nauicula prope Aegyptum occisus est, idque ab eo Ptolomaeo quodammodo, cuius patrem ipse in eam regionem ac Regnum reduxerat. Dyon. Hist. Rom. lib. 42. and statim post. Sic Pompeius, inter Romanos habitus antea potentissimus, vt Agamem non etiam cognominaretur, quod mille nauibus and ipse cum Imperio praefuiss^t, tunc quasi vnus de extremae fortis Aegyptijs, ad montem Cassium interijt, ea ipsa d^^, qua qu^ndam de Mithr^date and Piratis triumphum duxerat: vt ne in his quidem extrema cum primis conuenerint. that had commanded a thousand ships, restored the vse of the Sea to the Nations againe, and freed all others from the violence of Pyrates, sole Lord of that element, and the coasts adioyning; should (vpon that very day, which in memorie of thisThe P^raticall was the most honourable Warre that euer any Romane vndertooke, and iustly deserued a glorious triumph: but Pompey triumphed in his sinne, whilest hee included Iuries conquest, as put of that daies glorie, which the Lord for this reason would haue defaced by his miserable death, as he had polluted the solemnitie of Gods Saboath in Ierusalem, for hee tooke it on the Saboath day. Vid. Ioseph. lib. 1. de Bello Iudaico, cap. 5. matchlesse victorie hee had celebrated some few yeares before at Rome
 with greatest triumph and solemnitie) become a prey to a beggarly Aegyptian boat, and fall into such base hucksters handes, as knew not the worth of so great a Prince, but (as if hee had beene some rauenous Sea-monster, that had liued by publique harmes, of whose death only some pettie commoditie might bee made) present his head to the chiefe Magistrate in hope of reward, leauing that body, whose goodly presence had ouer-charged the greatest Temples, like a pestiferous carrion, or some offensiue garbage, or forlorne spawne, rather hid than buried in a little heape of sand.
2(...). Vix caperet te^plum quem parua recondit arena. Epitaph. Pompeij apud Appian. l. 2 de Bello Ciuili. The strange stupiditie, and more strange destinie of this famous Prince, so wise by nature, so well experienced, and alwaies before this time most fortunate, did argue to the Heathens apprehension, that he wasAppian. ibid. (...), as we would say, taken in the braine by the hand of God, and his hopes blasted from aboue. But such is the preposterous dullnesse of humane sagacitie in diuine matters, that euen where the print of Gods waies is most sensible and perspicuous,The Roma^s preposterous and impious collections vpon Pompeis ouerthrow. the wisest of vs runne counter still vntill his word direct our foote-steps, and his spirit giue life vnto our senses. For the most religious amongst the Romaines, deeming Pompey such as they held themselues, one that had neuer giuen iust offence to any of their Gods; vpon his miscarriage, either altogitherHence were these and like complaints. Marmoreo Licinus tumulo iacet: at Cato paruo, Pompeius nullo, quis putet esse Deos? disclaime the diuine prouidence, or else exclaime against the ingratitude or malignitie of celestiall powers; as if there had beene no other God or Gods, but such as they and Pompey had well deserued of. Whereas his fatall ouerthrow, whom their gods they thought had most reason to fauour, should haue instructed them, that there was a God of gods in Iurie, which did beare rule ouer the ends of the world, who would not bee worshipped after their fashion, as Pompey dreamed. For the reason of his desire to see the most holy place, was to bee resolued whether the Romaines, which worshipped the gods of euery Nation subdued by them, had not that God alreadie, which the Iewes adored:Romanerum primus Cn. Pomp domuit te^plumque iure victoriae ingressus est. Inde vulgatum, Nulla Intus Deum effigie vacuam sedem and Inania Arcana. Tacitus lib. 5 Histor. Vide Florum. l. 3. c. 5. but finding no grauen Image, nor likenesse of any thing in heauen or earth, many Romaines, which till that time had liued in suspence and admiration who this God of these Iewes should be, held their concealed mysteries for meere gulleries, and thought it folly to worship they knew not whom, forIucanus. Incerti
 Iudaea Dei; yet were his iudgments vpon this great Peere of Rome, the first among that people that had to doe with the Iewes, most certaine, yet iudgements mixt with mercie and long suffering. Seeing Vzzah and Vzziah King of Iuda, for intermeddling in the Priests office, were smitten, the one with sudden death, the other with continuall leprosie vntill his dying day: who can expect that this aliant should escape vnpunisht for like presumption? Neuerthelesse, because he did approch the most holy place, though with an vnsanctified heart, yet with no sacrilegious handes, hee had a longer time of repentance than his next Peere in might amongst the Romaines, his predecessor in like miserable and disgracefull death, though his successor in like, but more shamefull sacrilegious base prophanenesse.
The causes of Crassus mishaps3 That sacrilege was one especiall cause of Crassus miscarriage in the Parthian Warres, the Heathens of that time had obserued; and it may be, Plutarch, from vnwritten Traditions the nurse of error, did mistake the storie. Sure it was not the Goddesse of(...). etc. Plutarch in Crasso. Hierapolis, but the God of the holy Citie, which made the yong and aged to stumble one against an other. Or if Crassus and his sonne had this first Omen of their ouerthrow at their egresse out of this Goddesse Temple; this doth not argue that it was either soly or principally for his offence therein committed; albeit euen sacrilegious wrongs against the Heathen Gods, did oft redound to the true-Gods dishonour, being not intended by worldly minded men, so much against them in particular, as in contempt of the Deitie or diuine power simply: Nor are such warnings vsually sent immediatelyAs the destruction of Hierusalem did not immediately follow vpon our Sauiours, but vpon his seruants, S. Iames vniust death. vpon the principall fact, but rather after continuance in the like. And the vicinitie of thisThis Hiera-polis was Bambyca or Edessa, where Di^ceto the great Syrian Goddesse was worshipped, as appeares from Strabo, in hs 16. Booke. (...). 't was beyond Euphrates: whereas Crassus had ominous signes of his destruction at his first passage ouer that Riuer. Plutarch. places name, (which was a second witnesse of Crassus sinnes) might haue put him in minde of his former misdeedes in Ierusalem, with whose sacred treasure he had dealt iust so, as Plutarch saith hee did with the treasurie of that Goddesse of Hierapolis.
 Which makes me suspect that Plutarch did mistake the storie.Vid. Ioseph. l. 12. Antiq. cap. 13. For as Iosephus tels vs, he tooke away the 2000. talents, which Pompey left vntoucht, and 8000. besides. But such was the Heathens preiudice of the Iewes, that the least iniurie offred to their Idol-Gods was more than the most grieuous sacrilege that could be deuised against the God of Israel. The worst that could be done against his Temple, was, in many of their opinions, but as reprochfull words, which can beare no action, because not easily appliable to any determinate person: with many of them it was all one, Non esse Deos, and non apparere, represented in some visible shape or image. Thus Polibius, otherwise an ingenuous writer, imputes the cause of Antiochus Epiphanes suddaine and fearefull death, vnto his intended pillage of the Goddesse Artemis Temple; when as this miscreant was guiltie of that actuall crime before, for ransacking the temple of Ierusalem. But as the plenarie cause of Crassus miserable and shamefull death, was his shamelesly miserable and sacrilegious minde in generall: so in the meanes, or manner of this end, the Almightie would haue his particular offences against his Priest and Temple to be most eminent, and conspicuous.Eleazarus cum videret Crassum totum esse in colligendo auro times omnibus Te^pli orname^tis, trabe^ ha^c redemptione^ pro omnibus ei dedit, cum prius ca^ iurciurando obstrinxisset, nihil aliud loco moturum, contentum eo quod ipse trader^t, estimatum plurimis aureoru^ millibus. Haec trabs inserta erat trabi cauae ligneae quod caeteris omnibus ignotu^, solus scieb^t Eleazarus Crassus tame and hanc pro reliquo te^pli auro accepit, and mox violato iureiurando totum quantum intuserat ^g^ssit. Ios. l 14. ant. c. 12 Eleazar the high Priest, seeing him wholly bent to make a golden Haruest of the Parthian expedition, feared least hee should rake all the sacred Treasure into his coffers. For preuenting of which mischiefe hee presents him with a golden beame, whereon the hangings of the Temple hung, hoping thereby to redeeme the rest of the sacred Treasure; but hee hauing gotten this into his handes, which otherwise he could not haue found (being couered with wood) contrarie to his oath, most agreeable to his humour, seazed vpon all the residue. Yet gold, which hee thus greedily sought, as (to his seeming) the only sure nerue of warre, by the Almighties disposition, became the indissoluble chaine of his dismall faults. As loue to it had made him periure himselfe to circumuent Gods Priest; so did it expose him to circumuention by a periuredMax ma^ aute^ calamitatis partem Angarus ipsis Os^^enus attulit. Is Romanis ant^a foedere iunct sub Pomp. barbaroru^ partes tum poti^res habuit. Quod idem Alchandonius quoque Arabs fecit, semper potent^orise solitus adiu^gere: Caetera^ hic quia manifesto defecerat a Romanis ca^eri facile p^tiat. Angarus aute^, quum Partho studeret, simulauit se Crassi amicu^, liberaliterque ei pecunia^ sua^ impende^s, et explorauit omnia eius co^silia, et Partho enu^ciauit. Dion. lib. 40. villaine; who hauing found out his appetite, prepared a fit bait for his bane. For by feeding this greedie thirst of gold, he insinuated himselfe into the societie of his secrets, which he disclosed vnto the Parthian. Had Crassus wits naturally beene so dull, or had he vsually shewed himselfe so grosse and sottish, as hee
 proued in this expedition: he had neuer borne any place amongst the Romaines, much lesse had they euer permitted him to manage any forraine Warres.Vid. Plutarch in Crosso. Dionem l. citato. Appian. de E^ll. Parth. But partly from his prodigious stupiditie, vncapable of any warning by so many ominous signes and tokens, as did obstupifie his whole Armie besides, partly from his more than brutish facilitie, in taking an vncouth way (as hee had beene a tame beast before the drouer) vntill he came to the very stand, where his enemies stood with their bowes bent, and their arrowes of death made readie vpon the string for his destruction, all the Romaine writers agree, that hee was lead awry by sinister fates. Now if they had but once read what God he was, that had blinded Absolon to disclaime2. Sam. c. 17. Achitophels good counsaile, and ratifie Hushars plot for his ouerthrow; they would easily haue granted that the same God, and no other,Tum Cassiani rursum agre ferebat, and om^sso Crosse, qui monitores audi^e gra^abatur, connicijs meessabant Angarum. Sceleratissime hominum, quis te ma^ut damen ad nos adduxit? qu^^us veneficijs quicus ^raeflig^s persuafisti Crasso vt per vastas solitud^nes ^ter faceret, Numadae latronu^ trincipi magis decorum quam Romano Imperato i^ ille versip^ll^ confortabaters b^a^: ditijs, and hortabatur? d^rarent paulisper ^milites vero subleuabat accurr^ns, and cum risu cauillabatur: Quid, per Campa^^m vos iter facere putatis, vt requiratis sontes ac riuos and vmbras, scilicet, balneasque and con^anita ^enae ^^er^^ia: non meminestis vos tranfire per Arabum and Assyriorum confinia? Jtatum A^ge^^ (...) ut quidam castigabat: and priusquam deprehenderetur in perfidia abequ t^^t, (...) C^s^^, finge^s se curaturum necessaria and turbaturum consiliae hostium. Ap^ian de Ecl^ ^^ra. (...) ^^od vti^^e coafilium Crassus caepisset, dehortatus hominem eft: sin damnosum, (...) rem confecit. Crassus Seleuciam contendere decreuerat, quo se t^to cum exer^^t^ (...) com^ae (...) E^^^^atem ac trans cundem peruenire posse cogitabat: a Seleucia (quam, vt a Grae^^s (...) p^rabat in suaem potestatem venturam) ad Ctesiphontem Vrbem haud difficulter se tre^^^^urum (...) tanquam multo tempore opus habiturum, vt repudiaret, ac potius aduersum Surene^ qu^ p^opt (...) manu esset, iret Abgarus persuasit. Quibus conflitutis cum Crassum, vt periret, Sur^^e^ (^um qui ^andi praetextu frequenter congrediebatur) vt superare posset parauisset, Romanos nihil souert^^, e^ (...) ad certam iam victoriam proficiscentes, eduxit: in eisque tum per infidias opprimendis Parthum ad^^t. vi^ Dionem lib. 40. As he came into this danger by Abgarus trecherie, so was he slaine after he had yeelded himselfe vnto Surenas, contrarie to his oath and promise, Vid. Appian. l. citato, and Strabon. l. 16 had infatuated Craessus heart to renounce Cassius, and other graue experienced Roman warriors holesome aduise, and betake himselfe wholly to the barbarous fugitiue Abgarus directions, suborned by the Parthian to betray him.
4 But Cassius, much wiser than his Generall in this one particular of mistrusting forainers, was afterwards as farre ouer-seene in the maine chance, and ouer-taken with that sinne which had caused Crassus blindnesse: First, polluted with likeSo saith S. Austine de Ciuit. Dei l. 18 c. 45. Postea (i. post. Pompeij victoriam de Iudae is) Cassius Templu^ spoliauit, Of his cruell exactions in Iudea, Vide Ioseph. antiq. l. 14. c. 18. sacrilege, and cruell oppression of these Iewes: then with his owne bloud,Of his grosse error in mistaking his victorious friend for his persecuting enemie. Vide Velleum Paterculum l. 2. and reliquos Rom. hist. autores. shed by his seruant at his commandement, vpon as grosse an ouer-sight as Crassus had committed So shall they all, sooner or later, bee infatuate, that robbe God of his honour, and put their
 trust in wrong or violence. And thus till this time did they perish all, as many as bare ill will to Sion; for Hierusalems houre was not yet come, because the day spring had not visited her from on high. The glorie of her glorious Temple was not as yet reuealed; vnto whom after her children had offered greater disgrace than the Romaines had done to their Temple, the staffe of her wonted stay begins to breake, the bones of her former peace vntwine, and only one part of her double fates remaine; if then shee fall, shee riseth not againe, shee hath no inclination left but to destruction; the burthen of the Fathers sinnes, and the yoke of captiuitie due thereto, growes heauier and heauier in the descent vpon posteritie, without all hope of recouerie, much lesse of reuenge, vpon such as offer her greatest violence; but rather happy shall that man be thought, and highest earthly honour shall bee the wages of his seruice, that rewards her children, as they had serued their Lord and Sauiour. But these times were not come in Crassus or Cassius daies, in which som reliques of her ancient hopes remained, to see the rods and scourges of her correction consume and wither, after once the Almightie had taken off his punishing hand. And if vnto these three aboue mentioned, we adde the like destinie of Antonie and Scipio; the ill successe of other Romaines, who had ought to do with these Iewes before our Sauiours time: we may conclude, that although the Romaines were then Lords of the earth, yet this people, whom they held as base, retained the priuilege of Gods roiall Priests. Although the soules of all flesh were the Lords, who for this cause reuenged the oppressed in euery Nation: yet,Ierem. 2. v. 3. Leuit. 22. v. 10. Israel only (as the Prophet speakes) was as a thing hallowed vnto the Lord, his first fruits; all such as deuoured them did offend, euill should come vpon them, although inflicted by their owne, or their seruants handes at their appointment. Lastly, if we call to minde the former distinction of Ages, and the diuers manner of Gods dealing with them, before and after the Babylonish captiuitie; the contraction or abridgment of their large priuiledges, in the long suceession of times foretold, by ancient, and acknowledged by their owne latter writers: wee can not mistrust the amplitude of their fundamentall charter, of their historicall narrations of what the Lord had done of old vnto Iabin, Sisera, and Sennacherib, would we (allowing some different condition of times) compare
 theirs with Pompeys and his Complices vnusuall Fates.Gods iudgements vpon Pompeys factio^, were most iust, because they had participated of his sinne Gods power was more immediately manifested in the one, his Wisedome more admirable in the other, his Iustice the same in both. Yet a Romaine would reply: If Pompey had so grieuously offended, why should he not haue borne the whole burden of his sins? So he should, had either he alone offended, or the Romains suffered him to haue liued a priuate life; but if they will associate themselues as members to their naturall head, and professe their seruice and alleageance vnto him, that stood as proscribed by the court of heauen: Gods quarrel with the whole faction is most iust, all of them are guiltie of their Generalls sinnes, all liable to the plagues and punishments due thereto. Yet, besides that Pompeys intolerable presumption and prophanenesse, according to the vsual course of Gods iustice, might propagate his destinie vnto such of his adherents, as had beene free from any actuall wrong done to the Iewes, or contempt of their God and religion in particular: a great many of his chief followers had polluted themselues with the like sinne in Ierusalem. For, asIudaeorum quidem 12. millia occubuerunt: Romanorum vero perpauci, sed plures sauciati sunt Nih^l autem grau^us in illa clade Iudoeorum genti visum est, quam sanctum illud arcanum, neq, cuiqu^m pr^us visum alienis esse detectu^. Denique Pompeius vna cum suis comitibus in templum ingressus, vbi neminem praeter pontifice^ adesse fas erat, quae intus erant candelabra cum ly^hnis and me^sis in quibus tibare atque adolere moris est, and vascula ex auro cuncta spectauit. Ioseph. de Bello Iudaico. l. 1. c. 5. Iosephus tels vs, Pompey went not into the most holy place alone, but accompanied with a multitude. All of them, no doubt, had sinned presumptuously against the admonitions and threatnings of Gods Priests; and this peoples curse (it seemes) did follow them, whither they themselues durst not; for as the same writer testifies, no other calamitie in that war did grieue the Nation so much, as this pollution of their Temple.
5 Whatsoeuer Tullies or other politique Romains conceipt of this people in his time was; many amongst the Romains, as well as in most other Nations, had (without question) either obserued the like fatal mischances of such as vexed them, or else had felt som good in obseruing their lawes, whose persons vnknowne they hated. In one, or both which respects, these Iewes stood vpon better termes with their Conquerors, than any other captiues did. And vnlesse it had been a receiued opinion amongst other Romaines, that this had been a people fauoured of the diuine powers; why should Tullie haue obiected their late conquest by Pompey to proue the contrarie? And (me thinks) it might haue moued him and others, so much deuoted to the Romaine Gods before, to haue thought these Iewes did serue a better God then they knew any, after they had seene their owne state vtterly ruined
 without all hope of recouerie, and their Gods either vnwilling, or vnable, (as Tullie doubtfully complaines) to redresse those miseries and calamities, of which they should at least haue giuen them warning, when as Hierusalems wals,Vide Ios. l. 14. Antiq. c. 14.15.16. which Pompey had demolished, were (according to this peoples hope, from which no power on earth could deiect them, being supported by the expresse promise of their God) reedified within twentie yeeres, and they graced with great Priuiledges for their good seruice performed to Caesar. For chiefly by their meanes became hee Lord of Aegypt, the first and surest ground of his good successe in Africke; as Iosephus out of the publike Decrees and testimonies of Romain Writers boldly auoucheth, challenging the heathen to disproue him or his testimonies, if they could, though this they easily might haue done, if hee had cited them amisse, because the Originals were then euery where extant.
6 The former testimonies alleaged out of Tullie (whose works we haue) so well agreeing with Iosephus, who it seemes had neuer read them, will not suffer any ingenious man to suspect the truth of that which the same Iosephus cites, out of Strabo the Cappadocian, whose workes are lost.Quatuor erant genera in Cyrenensium vrbe, ciues Agricolae, inquilini, and quarti Judaei, hoc^am in omnes vrbe^ subrepsit, necest facile inuenire locum in vrbo habitabili, quem recepta semel non obtineat, na^ and Aegyptus and Cyrene, vtpote eisdem solitae parere dominis, aliaeque multae regiones horum^itus admiserunt: alunique congregationes Iudaeorum maximas, tempore coalitas and vtentes institutis domesticis, per Aegyptum sane etiam coloniae sunt eius gentis, absque eo quod Alexandrinae vrbis magna pars ei assignata est. Habet etiam proprium magistratum, qui suae gentis res administret, controuersias ex iure dirimat, and contractus atque pacia rata habeat, non aliter quam fit in absoluta republica. Iosephus Antiq. Iud. lib 10. c. 12. The Iewes (saith he) haue crept into most Cities, nor can a man almost name any place in the inhabited world, where they once get footing, but they hold possession. Aegypt, Cyrene, and many other nations haue admitted their rites, and in lieu of them, nurse huge multitude of Iewes vsing their owne domestique lawe; besides that a great part of Alexandria is assigned to their vse, there be colonies of this nation throughout Aegypt which enioy magistrates of their owne, for determining all controuersies of right and wrong in such sort and forme as is vsed in absolute states.Seneca inter alias ciuilis theologiae superstitiones reprehendit Sacramenta Iudaeorum. Christianos tameniam tunc Iudaeis inimicissimos in neutram partem commemorare ousus est, ne vel laudaret contra suae patriae veterem consuctudinem, vel reprehenderet contra propriam forfitan voluntatem. De illis sanc Iudaeis, cum loqueretur ait, cum interim vsque eo sceleratissimae gentis consuetudo conualuit, vt per omnes iam te^ras recepta sit, victi victoribus legem dederunt. Mirabatur haec dicens, and quid diuinitus ageretur ignorans, subiecit plane sententiam, qua significaret, quid de illorum sacramentorum ratione senti^et. Ait enim: Illi tamen causas ritus sui nouerunt, and maior pars populi facit, quod cur faciat, ignorat August. l. 6. de ciu. D. c 11.4 Sequitur auri illa inuidia Iudaici. Hoc nimirum est illud, quod non longe a gradibus Aurelijs haec causa dicitur, ob hoc crimen hic locus, abs te Laeli at illa turba quaesi ta est. Scis quanta sit manus, quanta concordia, quantum val^t in concionibus etc. Cic. Orat. pro Flacco. Saint Augustine hath the same
 obseruation out of Senecaes books concerning superstition which this reuerend father had perused, though, with diuerse others of that famous Phylosophers workes, now lost.
7 What Strabo had obserued of these Iewes in Syllaes, and Seneca in his time, is intimated by Tullie in fewer words. You know well (saith hee vnto his aduersarie) what a great faction it is: how closely they band together, what sway they beare in assemblies. But how great soeuer the number of this people was at Rome, they durst not haue beene so bold in the Mistresse-citie of the world, vnlesse their patrons there had beene many. And it seemes by Tullies conclusion,Aurum in aerario est, furtum non reprehenditur, a iudicibus oratio auertitur, vox in coronam turbamque effunditur. Cic. Orat. pro Flacco. in the fore-mentioned place, that the bare vnkindnesse offered by Flaccus to this Nation, was worse taken at Rome, than the wrongs and violence, which he was accused to haue done to sundrie other people. The gold (saith he) for which Flaccus was accused, is in the treasurie: you charge him not with theft, but onely seeke to make him odious: your speech is turned from the iudges and directed to the companie.


CHAP. III.
Tacitus obiections against the Iewes refuted, by their palpable grosnesse, and more competent testimonie of other Heathen writers.
1 VNto this their powerfulnesse in perswading other Nations to renounce their owne, and imbrace their Lawes and religion,Hiritus quoqu^ modo inducti, antiquitate defendu^tur. Cetera instituta sinistra, foeda prauitate valuere. Nam pessimus quisque spreta relig onibus patrijs, tributa and stipes illuc congerebant. Tacit. Histor lib. 5. Tacitus ascribes the increase of these Iewes estate; albeit hee maliciously attributes this attractiue force vnto the impietie of their Lawes, as if by sympathie they had wrought most vpon wicked and depraued natures. But wherein did their impietie consist?Profana illic omn a, quae apud nos sacra. Rursum concessa apud illos, quae nobis incesta. Tacit. Hist. lib. 5. What wee Romanes esteeme sacred, they account prophane, what is polluted to vs, is lawfull to them. This argues that either the Romaine religion, or theirs, was superstitious and prophane: and hee, like a true patriot and right Romanist, loath to suspect that Religion wherein his
 forefathers had prospered so well, charges the contrarie orthodoxall with superstitious impietie. Most true it was, that the Iewes of his time were a wicked people, but euery way of the loosing hand: their strength, which had been long in gathering, was suddenly broke by Titus, and their wonted means of encrease, by addition of Proselytes, quite cut off. For after the Temples destruction, Nullus ad amissas ibat amicus opes: from the first day of our Sauiours Ministeriall Function, they woon not halfe so many Gentiles to Iudaisme, as our Sauiour and his Disciples did Iewes vnto Christianitie. Tacitus then spake not of such Iewes as liued in his time, when their strength and greatnesse was in the wane; but of their ancestors, during the time of the second Temple. Nor was it that, which was most wicked indeed in this people, or their predecessors (as their particular opposition, or contrarietie vnto diuine truths) but rather what was onely good in them, as their constancie in their Religion, and stedfast profession of Abrahams faith, common to them and the Christians; which was the ground of this Politikes hatefull censure,The grounds of worldly greatnesse hate against resolute professors of truth. Jnterim in ijs, qui ad me tanquam Christiani defer ebantur, hunc sum sequutus modum. Interroga^i ipsos, au essent Christiani: confit^ntes iterum ac tertio rogaui, suppliciu^ minatus, perseuerantes duci iussi. Neque enim dubitabam, qualecunque esset quod faterentur, peruitacia^ certe, and inflexibilem obstinationem debere pun^ri. Plin. Epist. li. 10. Epist. 100. for this reason a-like bitter against them and the Christians. That either should be so resolute in defence of their owne Religion, was, in his opinion, a pernicious superstition, lyable to any punishment that could be proposed, as another Romane Writer of his time was not ashamed to auouch. If wilfulnesse simply deserue punishment, the carnall minded make no scruple in what measure it be inflicted, seeing the more grieuous the torture is, the greater it alwayes argues their wilfulnesse or obstinacie to be, that will vndergoe it rather than obey their superiors, so as the crime seeme alwayes commensurable to the punishment, though it could be increased in infinitum. And Tacitus seemes well to approue of Antiochus sauage crueltie against the Iewes, as a medicine that would in time haue wrought a reformation, if the Parthian had permitted this cruell Surgeon to haue applyed his patients with change of such corroding playsters. So immoderate is the ambitious mens desires, that to be Lords of others bodies doth but whet their appetite, and stirre vp a longing to become Lords of their faith and consciences also. And to refuse absolute obedience in matters spirituall, as well as temporall, vnto such as are
 competent Iudges of the one, not of the other, is a sinne as hatefull as rebellion, the foulest crime that can be conceiued or fashioned in the Polititians braine.
2 Besides these generall motiues, which would minister enough of matter for superiours to condemne their inferiours: it did in particular exasperate Tacitus, and other politicks of his temper,Iudei (vt est ge^s ea irae, si semelea correpta fuerit, acerbiasimae) multa ac grauia mala quidem ij Romanis intulerant, longe tamen plura passisunt Dion. lib. 49. to see so many naturall Romaines renounce their name and countrie, forsake father and mother, friend and alliance, for maintenance of Iewish religion. And albeit that Nation had sustained incredible calamities by the Romaines, yet it vexed him to remember, that they should bee able to haue done the Romaines so much mischiefe, alwaies stirring when others were quiet; being, to his seeming, a base people in respect of many other subiect to the Romane Empire. Perhaps his hate to Christians was propagate from his inueterate malice against these Iewes, in whose region Christianitie (as he confesseth) did first spring. ButAffirmabant autem hanc esse summam, vel culpae sue erroris, quod essent solitistato die ante lucem conuenire, carmenque Christo quasi Deo dicere sec^m inuicem, seque sacramento non inscelus aliquod obstringere: sed ne furta, ne latrocinia, ne adulteria committ^rent, ne fidem fallerent ne depesitum appellati abnegarent Quo magis necessariu^ credidi ex duabus ancill^s, quae ministrae dic^ba^tur, quid esse veri, et per tormenta quaerere Sed nihil aliud inueni, quam superstitionem praua^, and immodicam: ide^que d^lata cognitione ad consulendum te de cucurri. Plin. Epist. 100. l. 10. Plinies testimonie will sufficiently quit the Christians from those imputations, whichTransgressi in morem eorum idem vsurpant, nec quidquam prius imbauntur, quam contemnere Deos, eruere patriam: parentes, liberos, fratres, vilia habere. Tacit. Hist. l. 5. Tacitus vniustly layeth vpon them. As for that impietie, wherewith hee chiefly chargeth these Iewes religion, it implies an euident contradiction. Such as conforme themselues (saith he) to their customes are likewise transformed in minde. The fist precept, wherewith they are seasoned, is to contemne the Gods, to put off all naturall affection to their Countrie. If the Iewes did either forsake father, or mother, or other kindred, It was for the loue of their God, religion and Countrie. For, vnlesse the greatnesse of their loue to God drowned the other, no people in the world did euer match them in loue to their kinsfolkes, friends and countrimen. But if they perswaded the Romains to esteem the glorie of Rome as vile, in respect of Hierusalem; and account Romish rites and ceremonies, compared with theirs, as sacrilegious and prophane; they did that but vpon good and warrantable grounds, which any true Romane would haue done vpon farre worse; that is, they sought their countries good, by winning the good will of others to their estate and religion.
3 But what madnesse possessed Tacitus his minde, that hee should thinke, or rather write, for I doe not thinke that hee thought, or cared what he wrote, when he auouched, that the wickednesse of their lawes was a meanes of alluring wicked or lewde companions to their obseruance? I would their greatest enemies were admitted Iudges; whether such as indeed were, or such as any ciuill heathen would so account, not such as it pleased Tacitus only, out of meere pride and splene, to call wicked persons, would not in all likelihood, be more readie to subscribe vnto the rites of Venus or Bacchus, (whose seruice Tacitus so well likes,) or any other of the Romish Gods, than tie themselues vnto Iudaicall ceremonies; which, once subscribed vnto, were to be most strictly obserued by Aliens as well as Iewes; nor could they bee throughly acquainted with their lawes, or admitted to other mysteries, vntill they had communicate with them in that Sacrament of Circumcision, alwaies most loathsome and grieuous to flesh and bloud. What pleasures of the flesh, what dissolutenes or luxurie, or what, that can be properly called sinne or enormitie, did their lawes maintaine or nourish, or their rites or ceremonies any way insinuate? All that Tacitus (comparing their rites with those of Bacchus) could in conclusion say against them, was; ThatTiber Jestos loetosque ritus pos^t: Judocor^^mos absurdus sord^dusque. Tacit. Hist. lib. 5. Bacchus his rites were merrie and pleasant, theirs absurd and base.
4 Vnto these politicall surmises of Tacitus, altogether ignorant of forraine antiquities, I will oppose the iudgement of a lesse partiallStrabos testimonie of the Iewes religion Lib. 16. writer, and a professed antiquarie, liuing about our Sauiours time: from whom amongst o^hers we may gather, that the famous and conspicuous hill of Sion stood as a probleme to oppose the nations; and from admiration of her strange and vnobseruable fortunes and change, were so many opinions blazed abroad of those Iewes originall and state. Of all that were extant in his time, capable of any credit, this following went best for currant, and did sway the most, to wit; That these Iewes (as you heard before of his errour in this particular) were descended from the Egyptians: the cause of their departure out of Egypt, was to seeke a place, where they might worship God aright, perswaded hereunto by Moses, whom he takes for an Egyptian Priest, but one that condemned the Egyptians for painting beastes, the
 Africans and Grecians for vsing pictures of men to represent God; deeming it a madnesse to imagine, that he that contained all things, could be represented by any visible or sensible creature. Chastitie and holinesse were the dispositions of such as sought him, or could hope to know-his will: and for this purpose ordained sacrifice, neither chargeable to such as should vse them, nor otherwise offensiue by their vndecencie, lewdnesse or absurditie.(...). With these perswasions (saith he) Moses preuailed with the better sort, and such as feared God, to forsake Egypt: and seated once about Ierusalem, neighbour countries did associate themselues vnto them, allured by the equitie of their lawes, and the puritie of the religion which they professed, whence he erected a new kingdome, and that no meane one. And his successors for a time continued in his institutions, iust and rightly religious. But after they had (as Tacitus obserueth) ioyned the Priesthood to support their Kingdome, they grew moreYet was Strabo somewhat offended with Circumcision and their Sabboths, as being ignorant of their caus^s, as their Sabboths indeed were then superstitious. superstitious, tyrannicall to their owne, and noisome neighbours to other countries. d Yet was their Hold or Fort still had in honour: not detested as a nest of thieues or seat of tyrants, but reuerenced as a temple. Thus farre Strabo. Who, although he were mistaken in sundrie particulars of this peoples antiquitie, (as all beside themselues of necessitie were, by reason this sacred volumne was kept secret from all such as did not obserue their rites) yet from tradition he had learned as much, as could be knowne of them in generall: That Moses their first Law-giuer was a Prophet, and one that relyed not vpon pollicie, but the diuine Oracles; that this people in ancient times had bene much better, and had prospered accordingly.
13 With this Strabo the Geographer, that noble Historian Dion Cassius well accords, but more fully with Strabo the Cappadocian, whose works, now lost, Iosephus cited. This people (saith he) differ from others, as in many other points and daily practise of life, so especially in this that they worship no other Gods, but onely one of their owne, whom they hold to be inuisible and ineffable,(...). Strabo lib. 16 D^o^^ acquitall of these Iewes from Tacitus imputations. Diuersum a reliquis hominibus obtinent cum a^^s in veb^^, vsuque vitae quotidiano, tum eo praesertim quod n^llum ex coeteris diis colunt, vnum autem quendam s^mr^^ s^^dio venerantur. Nec vllum simulac^rum. Hierosolymis vnqu^m habuerunt; nimirum suum illum Deum (...) ^ilem and formae expertent, relig^oso eius cultu coeteros mortales superant. Dion. lib. 37. and for this cause admit
 not any Image of him: yet doe they worship him more deuoutly and religiously then any other people do their Gods. But who this god of theirs was, or bow he came at first to be thus worshipped, how greatly he was feared of this people, were points he listed not meddle withall, many other had written thereof before him.Regioipsa, Iudaea gens, Iudaei appellantur. Id cognome^ti vnde initium coeperit haud scio. Quinus and alii homines qui secundum eorum statuta viuant, id gerunt, quanquam alieniginoe Est^d genus hominum apud Romanos etiam, atque tametfi soepe in minutum sicerit, ita tamen auctum est, vt etiam condendi leges licentiam sibi vinaicarit. Or as some read, vt legum qucq^e potestatem vicerit. Dion. ibid. It seemes he gaue but little credence vnto Tacitus discourse of their originall, for he ingeniously professeth: That he knew not whence they had this name of Iewes, but others that followed their rites, although Aliens by birth and progenie, did brooke the same name or title, euen amongst the Romans themselues there were of this profession. Hee addeth; Although this people had beene often crushed and diminished: yet did they rise and increase againe aboue the controll of all other lawes, onely subiect to their owne. Thus hee spake of the Iewes liuing in Pompeys time, after which they had beene often crushed before Tacitus wrote, yet recouered strength againe.


CHAP. IIII.
The meanes of these Iewes thriuing in captiuitie. In what they exceeded other people, or were exceeded by them.
1 THese allegations, and many other, which out of Heathen writers I could bring, sufficiently proue, that albeit these Iewes tasted of as bitter calamities as any other did: yet had they this strange aduantage of all; that whereas all other were forsaken of their friendes in their aduersitie, and their Lawes vsually changed by their conquerours, oft-times abrogated or neglected by themselues vpon their ill successe: these Iewes still found most friends, and their lawes (neuer forsaken by them) most earnest fauourers, in the time of their captiuitie and distresse. This was quite contrarie to nature, politike obseruation, or custome of the world. Wherefore seeing nature and policie can affoord vs none, we must seeke resolution from their lawes. The reasons subordinate to the cause of causes (Gods
 prouidence) were these. In the time of their distresse, they did more faithfully practise their lawes themselues, and had better opportunitie, or greater necessitie of communicating them vnto others: they being of themselues alwaies most potent to allure sober and discreet mindes to their obseruance, made knowne and not preiudiced by the foolish or sinister practise of their professors. So their great Law-giuer had foretold, Deut 4. vers. 4.5.6. Behold I haue taught you ordinances and lawes, as the Lord my God commanded me, that ye should do euen so in the land, whither ye goe to possesse it. Keepe them therefore and doe them; for that is your wisedome and vnderstanding in the sight of the people, which shall heare of all these ordinances, and shall say; Onely this people is wise and of vnderstanding, and a great Nation. For what Nation is so great vnto whom the Gods come so neare vnto them, as the Lord our God is neare vnto vs in all that wee come vnto him for? And what Nation is so great that hath Ordinances and Lawes so righteous,God was more sauourable to these Iewes than to any other people. as all this law which I set before you this day? That they had not in later times so great prosperitie, as others had, was no argument that their God was not more neare to them, than the Gods of other Nations to their worshippers; for he was a God of Gods, and Lord of Lords, which did good to euery Nation; yea he made the Romanes so great a Nation, albeit they knew it not. That these Iewes were now in subiection, and the Romanes Lords, was no argument, that he was better to the Romanes than to them, or that they were a lesse Nation, if we make an equall comparison. For if God should often recouer a man from dangerous diseases, and propagate his life vnto two hundred yeares, in health and strength competent for old age; this were no argument to proue that he were not more fauourable to him, than to men of yonger yeares or middle age, whose strength is greater for the present, but they vnlikely to recouer health often impaired, or to renue life once lost in humane estimation, or to account halfe so many yeares.In what sense the Iews might truly be thoght a mightie na^^^^. In like sort was this peoples often recouerie from so many ouerthrowes and captiuities; their long continuance a distinct nation from others: more extraordinarie than the Romanes present strength or greatnesse. And albeit many other Empires and States were larger, than the Kingdome of Israel was at any time, yet no other people could be said so great a Nation
 as this. For others continued the same rather by identitie of soyle, or like forme of gouernment, then by any reall or materiall vnitie or identitie of people: their increase was meerely politicall, and their greatnesse rose by way of addition or accumulation; that is, by admitting such mixture of others, that from the first erection of the Kingdome, ere it came to its full greatnesse, the number of Aliens might ouerspreade and hide the naturall inhabitants, or progenies of such as laid the fundamentall Lawes thereof, which were seldome so continuate by direct succession, as they might be rightly distinguished from others. And as Theseus his ship was accounted one and the same, because it retayned the same forme, though not so much of the same Timber, whereof it was first built, as did goe to the making of halfe the keele: so the greatest States amongst the Heathens, retayned perhappes some few fundamentall lawes, or relikes of Ancient Families descending from their first founders, in which respect alone they might be taken for one Kingdome, but not so properly termed one People or Nation, to whom greatnesse could be truly attributed, seeing a great many of seuerall people were to share in this title. But these Iewes (besides the perpetuall vnitie, their particular, as well as fundamentall lawes, lesse varied eyther by change, addition or abrogation, than the Lawes of any other Nation) continued still one and the same people by a strict Vnion of succession, their grouth was naturall, after the manner of vitall augmentation. For albeit they admitted some mixture of strangers, they could notwithstanding alwaies distinguish the progenie of forraine stockes from their naturall branches, which they could still deriue from their seuerall stemmes, and these all from one and the same roote: so that after so many changes, and alterations of their State from better to worse, and backe againe; after so many glorious victories, as Scriptures mention gotten by them ouer others; and so many captiuities of their persons and desolations of their countries, as others had wrought, they remained still one and the same people, by such a kinde of vnitie, as a great Oake is said one and the same tree, from its first spring to its last fall, whether naked and bereft of leaues by blastes of Autumne or Winters frost, or spoyled of boughes by the
 loppers axe, or beautified with pleasant leafe, or farspreading branches. If the glorie of other kingdomes were more splendent for a flash, presently to bee extinguished, (as being greater than their corruptible nature was capable of:) this no way impeacheth Gods promise for making Abrahams seede a mightie Nation; seeing it was not at any time so great a people as at al times it might haue beene, had they obserued the meanes appointed for their grouth. How incomparable the height of Sions roofe, aboue other nations, might haue bin, we may gesse from the capacitie of her foundations. The known altitude and continuance of her wals, though neuer finished to her founders desire,The strength and cont^nuance of these Iewes diseased estate, argues how incomparable their strength might haue beene had they followed their Lawgiuers precepts for the preseruation of their health. yet such, as who so shall looke vpon with vnpartiall eye, must acknowledge ordained for extraordinarie strength and greatnesse. For take wee this Kingdome with it defectes; what wonder can reuolutions of time affoorde like to this late mentioned? That by such an vnitie of naturall propagation from one roote, (almost perished before it sprouted,) and distinct lineall succession neuer interrupted, Abrahams seede should continue one and the same Nation for two thousand yeares: sometimes the mightiest amongest coeuall Kingdomes, a scourge and terrour to all neighbour countries; and after many grieuous wounds and deadly, in their estimation that gaue them, receiued from others, still preserued aliue, to see the successiue rise and fall of three great and potent Monarchies, yet able in decrepit daies, to hold play with the fourth, the mightiest that euer was on earth, euen whilest it was in it best age, full strength and perfect health, free from any intestine broyles, secure of all externall assaultes. Much better were these Iewes able to encounter the Romane Empyre, in Tacitus his life time, than it, within three hundred yeares after his death, to defend the imperiall seate against barbarous, seelie, and foolish nations, vnhatched when the Romane Eagle winges were spread ouer the most famous Kingdomes of the earth. Suppose the Romane Empyre had receiued,The Romane Empyre had neuer the like push before it fell, as it had by the Iewes in Adrians time. at the same time, but halfe so terrible a blow in Italy, as these Iewes had done in Iurie and Hierusalem vnder Vespasian, and his sonne: how easily had the commotions of their relickes, in Traeians and Adrians times, shaken the Romane yoake from off the Nations neckes? Or if the other tenne Tribes returne had beene but halfe so entire and
 complete, as Iudahes and Beniamins were, the Romane Eagles had neuer come to prey vpon their carkases in the territories of Iudea. But it was their strong God, which before had scattered Israel amongst the nations, and at the time appointed shut these Iewes vp in Hierusalem, as in a prison.
2 Againe, other Kingdomes gained little by their greatnesse, saue onely magnificent names or swelling titles. No other people euioied so great prosperitie, so good cheape, as this sometimes did, and all times might haue done. No other had so good assurance or securitie of that prosperitie or peace they enioyed, as this people had, vnlesse themselues had made a wilfull forfeiture: nor was the publike health or welfare of any other state or Kingdome so fully communicated to euery particular and inferiour member. For vsually the titularie or abstract brightnesse of that glorie, wherewith other great states outwardly seemed most to shine, was maintained with the perpetuall harmes and internall secret mischiefes of many priuate persons, as great flames are not nourished without great store of fuell: whereas the prosperitie of Dauids throne, as in other points so in this, was established like the Moone: that whilest they turned vnto their God, their state was capable of greatest splendour, without consumption of their naturall partes or substance. And euen whilest other States did for their sinnes preuaile against theirs, yet such Peeres as had beene principall instruments of their woe, and tooke occasion to disgrace their Lawes or Religion, in their captiuitie and distresse, had for the most part, (as was obserued before) fearefull and disastrous ends: and might more iustly haue taken vp that complaint, after their spoyles of Iewrie, which Diomedes did after the destruction of Troy,
Quicunque Iliacos ferro violauimus agros,-- nefanda per orbemSupplicia scelerum poenas expendimus omnes.What did Troies fall, or Phrygian spoyles, the Graecians state aduance?Whom fearfull plagues haunt through the world: such was the victors chance.

Many of them (no doubt) before their dying day, had obserued as he did, that they had foughten against some God, whilest they wrong'd this people: and would haue beene as vnwilling to beare armes against them againe as hee was against the relickes of the Troians:
Nec mihi cum Teueris vllum post eruta bellumPergama: nec veterum memini loetoruc malorum.What Troy my splene to Troia^s ceas^t, her flames quench th'heat of warI little ioy of what is past. Rub not a bleeding scar.
3 For these and many like consequentes, this people in the issue and vpshot of their greatest calamities, had both reason to reioyce, and Psal. 126.6. the Heathens iust cause to say: The Lord had done great things for them, albeit hee often suffered them to bee conquered. For euen this sicklinesse of their State was a meanes of its long life, their scourges and phlebotomies a signe of Gods tender care ouer their health, vntill they grew proud of his fauour, and waxed obdurate by his often fatherly corrections, as one of their owne writers well obserues. The Lord doth not long waite for vs,2. Mott. 6.14. as for other Nations whom hee punisheth, when they are come to the fulnesse of their sinnes. But thus he dealeth with vs, that our sinnes should not be heaped vp to the full, so that afterward he should punish vs, and therefore he neuer withdrawes his mercie from vs, and though he punish with aduersitie, yet doth he neuer forsake his people.
4 Finally, their decay and increase was such as could not bee measured by the rules of policie. Hence was it that Tacitus was not tacitus, but a tatler, transported from himselfe, his wonted sagacitie and ingenuitie, as being quite out of his naturall element, while hee medled with their affayres. That contrarietie, which he obserues betwixt theirs and the Romans Religion, was as great betwixt their policies. What was good in the one, was nought in the other: that which Rome did thinke might preserue her in helth, was apprehe^ded, by the wisest amongst this people, as readie poison for their state. Those plots which would haue
 crushed any other people once brought vnder, did oft worke their aduancement, and their enemies fall. Whence both their rising and falling, and consequently, the successe of such as opposed themselues against them, were, in other Nations apprehension, sometime in their owne, meerely fatall, altogether incorrigible by worldly policie, especially in more auncient times. Hence did the wise men of Chaldee, vpon the first notice of the windes turning for them, read Haamans destinie, but too late:Esther 6.13. If Mordecai be of the seed of the Iewes, before whom thou hast begun to fall, thou shalt not preuaile against him, but shalt surely fall before him. Achion the Ammonites speech to Holofernes, whether truly vttered by him, or fained by the pen-man of that storie, was framed (no doubt) according to the knowne experience of those times, and containes such aduise, as a faithfull counsellor, well acquainted with their estate, vpon like occasion should haue giuen vnto his Lord, not so well acquainted with it. This hee was bound vnto by the rules of Poetrie,Vnto what time this Historie is to be referred, Vide Sulpit. Seuerum. which the author of that booke (vnlesse perhaps his memorie faile him in the circumstance of time, an escape incident to fictions, for their affinitie with lyes) very well obserues, if his worke be rather to be censured for a Poeme than an Historie; his aduise was this:Iudith 5.21. And whiles they sinned not before their God, they prospered, because the God that hated iniquitie was with them. But when they departed from the way which he appointed them, they were destroyed in many battailes after a wonderfull sort, and were led captiue into a land that was not theirs: and the Temple of their God was cast to the ground, and their Cities were taken by the enemies. But now they are turned to their God, and are come vp from the scattering wherein they were scattered, and haue possessed Hierusalem, where their Temple is, and dwell in the mountaines which were desolate. Now therefore my Lord and Gouernour, if there be any fault in this people, so that they haue sinned against their God, let vs consider that this shall be their ruine, and let vs goe vp, that we may ouercome them. But if there be none iniquitie in this people, let my Lord passe by, least their Lord defend them, and their God be for them, and we become a reproach before all the world. The first root of all such effects or knowne experiments, as in auncient time, yeelded matter to their neighbours of this or like obseruation, was Gods first promise vnto Abraham:Gen. 12.2, 3. And I will make of thee a great Nation, and will blesse thee, and make thy name great, and
 thou shalt be a blessing. I will also blesse them that blesse thee, and curse them that curse thee, and in thee shall all the families of the earth be blessed.The causes and gr^unds of the heath^ns insulting ouer the Iewes in their distresse. Which promise, as it doth concerne the temporall state of the Iewes, was to be limited according to the tenor of Achiors speech, and did then onely take effect, when they followed Abrahams footsteps, and liued in faithfull obedience to Gods Lawes, or hauing transgressed them, did turne againe with their whole heart to seeke the God of their Fathers. From experience of their good successe in such cases, partly (I thinke) were their neighbour countries so sauage and mercilesse towards them in their greatest distresse, alwayes crying, vpon like occasion, as the Edomites did in the day of Hierusalem, Downe with it, downe with it euen to the ground; because they could not hope for any reuenge, but by waiting the turning of their fates, and taking them in the ebbing of their fortunes: for when they begun to rise, they knew there was no meanes to stay them. TheIudea terra quali nunquam ante mo^u co^cussa, magnam, per totam ^am re^^nem iacturam fecit pecud^m: q un and haminu^ oppr^ssa sunt rui^s aediu^ circ^ter de^e millia, militaris tamen multitudo nihil detrimenti accepit, vtpote sub d^ oagu^s. H ec ^lades etia^ in maius aucta rum^^ibus, quos ^u^cij ge^tilium od o i^ non ignari dabant auribus Arabum, im^^ane quanin extulit host^um animos, quasi subuersi^ Iudaeorum vrbibus and extinctis hominabus, nulli iam superessent. Legatos itaque gentis, quir^bus afflictis pacem patuuri venerant comprehensos necauerunt, moxque magna alacritate preperaueru^t ad hosti e^ exercitum. Joseph. Antiq. Iud. l. 15. c. 7. Arabians immane and sauage practises vpon their Embassadors, seeking compassion to their lamentable estate, torne and ruinate by the mightie hand of their God, in a fearefull and prodigious Earthquake, doe argue a deepe rooted memorie of their ancestors strange ouerthrowes (mentioned in Scripture) by this peoples forefathers; and these later Arabians, long lying in wait to do these Iewes a mischiefe, if they had not beene restrained by the mightie hand of God; who now, as they suppose, being turned their enemie, they apprehend this opportunitie of working a full reuenge. Nor are their hard hearts mollified with their publike miseries, nor their inueterate malice so quenched with their embassadors bloud, shed in the 7. yeare of Herods raigne, but it burst out againe in Vespasians time. For these Arabians, though neuer (as aAmmia. Marcel. lib. 84. Romane Writer notes) any true friends, or well-willers to the Romane state, were the forwardest men to assist Titus in Hierusalems last and fatall siege; for the same reasons were the Nations round about them as earnestly bent to hinder the reedifying of Hierusalem, after the returne from captiuitie, as these were now to pull it downe, as fearing least this peoples good fortunes should rise againe with their Citie wals. But as Nehemia notes,Nehem. 6.16. after the enemies had heard that the wall was finished, they were afraid, and their courage fayled them, for they knew that this worke was wrought by God.
5 These and like obseruations make me thinke, it was neither skill in Astrologie, or profession of such Arts as the Magi vsed, (whereunto this of all people was least addicted) which first hatched that opinion of the Iewes descent from the Magi:Refert Diog laertius, in Prooemio. rather the later Heathen, ignorant of their originall, and not able to deriue that strange successe which did haunt them, or their demeanour answerable thereto, from ordinarie or naturall causes, referred all to Magicke spels, or some secret Art of diuination. So vnwilling is flesh and bloud to acknowledge such as they hate, for the beloued of the Lord; and so powerfull is the Prince of darkenesse, either to blind the hearts of the worldly wise, or to auert their eyes from beholding an vnpleasant truth, that if at any time the finger of God appeare in the deliuerance or good successe of his people, the infidell or naturall man ascribes such effects as Magicians (vnlesse vpon presumption of trauellers priuiledge amongst the ignorant) durst not arrogate to themselues, vnto Magicall or some other like Arts; because the corruption of their nature is more capable of such practises, than of true beleefe in God, and they more prone in distresse to flye vnto Sorceries or Magick charmes,^ege Xiphilinum in libro Dionis 71. than with true faith and firme constancie to expect the deliuerance of the Lord by such meanes as the faithfull Israelites did. So when the Christian Legion had, by their prayers, relieued Antonius armie, readie to swound for thirst, with plentie of water, in as miraculous sort as Elias did sometime the hoast of Israel; the Heathens, acknowledging the effect for supernaturall, ascribed it to Arnuphis, the Egyptian Sorcerer, his acquaintance with Mercurie, and other supposed Gods of the ayre. In like sort the moderne Iew, acknowledging many wonders wrought by our Sauiour, takes it for a sufficient argument,Vide Munsterum in secundum caput Matth. Heb. That all of them were wrought by Magicke skill, onely because the Euangelist sayth, he had beene in Egypt; so is he blinded with wilfull malice, that he cannot see how, by this obiection hee layes all the wonders which Moses wrought, open to the like exception of Atheists, Infidels, and Heathen. For both Moses, in whom he trusts, and Abraham in whom he glories, and all the Patriarches, from whome the Iewes descended, had beene in Egypt, in ripe age, where our Sauiour came not, but in his infancie. As for his myracles, the testimonie of Moses,
 and other Prophets, whose diuine authoritie is acknowledged by the Iew, shall euince them, wrought by the finger of God. In the meane time the estate of the Iewes since their death, sufficiently knowne to all the world, and foretold by them, shall manifest against the Atheist, that they all wrote by the spirit of God.


CHAP. V.
That all the Heathens obiections against, or doubts concerning the Iewes estate, are preuented or resolued by Iewish Writers.
1 OVt of that which hath beene hitherto premised, this conclusion stands firme, supported both by forraine Writers obseruation, and these Iewes owne confession, That they were a people remarkable, for their vnusuall prosperitie and calamitie. I am fully persuaded it would haue giuen full satisfaction to any ingenuous Roman or later Heathen, that this was a people beloued of God, had they knowne as much as we doe: that all they could obiect in contempt of the Iewes, or their Religion, had beene conceiued before by the Assyrian and Babylonian, but falsified in the euent, fully answered by Iudaicall Writers, and plainely foretold by their Prophets; least such euents as occasioned others to contemne them, might haue proued temptations to the godly amongst this people, as if they had beene forsaken of their God. The dayes had beene, wherein the Babylonians had taken themselues for men, and their Idols for gods, as good as Rome had any; and these Iewes for as base a people as the world yeelded: They had gathered captiuitie as the sand,Abac. 1.10. mocked the Kings, and made a scorne of the Princes, deriding euery strong Hold: and hence (as the Prophet foresaw) they were as readie as the Romanes to take a courage in transgressing and doing wickedly, imputing this their power vnto their God. But the Prophet is not herewith dismayed, nor tempted to thinke his Gods power was lesse than theirs; albeit, to shew himselfe a true Patriot of Israel, hee complaines of their intolerable presumption, which in due time he wel foresees should be abated. Art not thou of old, my Lord my God, mine holy one? We shall not die:
 O Lord, thou hast ordained them for iudgement, and O God thou hast established them for correction. Thou art of pure eyes, and canst not see euill, thou canst not behold wickednesse: wherefore doest thou looke vpon the transgressors, and holdest thy tongue when the wicked deuoureth the man, that is more righteous than he? and makest men as the fishes of the sea, and as the creeping things, that haue no ruler ouer them? They take vp all with the angle, they catch it in their net, and gather it in their yarne, whereof they reioyce and are glad. Therefore they sacrifice vnto their net, and burne incense vnto their yarne, because by them their portion is fat, and their meat plenteous. Shall they therefore stretch out their net, and not spare continually to slay the Nations? No: he knew their time was limited, and other Nations, as Ieremie saith, When his time was come, were to serue themselues of him; though God at that time had exposed the Princes of Iudah to his violence, for their violent oppression of their brethren, as Habacuc expressely notes in the beginning of his fore-mentioned Prophecie. These Iewes, before the euent did proue the contrarie, were as incredulous they should bee brought into captiuitie by the Babylonian, or such foolish Idolaters, as the Romans were of their great prosperitie vnder Dauid or Salomon. And for to beat downe this proud humor in them, the Prophet Ezechiel foretels, That for their extreame crueltie the Lord would punish them by the most wicked of the Heathen: Make a chaine: for the land is full of the iudgement of bloud,Ezech. 7.23. and the Citie is full of crueltie. Wherefore I will bring the most wicked of the Heathen, and they shall possesse their houses: I will also make the pompe of the mightie to cease, and their holy places shall be defiled. When destruction commeth, they shall seeke peace, and shall not haue it: Calamitie shall come vpon calamitie, and rumor shall be vpon rumor: then shall they seeke a vision of the Prophet, but the Law shall perish from the Priest, and counsell from the Ancient. And least any should maruaile, why God would so vse his chosen people, he giues the reason in the words immediatly following, because he was a God of iustice. The King shall mourne, and the Princes shall be clothed with desolation, and the hands of the people in the land shal be troubled: I will doe vnto them according to their wayes, and according to their iudgements will I iudge them, and they shall know that I am the Lord.
2 More particularly both Tullies obiection, concerning their ouerthrow and conquest, is directly answered; Strabo and Senecaes
 doubt, concerning their thriuing in captiuitie, fully resolued; and Tacitus false imputations of their increase, sufficiently cleared by the Psalmist:Psal. 106.39. They were stained with their owne workes, and went a whoring with their owne inuentions: therefore was the wrath of the Lord kindled against his people, and he abhorred his owne inheritance, and he gaue them into the hand of the Heathen: and they that hated them were Lords ouer them. Their enemies also oppressed them, and they were humbled vnder their hand. Many a time did he deliuer them, but they prouoked him by their counsels, therefore they were brought downe by their iniquities. Had Tullie read thus much, he might haue bin fully satisfied, that it was not loue or hate of his immortall gods, which made the Romanes conquerors, the Iewes captiuate: but it was their loue to sinne, onely hatefull to their God, which brought them in subjection vnto the Romanes; and made Caesar, whom Tullie and other Romans hated, Lord ouer them, as they had bin ouer the Iewes. And if Strabo, Seneca, Tacitus, or others, that either enuied or maruelled at these Iewes prosperitie, had read what followes in the same Psalme, they had rested better satisfied with the reason that the Psalmist there giues, than with such as blundering Politicians guesse at:vers. 44. For although they had bin brought downe by their iniquitie, yet their God, who had giuen them into their enemies hand, saw when they were in affliction, and heard their cry. And he remembred his couenant towards them, and repented according to the multitude of his mercies, and gaue them fauour in the sight of all them that led them away captiues. The Psalmist had better vnderstanding of Gods dealing with these people, than Tullie and Strabo had; and in confidence of Gods mercies, which they had often tasted, he concludes with this prayer:vers 47. Saue vs, O Lord, our God, and gather vs from among the Heathen, that we may praise thy holy name, and glorie in thy praises. Though this godly Psalmist saw this people in greater distresse than they were in Tullies time, yet hee attributes not their captiuitie and oppression vnto any want of good will in their God towards them, but vnto their ingratitude towards him: for if they had beene thankfull vnto him, the blowes that light on them should haue beene spent vpon their enemies. But as another Psalmist complaines in the person of his God, Psal. 81.11. But my people would not heare my voice,Psal. 81.11. vers. 12, 13, 14, 15, 16. and Israel would haue none of me. So I gaue them ouer vnto the hardnesse of their hearts, and they haue walked
 in their owne counsels. O that my people would haue hearkened vnto me, and Israel had walked in my wayes, I would soone haue humbled their enemies, and turned my hand against their aduersaries: The haters of the Lord should haue beene subiect to them, and their time should haue endured for euer. I would haue fed them (saith the Lord) with the finest wheat, and with the honey out of the rocke would I haue satisfied thee.
3 This one place, to omit many other,Gods especiall fauors towards the Iewes, no way impeach his vnpartiall iudgements. abundantly proues the former assertion, That if this people had continued in well doing, all the Nations should continually haue wondered at their extraordinarie prosperitie. But here a Christian may as well doubt, as the Heathen wonder, Why Israel after so many transgressions, as the Author of the hundred and sixt Psalme complaines of, was not destroyed at once, as other great and mightie Nations had bin. For the more abundant fauours their fore-fathers had found, and the greater Gods blessings (layd vp for their posteritie) were, the greater was their ingratitude in rebelling, their rebellion it selfe so much more wilfully hainous; and alwaies the more wilfull or hainous any sinne is, the more grieuous, certaine, and more speedie punishment it deserues. How could that most just and holy one, which so often protesteth he respecteth no mens persons, spare this most vngratefull, stubborne, and rebellious people, longer than any other?
4 The full and necessarie consequence of these collections is thus much, and no more: The finall extirpation of these Iewes had bin accomplished many generations before it came to passe, had the Lord bin only iust, or respected onely their deserts, whom he so often preserued, when iustly he might haue destroyed them. But if wee looke farther into the waies of Gods prouidence, the true end and reason of destroying others, and preseruing them, wil appeare one and the same. For that suddaine execution of his justice vpon others, which did so much aduance his glorie, equally practised vpon them, had as greatly impeached it amongst the nations. This cause of their long preseruation the Lord himselfe assignes, Deut. 32.26.27. I haue said I would scatter them abroad, I would make their remembrance to cease from among men; saue that I feared the furie of the enemie, least their aduersaries should wax proud, and least they should say, Our high hand, and not the Lord, hath done all this. Againe, as the Lord was most iust, so was he most kind and mercifull towards all, euen towards the Gentiles, in these Iewes: for by their strange deliuerance and restauration
 the other might haue learned, that their God was a God of Gods, and Lord of Lords, most worthie to be honored of all the world, as he himselfe addeth in the fore-cited place:Vers. 36, 37, 38, and 39. For the Lord shall iudge his people, and repent towards his seruants, when he seeth that their power is gone, and none shut vp in Hold, nor left abroad: when men shall say, Where are their Gods, their mightie God in whom they trusted, which did eat the fat of their sacrifices, and did drinke the wine of their drinke-offering? Let them rise vp and helpe you; let him be your refuge: Behold now, for I, I am he, and there is no gods with me. Thus, since these Iewes began first to be a Nation, as well the wane as the encrease of their estate; or (to vse our Apostles words) as well their diminution as abundance might haue yeelded the docile and well disposed greater riches, than the spoyles of their Cities and Countrey did the proud and mightie amongst the Gentiles. And albeit they oft times sinned more grieuously than others did, yet were there alwayes left some godly amongst this people, which in their distresse knew themselues, and could teach others the right way to repentance; of which the Heathen, one and other, were altogether ignorant. And this was an especiall cause, though subordinate to the former, of their long preseruation. For when they were not so extreame bad, as to continue in former sinnes, but vnfainedly called vpon the Lord in their distresse, he heard their prayers; and being once receiued to his, they found fauour at their enemies hands. So Salomon had obserued, When the wayes of a man please the Lord, he will make also his enemies at peace with him. The truth whereof we haue seene continually experienced in these Iewes, before our Sauiours time, though much degenerate from their auncestors. But their posteritie, as much degenerate from them as they from the other, goe as farre beyond the middle sort in punishment, as they came short of their first fore-fathers in all graces and fauours bestowed vpon them by their God. Though these (such I meane as liued since our Sauiours time) cry vnto the Lord, yet doth he not heare them, although their distresses haue beene more and more grieuous, many hundred yeares together, than their fore-fathers.P^o. 28. What is the reason? Because they haue turned their eares continually from hearing the Law, therefore their prayers are continually turned into sinne.
5 Thus though the alteration of the Iewish state be such, as all the world might wonder, and stand amazed at; such as would make the wisest heathen giddie, that should seeke to compasse the true causes thereof by politique search; yet vnto vs Christians, that haue the Oracles of our God, their estate cannot seeme strange; seeing nothing, good or bad, that hath befallen this people, from their first beginning to this present day, but is foretolde in the sacred storie, which hath continually proued it selfe as infallible a Prognostication for what is to come, as it is an authentike register of all things past.
6 The particular kalendars, wherin their good or dismall daies are distinguished, according to the diuersity of their ways, we may find Leuit. 26. and Deut. 28. After Moses had proposed extraordinary blessings, if they would walke in the Lawes which he had giuen them, hee threatneth them with plagues and calamities in their owne Land; as with bodily sickenesse, incursion of enemies, oppressions and spoyle of goods, strange ouerthrowes in battell, and fearefulnesse of heart. And if these would not reclaime them, then hee threatneth to punish them seauen times more according to their sinnes: as with barrennesse of soyle, prodigious famine, and scarcity of fruit. And yet if they hold on stil to work stubbornly against him, he threatneth to multiply the former plagues seuen times; by sending wild beasts among them, which should spoile them and destroy their cattell, and make them few in number, and your high-waies shal be desolate. The like multiplying of his plagues, for the increase of their stubbornenesse, hee reiterates twice againe ver. 23. to the 27. Yet, if by those you will not be reformed etc. hee increaseth all the former plagues seauen times againe, verse 28, 29, 31, 32, 33, and 36, 37, 38. The first and second degrees of the plagues threatned, were in part fulfilled in the dayes of Gedeon, Samson, Samuel, and Saul: The third likewise (by wilde beastes) wee see fulfilled in the dayes of Iehoram King of Israel, 2. Kings chap. 17. verse 25, 26. The fourth and fift likewise wee see in part fulfilled in the captiuitie of Israel in Iudah, in the dayes of Manasseh, Iehoiachim, and Tzedechiah; and in the time of Ptolomie the first, vnder Antiochus Epiphanes. Both their owne and other Stories, giue vs full Experiments of this Prophecie. As their stubbornenesse did increase, so Gods plagues here threatned did wax more grieuous:
 these later calamities being, at the least, seuen times greater (both for extent and duraunce) than the former persecutions, which they suffered by the Philistims, Moabites, Aramites, and Ammonites. But the fifth and last plague, which containeth all the rest more than seuen times, was not accomplished till after our Sauiours death: after which time, all the rest (in part fulfilled before) are more than seuen times multiplied vppon them. In their former ouerthrowes or captiuities, they suffered the violence of warre, and oftentimes felt the extremitie of hostile lawes: but after these storms were past, so they would submit themselues vnto their conquerors, they vsually found (as was shewed before) more than ordinary fauour at their hands. But since our Sauiors death, the memory of their former plagues hath bin but an inuitation of the like, or worse; their continuall bad vsage prescribing the lawfulnesse of their abuse. In the 28. of Deuteronomy, the extraordinary blessings, and plagues which were to ouertake this people, either in their life or death, are more exactly calculated. And albeit their blessings might haue beene more, and more admirable, than the curses that haue befallen them: yet Moses (it seemes) foreseeing, or fearing what would be, rather than hoping the best that might be, is almost foure times as long in calculating their plagues, as their blessings. So haue the miseries of these later Iewes beene foure times as long, as the prosperitie of their worthy auncestours; if wee would take an exact measure of the one, from the sacred histories before the Babylonish captiuity, of the later from experience and relation of Iewish or Heathen writers. And yet no plague, either knowne by experience, or related by any Writers, but is euidently foretold by Moses. His particular praedictions shall be inserted, as the euents shall giue the occasion throughout this Discourse. To beginne with that most horrible plague, Deut. 28.53. etc. And thou shalt eate the fruit of thy body, euen the flesh of thy sonnes and thy daughters, which the Lord thy God hath giuen thee, during the siege and straitnesse, wherein thy enemy shall inclose thee. So that the man that is tender and exceeding daintie among you, shall be grieued at his brother, and at his wife that lieth in his bosome, and at the remnant of his children, which he hath yet left. For feare of giuing vnto any of them of the flesh of his children, whome he shall eate, because he hath nothing left him in that siege and straitnes,
 wherein the enemy shall besiege thee in all thy Citties. The tender and dainty woman among you, which neuer could venture to set the soale of her foote on the ground (for her softnesse and tendernesse) shall be grieued at her husband that lieth in her bosome, and at her sonne, and at her daughter, and at her after birth, that shall come out from betweene her feet; and at her children which she shall beare. For when all things lacke she shall eate them secretly, during the siege and straitnes, wherein thine enemy shall besiege thee in thy Cities. This prophecie we see fulfilled, to an haires breadth, in Vespasians time.


CHAP. VI.
The fulfilling of Moses and others Prophecies touching the desolation of Iewry, and destruction of Ierusalem: and the signes of the time, witnessing Gods wonderfull hand therein.
1 SEeing that part of Tacitus is lost, whence we might haue knowne more, concerning their calamitie, than we can find now in heathen writers, we must take the conclusions, answerable to Moses praedictions, from Iosephus a Iew by birth, no way partiall for Christians. And his conclusions (to omit Moses his authority or other inducements) can not seeme improbable, from such premisses, as the Romane writers haue confirmed; though little intending such inferences, as we now make from them. Doe the calamities of those Iewes, related by Iosephus and Eusebius, seeme strange? They iustly may, if wee consider this people as naturall or ordinary men, not as paternes of GODS extraordinarie iudgements. Straunge might theyr iudgements seeme, and incredible to the Romanes or worldly-wise, vnlesse other circumstances of that Time, witnessed by the Romanes themselues, were as rare. But if, by the Romanes opinion, euerie vnusuall effect in Nature did portend some such strange euent in humane affaires: why should not the wisest of them in that Age, expect some extraordinary or miraculous matters of
