 that true happinesse, whereat all States aime, but only such shall light on, as put these Sacred Lawes in execution. It is the common errour of all corrupted mindes, to seeke that farre off which (as the Lord told his people) is within them, euen in their hearts and in their mouthes, so they would bee doers not hearers onely of the Law. Many Heathens haue vsed such diligent care and vnrelenting resolution for iust execution of their defectiue and erroneous Lawes, as would the coactiue power, euery where resident where Christianitie is professed, vse the like for establishing an vniforme and vnpartiall, though but an externall and ciuill practise of the ten Commaundements and other Sacred morall precepts, of whose truth no Christian doubts, about whose meaning nor Protestant, nor Papist, nor any Sect this day liuing, doe, or can contend: fallible Iudges might effect, what the Papists pretend, as infallibly, as if euery particular congregation had such a true infallible teacher as they falsely deeme or faine their Pope, to cathechise them ex cathedra thrice a weeke. For who could better resolue vs in all points of moment, or retaine our heartes in vnitie of faith, then truth it selfe, once clearely seene or made knowne vnto vs: yet is it in it selfe, much brighter then the Sunne we daily see; which it likewise heerein exceeds, That whilst Gods Word endures amongst vs, it still remaines aboue our Horizon, and cannot set: onely grosse and foggie interpositions (raised from exhalation of such foule lusts and reeking sinnes, as seuere vnpartiall execution of knowne Lawes might easily restraine,) vsually eclipse or hide it from vs. Such as are not so Eagle-sighted as to behold the brightnesse of euery diuine truth in it proper Sphere, might yet safely hehold the reflexion of it in one part or other of the Sacred fountaine, were it not troubled with the muddy conceipts of vnsetled and vnquiet braines; or were not such men oftentimes in great places, as minding nothing but earthly thinges, alwayes mingle filth and clay with the Christall streames of the water of life. Happie is that man of God, that in this turbulent age, can, in points of greatest moment, see the diuine truth himselfe; small hope haue any of causing others to see it, whilest carnall mindes may euery where without feare of punishment, but not without terrour of such Ecclesiastique power as shall controule them, forme out their owne shame, and ouercast the face
 of Heauen, whence light should come vnto their soules, with blasphemous vnhallowed breath: whilst dunghill sinkes may bee suffered to euaporate the abundance of that inward filth, (which is lodged in their hearts) as it were of purpose to choake the good Spirit of God, whilst it seekes to breath in others mouthes whose breasts it hath inspired with grace.
14 In breefe (least my aduersarie should challenge me of partialitie,) As the meanes which our Church from Gods Word prescribes for establishing mens hearts in the vnitie of true faith, (were the execution of knowne Lawes any way correspondent,) might (as is said) infallibly effect what the Papists falsely pretend: so in truth it cannot without hypocrisie bee dissembled; that whiles our practise is so dissonant to our doctrine, and our publique discipline so loose, though in detestation of their errors we haue turned our backs vpon them, with protestation to follow a contrarie rule, yet for the most part we iump with them at the iourneys end. To omit more finall agreements of our contrarieties, elsewhere shewed. They wholy permit the keyes of the Well of life to one mans hands; who, for his owne aduantage, we may bee sure, will locke it vp so close, as none shall looke vpon it but with spectacles of his making. For as the head is, such we must expect the eyes will bee, if the one, the other must bee vniuersall too, such as will leaue nothing to bee seene by priuate or particular eyes but what they haue seene before; or in one word, if we admit one absolute visible head, his must be the onely eyes of the Church. We, not through default of publike constitutions, nor so much by conniuence of Ecclesiastique Magistrates, as by presumptuous disobedience of inferiours, are so farre from committing the custodie of this sacred fountaine into one or few mens hands; that the flocke for the most part neuer expect the pastors marshalling, but rushing into it without order trample in it with vncleane feet. If any beame of truth haue found entrance into one of their soules, though quickly eclipsed or smothered by earthly cogitations, he straight-way presumes, Gods word more plentifully dwels in him, then in all his teachers: whence if his purse be strong, it is with him as with an horse when prouender pricks him, he kicks against all Ecclesiastike authoritie, and spurns at his poore Ouerseer that should feede him; like the wanton Asse in the fable
 that seeing the Moone lately shining where she was drinking suddainely couered with a cloud; vpon imagination she had dru^ke it vp, ran winsing out ere her thirst was quenched and threw her rider.
15 Thrice happie is that Land and State where ciuill pollicie and spiritual wisedome, graue experience and profound learning, (in whose right commixture consists the perfect temperature of euery Christian State) doe rightly symbolize. These, where they mutually claspe in their extreames (without intermedling in the essence of each others profession) are like the side-postes or arches in the Lords house: and the awful respect of Christ Iesus the Iudge of both, and that dreadfull day co^tinually sounding in their eares, by the voice of Gods faithfull and sincere Ministers, would bee as the binding stone, or coupling, to fasten them surely in the ioyning. But whilst these (each iealous of other) start a sunder, that brea^h is made, whereat the enemies of the Church and State hope for speedie entrance, to the vtter ruine of both.


CHAP. VII.
Briefly collecting the summe of this second Booke.
1 TO draw a briefe mappe of these large disputes. As the occasions that breede, so the right meanes to auoid all contentions and schismes, are most perspicuously set down in scriptures, amo^gst others most necessary for this purpose, (for the plantation, increase, and strength of true and liuely faith,) sincere obedience to spirituall authoritie is the chiefe. For more willing and chearfull performance hereof, choice should be made of Pastors or ouerseers, qualified as Scripture requires men of so high a calling should be; men not giuen to quarrels or strife, men of milde and lowly spirits, fearing God and hating couetousnesse; men esteeming the hidden treasure of a good conscience at so high a rate, as neither feare of man, nor hopes of any worldly fauour can moue them to hazard or aduenture it. Were these rules, by such as haue the ouersight of Gods flocke, as faithfully practised, as they are by Scripture plainly taught the knowledge
 of Gods word should daily encrease; pietie, deuotion and Christian charitie continually flourish; all strife and dissention quickly fade.
2 But if through the default of Princes or Potentates, no fit choice be made of spirituall gouernours, if by their negligence, worse be made of inferiour Ministers: the cause comes not by deuolution, to be reformed by the congregation: What then must they be altogether silent at such abuse? No; the Scripture hath giuen as plain a rule for their imployme^t as for the others. The more or more often higher powers offend, the more feruently frequent should the lower sort be in powring out1. Tim. 2. v. 1.2 supplications, prayers, and intercessions for kings, and for all that are in authoritie, that they may rule according to Gods word. In the meane time albeit they rule otherwise, inferiours should consider, that God giues them such superiours for their pronenesse to disobedience, scurrilitie, scoffing at lawfull authoritie, or other like sinnes, expresly forbidden by his word. To euery people as well as Israel hee giues such rules in his wrath, as shall not seeke them but theirs, not his glory in their saluation, but their owne glory by their harme.
3 But as the tongues of inferiours must bee tied from scoffing or ieasting at men in authorities bad proceedings: so must not the word of God be bound. If their consciences (rightly and vnpartially examined) direct them otherwise then their gouernours command, they must (notwithstanding their superiours checks) speake as they thinke, vntill death command them silence; if for the freedome of their speech (vpon good warrant of conscience) they be punished, vengeance is Gods, hee will repay superiours for it: vnto whose lawfull authoritie whilst obedience is denied, vpon sinister pretences, or humorous though strong perswasions of conscience, counselling vs to the contrarie: our punishment in this life is iust, whose present smart should teach vs to beware of farre more grieuous in the life to come. But whether offend or swerue more from the rules of Scripture prescribed for their seuerall Christian carriage; superiours in commaunding wrongfully; or inferiours in disobeying iust commaunds; cannot oft-times (if wee speake of particular actions) be infallibily knowne in this life; but must be reserred vnto that day of finall iudgement. The dread of which should in the
 meane time inforce euery superiour daily to consult his owne heart, and strictly to examine his conscience, whether it bee not likely then to giue euidence against him, for imposing too heauie burthens vpon his inferiours. And so must euery inferiour againe vse the like diligence in the daily examination of his conscience, whether it be likely or no to conuince him before the Iudge of quicke and dead, of disobedience to such as he had set in authoritie ouer him, or of such sinister pretences for vsing the libertie of conscience, as conscience it selfe neuer sought after, but were suggested onely by humour, popularitie, or other desires, whose maintenance haue either inforced him to obey man against God, or not to obey man commanding for God.
4 Our partialitie it is towards our selues, or rather to our sensuall delights or pleasures, that makes vs so ignorant in all things which concerne our weale. For would we truly and vnpartially1. Cor. 11. vers. 31. Iudge our selues, we should not be iudged. Not the best experienced Iustice in this land, can, by examining ordinarie malefactors, discerne, what issue their cause shall haue before an vnpartiall Iudge, better then we, by this strict pre-examination, might foresee what finall sentence were prepared for vs, good or bad, according to the diuersitie of our actions and course of life. To this end hath Christ left euery mans conscience in full authoritie, during his absence, to examine, reproue, conuince, and sentence the desires of his owne heart: of which would we daily in sobrietie of sp^rit, and feare of this last iudgement, aske counsell, and patiently expect Gods prouidence; we should by this ordinarie meanes discerne, who commaunded aright, who otherwise, as cleerely as others heretofore haue done by meanes most extraordinarie. For euen the most extraordinarie miracles did ascertaine the Anc^ent of diuine truth, and confirme them in the practise of Chr^^tian obedience, not immediately, as part of their rule of faith, whereon finally to relie, but by informing them to looke into their o^ne soules and consciences, in which truth was already writte^, if th^y had vrged it to confession. If our examination without m^racl^s were as strict, our beleefe would be as firme; spirituall g^u^^^ors commaunds as Christian-like, and inferiours obedience in all points as sincere as was theirs.
5 For conclusion I would giue the Christian Reader a present
 antidote, against al the poisonous inchantments of Romish sorcerers. The medicine is very briefe and easie; onely to thinke euery morning next his heart, or at other seasonable houres, That there is a diuine prouide^ce in this life to guide vs, and after this life ended, a fearefull iudgement to passe vpon all such, as heere abiuring the guidance of it, follow eyther the waies of flesh and bloud in breeding, or of carnall wisedome in composing, strife and dissension about matters spirituall. He that will seriously ruminate on these matters in his vacant well composed thoughts, calling the aduersaries arguments home to the point, which they must touch ere they can wound vs: let me haue onely his dying curse in recompence of all my paines, if any difficultie, any Iesuite or other learned Papist, eyther hitherto hath, or euer shal be able to bring, do trouble his minde. Whatsoeuer can bee brought eyther to countenance their vnchristian doctrine, or disparage our orthodoxall assertions, eyther presuppose a secret deniall of Gods peculiar prouidence, and inward calling of men, or else proceede from want of consideration, that there is a finall iudgment, wherein all controuersies must be taken vp, all contentious and rebellious spirits punisht according to their deserts: Indeed if the authors or abetters of schisme and heresie might escape for euer vnpunished, or Christian modestie and humilitie be perpetually ouerborne by impudencie, scurrilitie, and violent insolency, the inconueniences, obiected by the Romanists, might as much trouble vs, as the wickeds thriuing, did the heathen, that knew not God nor his prouidence. But whilst we acknowledge him and it, the best arguments our Antagonists bring, will appeare as improbable as they are impious.


FINIS.
